===== Hao2023_JGenetGenomics_scRNAseq+Cd168+SkeletalProgenitors =====
Original research
Single-cell transcriptomic analysis identiﬁes a highly replicating
Cd168þ skeletal stem/progenitor cell population in mouse long
bones
Rui-Cong Hao a, b, 1, Zhi-Ling Li b, 1, Fei-Yan Wang a, b, 1, Jie Tang b, 1, Pei-Lin Li b,
Bo-Feng Yin b, Xiao-Tong Li b, Meng-Yue Han a, b, Ning Mao c, Bing Liu d, Li Ding a, e, *,
Heng Zhu a, b, *
a Basic Medical College of Anhui Medical University, Hefei, Anhui 230032, China
b Beijing Institute of Radiation Medicine, Beijing 100850, China
c Beijing Institute of Basic Medical Sciences, Beijing 100850, China
d State Key Laboratory of Experimental Hematology, Department of Hematology, Fifth Medical Center of Chinese PLA General Hospital, Beijing 100071,
China
e Air Force Medical Center, PLA, Beijing 100142, China
A R T I C L E
I N F O
Article history:
Received 23 January 2023
Received in revised form
8 April 2023
Accepted 9 April 2023
Available online 17 April 2023
Keywords::
Skeletal stem cell
scRNA-seq
Bone development
Fracture healing
Bone regeneration
Development
A B S T R A C T
Skeletal stem/progenitor cells (SSPCs) are tissue-speciﬁc stem/progenitor cells localized within skeletons
and contribute to bone development, homeostasis, and regeneration. However, the heterogeneity of SSPC
populations in mouse long bones and their respective regenerative capacity remain to be further clariﬁed. In
this study, we perform integrated analysis using single-cell RNA sequencing (scRNA-seq) datasets of
mouse hindlimb buds, postnatal long bones, and fractured long bones. Our analyses reveal the hetero-
geneity of osteochondrogenic lineage cells and recapitulate the developmental trajectories during mouse
long bone growth. In addition, we identify a novel Cd168þ SSPC population with highly replicating capacity
and osteochondrogenic potential in embryonic and postnatal long bones. Moreover, the Cd168þ SSPCs
can contribute to newly formed skeletal tissues during fracture healing. Furthermore, the results of multi-
color immunoﬂuorescence show that Cd168þ SSPCs reside in the superﬁcial zone of articular cartilage as
well as in growth plates of postnatal mouse long bones. In summary, we identify a novel Cd168þ SSPC
population with regenerative potential in mouse long bones, which adds to the knowledge of the tissue-
speciﬁc stem cells in skeletons.
Copyright © 2023, The Authors. Institute of Genetics and Developmental Biology, Chinese Academy of
Sciences, and Genetics Society of China. Published by Elsevier Limited and Science Press. This is an open
access article under the CC BY-NC-ND license (http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/).
Introduction
Skeletal stem/progenitor cells (SSPCs) have recently been iden-
tiﬁed as tissue-speciﬁc osteochondrogenic progenitor cells in skel-
etons which contribute to bone development, homeostasis, and
regeneration (Tichy and Mourkioti, 2018; Murphy et al., 2020;
Salhotra et al., 2020). However, it has been proved that SSPC pop-
ulations are highly heterogenous for their spatiotemporal distribution
and regenerative capacity (Ortinau et al., 2019; Maruyama et al.,
2021; Zhang et al., 2022a, 2022b). During embryogenesis, limb bud
mesenchymal progenitors differentiate into osteochondrogenic pro-
genitor cells, generate primitive cartilage templates, and initiate the
endochondral ossiﬁcation process to form long bones. With the
development of primary ossiﬁcation center, perichondrial progenitor
cells and blood vessels extend into cartilage lacuna formed by
chondrocyte degradation in the cartilage template, and build bone
marrow cavity (Watson and Adams, 2018; Dirckx et al., 2019;
Tuckermann and Adams, 2021; Veis and O’Brien, 2023). In postnatal
life, osteochondrogenic progenitor cells differentiate into mature
osteoblasts and orchestrate with osteoclasts to undergo long bone
remodeling and regeneration (Long, 2011; Sims and Martin, 2020).
Thus, identifying various types of SSPCs in different anatomical
structures of bones and revealing their properties under physiological
* Corresponding authors.
E-mail addresses: dingli7578@163.com (L. Ding), zhudingdingabc@163.com
(H. Zhu).
1 These authors contributed equally to this work.
Contents lists available at ScienceDirect
Journal of Genetics and Genomics
Journal homepage: www.journals.elsevier.com/journal-of-genetics-
and-genomics/
https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jgg.2023.04.004
1673-8527/Copyright © 2023, The Authors. Institute of Genetics and Developmental Biology, Chinese Academy of Sciences, and Genetics Society of China. Published by
Elsevier Limited and Science Press. This is an open access article under the CC BY-NC-ND license (http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/).
Journal of Genetics and Genomics 50 (2023) 702e712
and pathological conditions are important to understand skeleto-
genesis and may be helpful in developing SSPC-based bone
regeneration strategies.
To date, SSPCs are mainly enriched either by ﬂuorescence
activated cell sorting or ﬂuorescence labeling in reporter mouse
(Gulati et al., 2018; Matsushita et al., 2021; Yu et al., 2022).
Although lineage tracing mouse models reveal spatial allocation
and fate mapping of SSPC populations through development and
adulthood, the reporter models also mark differentiated cell types
and non-skeletal lineages, which limits their applications in SSPC
identiﬁcations (Mizoguchi et al., 2014; Zhou et al., 2014; Mizuhashi
et al., 2018; Matsushita et al., 2020). Recent progress of scRNA-
seq has provided a more comprehensive view of the SSPC het-
erogeneity in the spatiotemporal distribution of SSPC populations
including in long bones and the regenerative capacity of SSPCs
(Greenblatt et al., 2019; Tikhonova et al., 2019; Wolock et al., 2019;
Kelly et al., 2020; Li and Wang, 2021; Mo et al., 2022; Sivaraj et al.,
2022). Chan et al. (2015) mapped skeletal lineage cells in postnatal
mice long bone using scRNA-seq and revealed a SSC population
(CD45Ter119Tie2AlphaVþThy6C3CD105CD200þ)
with
differentiation potential for bone, cartilage, and stromal tissues. In
addition, the potential regulators of the lineage commitment were
explored by analyzing the transcriptome of SSCs (van Gastel et al.,
2020). Further investigations showed that postnatal long bones of
mice contain at least two types of bone progenitors which
orchestrate
long
bone
skeletogenesis
(Jeffery
et
al.,
2022).
Notably, they identiﬁed PDPN as a novel surface maker for the
SSC population in human fetal long bones via performing scRNA-
seq and validated the regenerative capacity of PDPNþ SSCs by
serial renal capsule transplantation (Chan et al., 2018). In addition,
our recent work dissected human embryonic SSC ontogeny by
single-cell transcriptomic and functional analyses, revealing that
CADM1 marks one regenerative SSC population in human long
bone and calvaria development (He et al., 2021). Therefore,
scRNA-seq greatly enhanced the exploration of the markers which
reﬂects the SSC spatiotemporal distribution and regenerative
capacity.
Cd168, also known as the receptor for hyaluronic acid-mediated
motility (Rhamm or Hmmr), has been proved to be a promoter of cell
proliferation, transformation, and motility (Yang et al., 2017; Cui
et al., 2019; Xu et al., 2019; Markasz et al., 2021; Shan et al.,
2022). Increasing studies showed that the expression of CD168
on tumor cell surfaces is related to carcinogenesis and tumor pro-
gression (Wang and Zhang, 2016; Zhu et al., 2022). In addition,
CD168 positive tumor cells possess high metastatic and poor dif-
ferentiation potential (Burren et al., 2021; Soliman et al., 2022).
Moreover, the expression of CD168 was signiﬁcantly increased in
tumor tissues with microcalciﬁcations and was correlated with the
expression of BMP-2 and poor outcomes (Wang et al., 2022).
Notably, a previous report demonstrated that CD168 is expressed
by mesenchymal stem cells (MSCs) and contributes to chondro-
genesis via stem cell-extracellular matrix (ECM) signaling (Bian et
al., 2013). Therefore, accumulated evidence suggests that CD168
may enrich cell properties including highly proliferation and osteo-
chondrogenic potentials.
In the current study, we performed an integrated analysis based on
scRNA-seq data of mouse embryonic limbs and long bones of
postnatal mice. The spatiotemporal distribution of highly-replicating
osteo-chondrogenic cell populations during the formation and
regeneration of mouse long bones was analyzed. Our analyses
identiﬁed a Cd168þ osteochondrogenic population at the articular
cartilage surface and metaphyseal region in postnatal mouse long
bones.
Results
Deciphering murine long bone development at single-cell
resolution
To gain a better understanding of the development of murine long
bone, we performed integrated analysis using scRNA-seq datasets
from previously published studies (Kelly et al., 2020; Sivaraj et al.,
2021, 2022), including cells collected from murine hindlimb buds at
embryonic day (E) 11.5, E13.5, E15.5m and E18.5 as well as long
bone stromal cells (Cd45Ter119) isolated from 3-week-old juvenile
mouse and 12-week-old adult mouse, spanning 6 stages from the
onset of limb bud formation until the postnatal bone is fully devel-
oped. After quality control and normalization, we performed down-
sampling to ensure the representability and comparability of each
time point. Harmony algorithm is used for dataset integration and
batch effect correction. A total of 14,116 single cells were selected
for further analysis (Fig. 1A). Safranin O/Fast Green staining
demonstrated the cartilaginous morphology at different develop-
mental time points (Fig. 1B). We identiﬁed 10 different cell clusters
according to the expression of established feature genes: including
limb bud mesenchymal (LBM) cells (Prrx1, Osr2) (Leussink et al.,
1995; Stricker et al., 2012), chondrocytes (Acan, Sox9) (Doege
et al., 1991; Foster et al., 1994), osteochondral progenitors (OCP)
(Runx2, Sp7, Alpl) (Nakashima et al., 2002; Huang et al., 2007),
endothelial cells (EC) (Pecam1, Kdr, Cdh5) (Raﬁi et al., 2016),
mesenchymal stem cell (MSC) (Pdgfra, Lepr) (Morikawa et al., 2009;
Ding et al., 2012), pericytes (Pdgfrb, Rgs5, Tagln) (Li et al., 1996;
Bondjers et al., 2003; Winkler et al., 2010), apical ectodermal ridge
(AER) (Fgf8) (Lewandoski et al., 2000) (Fig. S1), skeletal muscle cells
(SMC) (Tnnt1, Myog, Myod1) (Tapscott, 2005; Zhu et al., 2013; Wei
and Jin, 2016), immune cells (Ptprc, Lyz2, Cd52) (Hall et al., 1988;
Xia et al., 1991; Clausen et al., 1999), and epithelial cells (Cdh1, Krt17)
(Mareel et al., 1997; Depianto et al., 2010) (Fig. 1C and 1D).
Heterogeneity of osteochondral lineage cells during murine
long bone development
Next, we focused on the most abundant resident osteochondral
lineage cells (OCLCs). To facilitate the delineation of osteochondral
lineage, we removed haematopoietic lineages as well as unrelated
mesenchymal lineages including endothelial cells, pericytes, AER,
epithelial cells, and skeletal muscle cells. A total of 12 distinct cell
clusters were identiﬁed by unsupervised clustering (Fig. 2A). Cluster
identities were assigned based on the expression of marker genes
(Fig. 2B). We annotated cluster 1, cluster 2, and cluster 3 cells as limb
bud mesenchymal cells (LBM1, LBM2, and LBM3, respectively).
Cluster 2 cells showed relatively higher expression of Mki67 and
Cdk1, two widely accepted markers of cell proliferation. Perichon-
drium (cluster 4) highly expressed Thy1 (Nakamura et al., 2010) and
Crabp1 (Zhang et al., 2022a,b). Cells in ﬁve clusters (cluster 5, 6, 7, 8,
9) all expressed the chondrocyte-lineage genes Sox9, Acan, and
Col2a1 (Doege et al., 1991; Foster et al., 1994; Chan et al., 1995).
Embryonic chondrocytes (cluster 5) exist only at embryonic stages;
proliferating OCP (cluster 6) highly expressed proliferation-related
genes, indicating the superior proliferative capacity of this cluster.
Prg4þ chondrocytes (cluster 7) highly expressed articular chon-
drocyte markers Prg4 (Rhee et al., 2005) and Fmod (Murphy et al.,
1999). Prkg2þ chondrocytes (cluster 8) highly expressed Prkg2, a
gene required for the development and organization of growth plate
cartilage (Koltes et al., 2015). Col10a1þ chondrocytes (cluster 9)
expressed chondrocyte (pre-)hypertrophic markers Ihh (Vortkamp
et al., 1996) and Col10a1 (Arnold et al., 2007). Leprþ mesenchymal
R.-C. Hao, Z.-L. Li, F.-Y. Wang et al.
Journal of Genetics and Genomics 50 (2023) 702e712
703
stromal cells (referred to as Lepr-MSCs) (cluster 10) highly expressed
Lepr, a marker of bone marrow stromal cells (Ding et al., 2012), and
Cxcl12, an important haematopoietic stem cell (HSC) niche factor
(Morrison and Scadden, 2014). Two osteolineage clusters (cluster 11
and 12) differentially expressed Runx2, the master regulator tran-
scription factor (TF) controlling early osteoblastic differentiation
(Huang et al., 2007), Sp7, whose downstream target TF is required for
late osteolineage commitment (Nakashima et al., 2002), and the other
osteoblast
maturation
markers
Bglap3
(Brennan-Speranza
and
Conigrave, 2015).
We also observed a rise and fall in cell populations and their
gradual transition from embryonic to postnatal stage (Fig. 2C). At
E11.5 when early limb bud formed, mesenchymal cells constituted
the major populations. With the formation of precartilaginous con-
densations, the frequency of limb bud mesenchymal cells declined
and gave rise to immature chondrocytes, which further underwent
hypertrophy
and
contributed
to
longitudinal
bone
growth.
Leprþ MSCs emerged as early as E11.5 and expanded after birth,
gradually becoming the main skeletal stem/progenitor cell (SSPC)
population in the bone marrow, especially after adolescence. The
postnatal long bone is composed of undifferentiated osteochon-
drogenic progenitors and terminally differentiated mature cells
(Fig. 2C). Uniform manifold approximation and projection (UMAP)
revealed the temporal distribution of cells across 6 different devel-
opmental stages (Fig. 2D).
Delineating osteochondral lineage speciﬁcation during murine
long bone development
To investigate the transcriptional dynamics during osteochondral
lineage speciﬁcation, we reconstructed the in vivo developmental
trajectories of osteochondral lineage cells using Monocle3. We or-
dered the cells by manually selecting LBM2 (cluster 2) as the start
node. Cells were colored by clusters (Fig. 3A) and pseudotime value
(Fig. 3B), respectively. The branched trajectory recapitulated two
processes of bone development: osteogenesis (left) and chondro-
genesis (right). To further explore the temporal gene-expression
patterns along the trajectory, we grouped co-regulated genes into
12 modules using Louvain community analysis. Hierarchically clus-
tered heatmap showed the aggregate expression of genes in each
module across all the clusters (Fig. 3C). We then decomposed these
modules into three states: referred to as limb bud (root), chondro (fate
1) and osteo (fate 2) (Fig. 3D). We found that the expression of
extracellular matrix (ECM) organization-related genes like Foxp1 and
Twist1 was downregulated along both trajectories in the root. The
expression of chondrogenic genes like Sox9 and Acan, was initially
Fig. 1. The single-cell transcriptional atlas of the developing murine long bone. A: Schematic illustration of the experimental design. We performed integrated scRNA-seq analysis of
murine hindlimb buds and postnatal long bone stromal cells using publicly available datasets. The anatomical regions of limb buds were highlighted by red circles. The timeline below
indicates the developmental time points. B: Representative Safranin O/Fast Green staining of mouse hindlimb bud or femur sections at indicated time points (n ¼ 3). Experiments
were repeated independently for more than three times. C: Distribution of 14,116 cells derived from mouse hindlimb buds and long bones stromal cells. A total of 10 cell clusters were
identiﬁed by UMAP. D: Stacked violin plots showing the expression of representative marker genes in each cluster. UMAP, uniform manifold approximation and projection. Scale bar,
200 mm (B).
R.-C. Hao, Z.-L. Li, F.-Y. Wang et al.
Journal of Genetics and Genomics 50 (2023) 702e712
704
elevated, peaked then declined in fate 1. The expression of osteo-
genic genes, such as Runx2 and Bglap was gradually elevated along
the trajectory in fate 2 (Fig. 3E). Pathway enrichment analysis showed
the biological processes related to each state (Fig. 3F). We then
applied single-cell regulatory network inference and clustering
(SCENIC) to infer the TF activity in each cluster, and the expression
correlation between TFs was calculated too (Fig. 3G and 3H).
Characterization of Cd168þ osteochondral progenitors from
embryo to adulthood
Previous studies have identiﬁed a repertoire of cell surface
markers to distinguish and isolate mouse skeletal stem/progenitor
cells, including Grem1þ/FoxA2þ/PTHrPþ SSCs at the metaphysis
(especially under the growth plate) (Worthley et al., 2015; Mizuhashi
et al., 2018; Muruganandan et al., 2022), Pdgfrbþ/Leprþ/Cxcl12þ
SSCs in the bone marrow (Greenbaum et al., 2013; Zhou et al., 2014;
Kwon et al., 2021), as well as aSMAþMx1þ/Prrx1þ/Ctskþ SSCs in the
periosteum (Kawanami et al., 2009; Debnath et al., 2018; Ortinau
et al., 2019), etc (Fig. 4A). In search of novel SSPC populations in
murine long bones, we performed cell-cycle analysis by assigning
each cell a score based on its expression of G2/M and S phase
markers using CellCycleScoring function, which later provided a
predicted classiﬁcation of cell-cycle phase for each cell (G1/G2M/S)
(Fig. 4B). The results showed that early limb bud cells and osteo-
chondral stem/progenitor cells possessed higher proliferation ca-
pacity, while differentiated cells like osteoblasts and hypertrophic
chondrocytes are relatively quiescent. Of note, we identiﬁed an
actively proliferating cell population with the highest proliferation
score, even exceeded the score of other osteochondral stem/pro-
genitor cells. (Fig. 4C and 4D). We also identiﬁed Cd168, encoding
the hyaluronan-mediated motility receptor (also known as Rhamm or
Hmmr), as a marker gene expressed in the proliferating cell
population (Figs. 4E and S2A). UMAP plot showed the distribution of
the proliferating cell population across all stages (Fig. 4F). Differen-
tially expressed gene (DEG) analysis and Gene ontology (GO) anal-
ysis suggested that the proliferating cell population was involved in
skeletal system development and sequentially engaged in mesen-
chyme, cartilage, and bone development (Fig. 4Ge4I).
In situ mapping of Cd168þ osteochondral progenitors during
long bone development
To determine the existence of Cd168þ OCLCs, we performed ﬂow
cytometry using released cells from adult (12-week-old) mouse long
bones, and found that Cd45Ter119Cd31Cd168þ cells account
for 0.16%e0.18% of the released cells (Fig. S2B). Furthermore, in
accordance with the scRNA-seq analyses, the immunoﬂuorescence
staining conﬁrmed that Cd168þ osteochondral progenitors were
involved in murine long bone development. Cd168þ cells were
enriched in the limb bud mesenchyme condensations at E11.5,
emerged in the cartilaginous condensations at E13.5 and the primary
ossiﬁcation center at E15.5, then located at the articular surface and
the metaphysis of fetal bone at E18.5. After birth, Cd168þ cells
localized in the superﬁcial layer of articular cartilage and the growth
plate, but not in the periosteum or endosteum (Figs. 5A and S2C). In
addition, Cd168 co-localized with Runx2, Sox9, or Col2a1 at the
aforementiond sites, suggesting the osteochondrogenic capacity of
Cd168þ osteochondral progenitors (Figs. 5B, 5C, S3, S4).
Cd168þ osteochondral progenitors are closely involved in
bone regeneration during fracture healing
To determine whether Cd168þ osteochondral progenitors respond
to injury, we investigate its contribution to bone regeneration. We
performed scRNA-seq analyses using publicly available datasets
Fig. 2. Heterogeneity of osteochondral lineage cells at single-cell resolution. A: UMAP visualization of OCLCs colored by cluster. B: Dot plot showing the expression of features genes in
12 osteochondral clusters. Dot size represented the proportion of cells expressing speciﬁc gene and color represented the expression levels. C: Stacked area plot showing the
proportion of cell types across developmental time points. The colors indicated corresponding clusters in (A). D: UMAP of OCLCs split by developmental stage. UMAP, uniform manifold
approximation and projection; OCLCs, osteochondral lineage cells.
R.-C. Hao, Z.-L. Li, F.-Y. Wang et al.
Journal of Genetics and Genomics 50 (2023) 702e712
705
generated from fractured mouse femurs and age-matched unfrac-
tured controls. Non-hematopoietic stromal cells (Cd45eTer119e
double negative) were isolated by magnetic-activated cell sorting
(MACS) from 12-week-old mice at post-fracture day 14 (PFD14). After
quality control, downsampling, dataset integration and clustering, we
selected OCLC clusters for further analysis. All single cells were
clustered into 10 clusters based on the expression of marker genes
(Fig. 6A and 6C). We observed a signiﬁcant increase in chondrocytes,
ﬁbroblasts, and OCPs in the fractured bone, while Leprþ MSCs
decreased drastically, partly due to the occupancy of intramedullary
pins in the bone marrow of fractured femurs. Partition-based graph
abstraction (PAGA) analysis showed the connectivity among clusters
and the relative expression of chondro/osteogenic markers in each
cluster (Fig. 6D). We further compared the similarity of clusters in the
present fracture dataset with the time-sequenced developmental
dataset above, and found OCP1 speciﬁcally correlated to the afore-
mentioned proliferating osteochondral population (P < 0.05) (Fig. 6E).
Cell-cycle analysis conﬁrmed its proliferating status (Fig. 6F). Inter-
estingly, we noticed the increased expression of Cd168 in the re-
generated populations. The co-expression of Cd168 with Sox9 and
Runx2 was also elevated during fracture healing (Fig. 6G). To eluci-
date the cell-fate decision of Cd168þ osteochondral progenitors in
response to fracture, we reconstructed the differentiation trajectory in
regenerated clusters during fracture healing and found that Cd168þ
osteochondral progenitors gave rise to regenerated chondrocyte
populations (Fig. 6H).
We then performed immunoﬂuorescence analysis to validate the
spatial distribution of Cd168þ osteochondral progenitors during
fracture healing. We detected the expression of Cd168 within the
center of the fractured callus at day PFD7 and PFD14, which showed
low expression in the periosteum during homeostasis, suggesting
that Cd168þ cells might be recruited to the site of injury from else-
where. Our staining results demonstrated that Cd168þ osteochon-
dral progenitors co-expressed with Sox9 and Runx2 overlaid the
newly formed cartilaginous tissue, suggesting the involvement of
Cd168þ osteochondral progenitors in bone regeneration during
fracture healing (Figs. 7 and S5).
Discussion
In the current study, we identify Cd168þ mesenchymal cells as a
SSPC population in mouse limb buds and long bones according to
their highly-replicating and osteo-chondrogenic potential by scRNA-
seq analyses and in situ immunoﬂuorescence staining. In addition,
the involvement of Cd168þ SSPCs in mouse long bone fracture
repairing was also validated.
Fig. 3. Developmental dynamics of osteochondral lineage cells. A and B: Reconstructed developmental trajectories of osteochondral lineage cells colored by clusters (A) and
pseudotime value (B) predicted by Monocle3. C: Hierarchically clustered heatmap showing the scaled aggregate expression of co-regulated genes in each module across all clusters. D:
Visualization of modules related to speciﬁc state: limb bud (root, left), chondro (fate 1, middle), and osteo (fate 2, right). E: Pseudotime kinetics of indicated genes along corresponding
trajectories in each state in (D). F: Bar plot showing the GO terms enriched in each state in (D). G: Heatmap illustrating the activity of top differentially expressed TFs in each cluster
inferred by SCENIC. H: Heatmap showing the correlation of TF activity among clusters. GO, gene ontology; TF, transcription factor; SCENIC, single-cell regulatory network inference and
clustering.
R.-C. Hao, Z.-L. Li, F.-Y. Wang et al.
Journal of Genetics and Genomics 50 (2023) 702e712
706
Although scRNA-seq has been used to dissect the cellular
composition and identify rare cell populations in skeletal tissues in
recent years, the integrated scRNA-seq analysis of the spatiotem-
poral distributions of SSPC populations in mouse limb bud to post-
natal long bones have never been performed. Based on the self-
renewing capacity and osteochondrogenic potential of SSPCs, we
develop a strategy that captures highly replicating cell populations
via cell cycle analysis of integrated scRNA-seq data of embryonic
limb and postnatal long bones of mouse. Bioinformatic data
demonstrated that Cd168 enriched a mesenchymal cell population
with highly proliferative capacity in embryonic and postnatal long
bones. Previous studies have revealed that expression of Cd168
promotes numerous kinds of tumor cell growth, and enhances the
cancer metastasis, which supports our ﬁndings that Cd168 is
correlated with enhanced cell growth (Mizoguchi et al., 2014; Zhou et
al., 2014; Mizuhashi et al., 2018; Matsushita et al., 2021). Notably, a
recent study showed that the CD168 cell population remains present
in in vitro human umbilical cord derived MSCs (Li and Wang, 2021). In
addition, the CD168 on MSCs is expressed in a cell cycle-dependent
manner. Further, the CD168þ MSCs again showed similar CD168
expression patterns when they were sorted and cultured in vitro (Li
and Wang, 2021). The data further suggested that CD168 has a
role in maintaining cell self-replication.
Except for cell growth, the contribution of Cd168 to osteochon-
drogenic differentiation has been demonstrated by previous studies.
Mejia et al. (2021) explored the cellular composition of bone
morphogenetic protein 2-induced heterotopic bone in mice using
scRNA-seq. They found that Cd168 is expressed in a replicating
stem-like cell population, which replicates heterotopic bone in mus-
cles of recipient mice (Bian et al., 2013). In addition, 3D methacrylated
hyaluronic acid hydrogel environment supports the chondrogenesis of
MSCs through several cell surface receptors that are expressed by
MSCs including Cd168 (Bian et al., 2013). Moreover, the formation of
cellular microcalciﬁcations and BMP-2 expression were signiﬁcantly
increased with the increase of Cd168 expression in tumor tissues.
Thus, Cd168 proved to contribute to the cellular differentiation into
osteogenic or chondrogenic cells in multiple status. In the current
study, the GO and KEGG data suggest that Cd168þ SSPCs enrich
pivotal events during bone development and regeneration, such as
skeletal system morphogenesis, growth plate cartilage development,
and extracellular matrix organization. Moreover, Cd168 expression is
closely related with cell cycle phase transition. The immunoﬂuores-
cence data show that Cd168 co-expressed with osteochondrogenic
transcription factors Sox9 and Runx2, which further validated Cd168þ
mesenchymal cells contribute to skeletogenesis.
Moreover, our data reveal the spatial distribution of Cd168þ
SSPCs from embryonic bone formation to bone growth and regen-
eration in postnatal mice. The cells were concentrated in the con-
densated mesenchyme of limb bud, as well as the primary
ossiﬁcation center, articular surface, and metaphysis of embryonic
and fetal long bones. For postnatal mice, the Cd168þ SSPCs were
found at the superﬁcial zone of articular cartilage and the growth
plates of long bones.
However, we acknowledge that there are several limitations in our
study. First, although Cd168 was demonstrated to contribute to
embryonic and adult bone formation by scRNA-seq, lineage tracing
Fig. 4. scRNA-seq analysis identiﬁed Cd168þ proliferating osteochondral progenitors in long bone development. A: UMAP projection of established SSC markers for growth plate (top),
bone marrow (middle) and periosteum (bottom). B: Stacked bar graph showing the cell cycle distributions in OCLC subsets. C: Violin plot showing the proliferation score in each OCLC
subset. D: UMAP plot indicated the distribution of the proliferating osteochondral population. E: Feature plot (left) and violin plot (right) showing the speciﬁc expression of Cd168 in the
proliferating osteochondral population. F: UMAP visualization of the proliferating osteochondral population across different developmental time points. G: Heatmap showing the
expression of feature genes in the proliferating osteochondral population at each time point. H: Dot plot showing the enriched GO terms in the proliferating osteochondral population at
each time point. I: Violin plots showing the differential expression of limb bud mesenchyme- (top), chondro- (middle), and osteo- (bottom) related genes during development. UMAP,
uniform manifold approximation and projection; OCLCs, osteochondral lineage cells.
R.-C. Hao, Z.-L. Li, F.-Y. Wang et al.
Journal of Genetics and Genomics 50 (2023) 702e712
707
mice line may further validate the ﬁndings. Second, in vivo and in vitro
SSCs functional assays (such as self-renewal and differentiation
assays, as well as the transplantation of sorted Cd168þ cells com-
bined with reporter) will be helpful to deﬁne the osteochondrogenic
potential of Cd168þ SSPCs. Third, the application of more animal
models in future studies will deepen the understanding of the
contribution of Cd168þ SSPCs to bone formation and regeneration.
Fourth, we found that Cd168 was also enriched in limb bud mesen-
chyme 2 (LBM2) population, which indicates that Cd168 marks cell
populations from mesenchyme to OCP. Further investigations to
identify the nature of LBM2 are needed.
In summary, our ﬁndings may be helpful to understand the
spatiotemporal dynamics of osteochondrogenic cells in mouse long
bone development and regeneration at single-cell scale, and suggest
that Cd168þ SSPCs may be a novel cellular target to improve skeletal
health.
Materials and methods
Animal models and tissue collection
C57BL/6 mice used in the study were purchased from Beijing Vital
River Laboratory Animal Technology Co., Ltd. All mice were housed
in a speciﬁc pathogen-free environment and maintained under a 12-h
light/dark cycle with ambient temperature and humidity. All animal
procedures were approved by the Institutional Animal Care and Use
Committee of Military Medical Science (approval number: IACUC-
DWZX-2022-860).
For embryonic analysis, mouse embryos were generated from
timed natural matings. Hindlimb buds were dissected from the body
trunk at embryonic days E11.5, E13.5, E15.5, and E18.5, respectively.
For postnatal analysis, 3-week-old and 12-week-old female mice
were sacriﬁced by cervical dislocation. Femurs were dissected and
cleaned from surrounding soft tissues. For fracture healing analysis,
bone fractures were generated on the left femurs of 10-week-old fe-
male mice as described previously (Liu et al., 2022) with minor revi-
sion. Brieﬂy, mice were anesthetized with an intraperitoneal injection
of sodium pentobarbital (40 mg per kg) and the left hind limbs were
shaved and sterilized for surgery. A small incision was made at the
medial knee and a 0.5-mm-diameter needle was inserted into the
femoral intramedullary canal at the intercondylar notch to stabilize the
impending fracture. Then a complete transverse, mid-diaphhyseal
fracture was made using microscissors, followed by the incision su-
tured. Mice were sacriﬁced by cervical dislocation at post-fracture
day (PFD) 7, 14, and 21, and femurs were collected for further anal-
ysis. The 12-week-old wild-type females were used as control.
Histology and immunoﬂuorescence
Freshly dissected limb buds and femurs were ﬁxed in 4% para-
formaldehyde for 48 h and demineralized with 10% EDTA until
complete decalciﬁcation, then dehydrated, embedded in parafﬁn,
and sectioned at 4-mm thickness. Representative sections were
processed and stained with Safranin O/Fast Green using the modi-
ﬁed Saffron-O and Fast Green Stain Kit (For Bone) (Solarbio, G1371)
according to the manufacturer’s instruction. For immunoﬂuores-
cence (IF) analyses, antigen retrieval was performed by boiling the
slides in sodium citrate buffer (pH 6.0) (Servicebio, G1202) for 15 min,
followed by cooling for 60 min. The slides were then blocked in 10%
goat serum for 60 min and incubated with primary antibodies at 4C
overnight and with secondary antibodies for 30 min at room tem-
perature. After washing in PBS (3  5 min), nuclei were stained with
DAPI (Servicebio; G1012). Primary and secondary antibodies were
used as follows: anti-HMMR antibody (Proteintech; 15820-1-AP;
1:200), Rabbit IgG control Polyclonal antibody (Proteintech; 30000-0-
AP; 1:200), anti-SOX9 antibody (Proteintech; 67439-1-Ig; 1:200),
anti-RUNX2
antibody
(Proteintech,
20700-1-AP;
1:200),
anti-
Collagen type 2 antibody (Proteintech, 28459-1-AP, 1:200); Cy3-
Fig. 5. Involvement of Cd168þ osteochondral progenitors during embryonic and postnatal bone development. A: Representative immunoﬂuorescence images showing the expression
of Cd168 (red) in the hindlimb buds or femurs at indicated time points. The spatiotemporal expression of Cd168 alters with development. B: Representative immunoﬂuorescence images
of mouse hindlimb bud sections stained with Cd168 (red) and Sox9 (green) at indicated time points. Cd168þSox9þ cells were found in the limb bud mesenchyme condensations at
E11.5, and the articular surface of fetal bone at E18.5 (n ¼ 3). C: Representative immunoﬂuorescence images of 12-week-old mouse femur sections stained with Cd168 (red) and Sox9
(green) at indicated anatomical sites. Cd168þSox9þ cells localized in the superﬁcial layer of articular cartilage and the growth plate of postnatal femurs (n ¼ 3). All experiments were
repeated at least three times, independently. Scale bars, 100 mm (A); 50 mm (B and C).
R.-C. Hao, Z.-L. Li, F.-Y. Wang et al.
Journal of Genetics and Genomics 50 (2023) 702e712
708
conjugated goat anti-rabbit IgG (Invitrogen; A10520; 1:500), Alexa
Fluor 488-conjugated goat anti-mouse IgG (Servicebio; GB25301;
1:500), Alexa Fluor 488-conjugated goat anti-rabbit IgG (Servicebio;
GB25303; 1:500).
Time-sequenced scRNA-seq datasets of the developing
murine long bones
To investigate murine long bone development, we analyzed
publicly available scRNA-seq data derived from murine embryonic
hindlimb tissues at E11.5 (GSM4227224), E13.5 (GSM4227225),
E15.5 (GSM4227226), and E18.5 (GSM4227227), as well as mouse
long bone stromal cells isolated from 3-week-old (4 replicates from
GSM4735393 to GSM4735396) and 12-week-old mice (4 replicates
from GSM4667551 to GSM4667554). For each sample, genes
expressed in three or more cells were retained for further analysis.
Cells that expressed fewer than 500 or more than 5000 genes were
ﬁltered out. Cells that have more than 10% mitochondrial counts
were also removed.
Bone fracture dataset
The bone fracture scRNA-seq data is from Sivaraj et al. (2022)
(GEO accession number GSE154247). For each matrix, cells that
expressed fewer than 500 genes, more than 6000 genes, or greater
than 5% mitochondrial reads were removed.
Pre-processing of single-cell RNA sequencing data
After getting the UMI count matrix from GEO, we performed
quality control (QC) to ﬁlter out low-quality cells as described above.
Doublets in each dataset were predicted and removed using Dou-
bletFinder package (v.2.0.3). Cell-cycle phases were calculated and
assigned using the CellCycleScoring function in Seurat (v.4.1.1). We
further employed LogNormalize method to normalize the feature
expression in each cell by the total expression, and identiﬁed 2000
highly variable features for each dataset using FindVariableFeatures.
Datasets were scaled and mitochondrial gene percentages were
regressed
out using the
ScaleData
function for downstream
Fig. 6. Cd168þ osteochondral progenitors contribute to fracture healing. A: UMAP visualization OCLCs in control (left) and PFD14 (right) samples. B: Pie chart illustrates the proportion
of clusters in each replicates. C: Heatmap showing the expression of marker genes in each cluster. D: PAGA graphs showing the connectivity among clusters in (A) and the mean
expression of representative genes (chondrogenic: Sox9 and Acan; osteogenic: Runx2, Alpl, and Bglap2) in each cluster. The thickness of the line represents the strength of connectivity.
E: Heatmap showing the cluster correlation of fracture dataset with previous developmental datasets. F: Stacked bar plot showing the cell cycle distributions in each cluster during
fracture healing. G: UMAP projection of Cd168, Sox9, and Runx2 expression during fracture healing. H: Differentiation trajectories of regenerated clusters during fracture healing
predicted by RNA velocity (top) and CytoTRACE (bottom). UMAP, uniform manifold approximation and projection; OCLCs, osteochondral lineage cells; PFD, post-fracture day; PAGA,
partition-based graph abstraction.
R.-C. Hao, Z.-L. Li, F.-Y. Wang et al.
Journal of Genetics and Genomics 50 (2023) 702e712
709
analysis. We then conducted Principle component analysis (PCA) on
the scaled data for dimensionality reduction. “JackStraw” test and
“Elbowplot” were used to identify the most signiﬁcant PCs for
downstream analyses.
Batch effect correction, clustering, and annotation
For fast and robust dataset integration, we performed random
downsampling and applied Harmony (v.0.1.0) to reduce technical
batch effects. The top 20 components were used for UMAP dimen-
sionality reduction. Clusters were annotated based on speciﬁc
marker gene expression identiﬁed by the FindAllMarkers function.
GO pathway enrichment analysis was performed with R package
clusterProﬁler
(v.4.5.2.002).
ggplot2
(v.3.3.6)
was
used
for
visualization.
Trajectory inference analysis
To predict the differentiation trajectory of osteochondral lineage
cells during long bone development and fracture healing, R package
Monocle3 (v.1.0.0), CytoTRACE (v.0.3.3), and python package
Scanpy (v.1.9.1), scVelo (v.0.2.5) were performed according to the
tutorials.
SCENIC analysis
Python package PySCENIC (v.0.12.1) was used to calculate the
differential transcription factor activity between clusters. Raw count
matrix was used as input. The co-expression network was inferred by
GRNBoost2 and the regulons were identiﬁed by RcisTarget. The
regulon activity for each cell was scored by AUCell. The differentially
activated
regulons in
each
OCLC
subset
were
identiﬁed
by
wilcox.test.
Correlation analysis
TF activities were used as the input data to calculate the
spearman correlation among OCLC clusters, and the coefﬁcients
were visualized using pheatmap package (v.1.0.12). The correlation
of OCLC subsets between fracture datasets and developmental
datasets was examined as described previously (Cheng et al., 2021).
Isolation of primary mouse bone cells and ﬂow cytometry
We performed mechanical and chemical digestion to yield pri-
mary bone cells from adult (12-week-old) mouse long bones. First,
mouse long bones were dissected from surrounding soft tissues and
collected into a mortar added with moderate a-MEM (Gibco,
C12571500BT) supplemented with 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS)
(Kang Yuan biology, KY-01000S) (enough to soak the tissue). Bones
were crushed with a pestle to release the bone marrow and then
minced using ﬁne scissors in 2-mL centrifuge tubes (Eppendorf,
0030,120.094). Bone chips were transferred into a 15-mL centrifuge
tube (Servicebio, EP-1501-J) and added with 12 mL digestion buffer
for enzymatic digestion on a shaker at 37C for 30 min. The digestion
buffer contains 0.1% type I collagenase (Sigma-Aldrich, C0130) and
0.1% type II collagenase (Vetec, V900892), as well as 10% FBS, all
dissolved in a-MEM (Gibco, C12571500BT). Bone chips were settled
via centrifugation at 500 rpm for 3 min, and the supernatant was
ﬁltered through a 40-mm cell strainer (Biologix, 15e1040) and kept on
Fig. 7. In situ mapping of Cd168þ osteochondral progenitors during fracture healing. A: Representative Safranin O/Fast Green staining of control (top) and fractured femurs at PFD7
(middle) bud and PFD14 (bottom). Fracture healing recapitulated the process of endochondral ossiﬁcation, when stem/progenitors differentiated into chondrocytes and formed a
cartilaginous soft callus at the fractured site (n ¼ 3). B: Representative immunoﬂuorescence images showing the expression of Cd168, Runx2, and Sox9 in fractured femur sections at
PFD7. Cd168þSox9þ and Cd168þRunx2þ cells were enriched in the newly formed cartilaginous callus (n ¼ 3). C: Representative immunoﬂuorescence images showing the expression of
Cd168 and Runx2 in fractured femur sections at PFD14. Cd168þRunx2þ cells were enriched in the newly formed cartilaginous callus (n ¼ 3). All experiments were repeated at least three
times independently. PFD, post-fracture day. Scale bars, 200 mm (A); 50 mm (B); 100 mm (C).
R.-C. Hao, Z.-L. Li, F.-Y. Wang et al.
Journal of Genetics and Genomics 50 (2023) 702e712
710
ice. Then we added another 12 mL of digestion buffer into the
remaining bone chips for the second digestion. The centrifugation
and ﬁltering were repeated to remove bone chips. Red blood cells
were depleted using ammonium-chloride-potassium (ACK) lysis
buffer (Servicebio, G2015). Finally, cells were resuspended in PBS
supplemented with 1% BSA (Sigma-Aldrich, A1933) and stained for
Cd45, Ter-119, Cd31, and Cd168. Primary and secondary antibodies
were used as follows: APC anti-mouse CD45 antibody (BioLegend,
103,111), FITC anti-mouse TER-119 antibody (BioLegend, 116,205),
CD31 (PECAM-1) Monoclonal Antibody (390), FITC (eBioscience, 11-
0311-82), HMMR-Speciﬁc Polyclonal antibody (Proteintech, 15820-
1-AP), and Goat anti-Rabbit IgG (HþL) Secondary Antibody, PE-
Cyanine5.5 (Invitrogen, L42018). The experiment was repeated
three times. Analysis was performed using FlowJo (v.10.6.2).
Statistical analysis
Differential gene expression analyses were performed using
FindAllMarkers function (test.use ¼ “wilcox”, min.pct ¼ 0.5, logfc.-
threshold ¼ 0.5). Statistical analyses were performed to study the
correlations of TF activity or cluster similarity between subsets.
Statistical signiﬁcance was set at * P < 0.05. All statistical analyses
were performed using R (v.4.0.1).
Data availability
All scRNA-seq data used in this study are from publicly available
datasets in the Gene Expression Omnibus (GEO) and the accession
numbers are provided in the Materials and methods.

===== Haseeb2021_PNAS_SOX9+GrowthPlate+Dedifferentiation =====
SOX9 keeps growth plates and articular cartilage
healthy by inhibiting chondrocyte dedifferentiation/
osteoblastic redifferentiation
Abdul Haseeba,1, Ranjan Kca,1, Marco Angelozzia, Charles de Charleroya, Danielle Ruxa, Robert J. Towerb,
Lutian Yaob, Renata Pellegrino da Silvac, Maurizio Pacificia, Ling Qinb, and Véronique Lefebvrea,2
aDivision of Orthopaedic Surgery, Children’s Hospital of Philadelphia, Philadelphia, PA 19104; bDepartment of Orthopaedic Surgery, University of
Pennsylvania, Philadelphia, PA 19104; and cCenter for Applied Genomics, Children’s Hospital of Philadelphia, Philadelphia, PA 19104
Edited by Denis Duboule, University of Geneva, Geneva, Switzerland, and approved January 13, 2021 (received for review September 19, 2020)
Cartilage is essential throughout vertebrate life. It starts develop-
ing in embryos when osteochondroprogenitor cells commit to
chondrogenesis, activate a pancartilaginous program to form carti-
laginous skeletal primordia, and also embrace a growth-plate pro-
gram to drive skeletal growth or an articular program to build
permanent joint cartilage. Various forms of cartilage malformation
and degeneration diseases afflict humans, but underlying mecha-
nisms are still incompletely understood and treatment options sub-
optimal. The transcription factor SOX9 is required for embryonic
chondrogenesis, but its postnatal roles remain unclear, despite evi-
dence that it is down-regulated in osteoarthritis and heterozygously
inactivated in campomelic dysplasia, a severe skeletal dysplasia
characterized postnatally by small stature and kyphoscoliosis. Using
conditional knockout mice and high-throughput sequencing assays,
we show here that SOX9 is required postnatally to prevent growth-
plate closure and preosteoarthritic deterioration of articular carti-
lage. Its deficiency prompts growth-plate chondrocytes at all stages
to swiftly reach a terminal/dedifferentiated stage marked by ex-
pression of chondrocyte-specific (Mgp) and progenitor-specific
(Nt5e and Sox4) genes. Up-regulation of osteogenic genes (Runx2,
Sp7, and Postn) and overt osteoblastogenesis quickly ensue. SOX9
deficiency does not perturb the articular program, except in load-
bearing regions, where it also provokes chondrocyte-to-osteoblast
conversion via a progenitor stage. Pathway analyses support roles
for SOX9 in controlling TGFβ and BMP signaling activities during this
cell lineage transition. Altogether, these findings deepen our cur-
rent understanding of the cellular and molecular mechanisms that
specifically ensure lifelong growth-plate and articular cartilage vigor
by identifying osteogenic plasticity of growth-plate and articular
chondrocytes and a SOX9-countered chondrocyte dedifferentia-
tion/osteoblast redifferentiation process.
cartilage | cell differentiation | lineage determination | SOX9 |
transcriptional regulation
L
ineage commitment and differentiation are pivotal decisions
that cells make regularly to ensure proper development and
homeostasis of multicellular organisms. These decisions are con-
trolled by complex molecular networks, downstream of which
dedicated transcription factor sets deploy specific genetic pro-
grams. These decisions are often thought to be irreversible, but
evidence is mounting that even differentiated cells exhibit plas-
ticity. Their switch to another lineage can occur through trans-
differentiation (transition from one to another differentiated cell
type) or through dedifferentiation (reversal to a progenitor state)
followed by redifferentiation (into a different cell type) (1). While
enforceable experimentally in many cells (e.g., induction of plu-
ripotent stem cells), it has been documented to this date in only a
discrete number of events in vivo (e.g., epithelial-to-mesenchymal
transition, cancer, and heterotopic ossification) (2–4). Thorough
evaluation of cellular plasticity prevalence and underpinnings are
prerequisites to understanding normal and disease processes and
eventually tailor efficient disease treatments.
The skeleton is a model system to study cell fate and differ-
entiation mechanisms. It arises developmentally from multi-
potent mesenchymal cells, often called osteochondroprogenitors.
Guided by spatiotemporal cues, these cells commit to chondro-
genesis or osteoblastogenesis to build cartilage or bone, respec-
tively (5–7). Cartilage exists in several forms. Articular cartilage
(AC) is a mostly resting tissue that protects opposing bone ends
in synovial joints throughout life, whereas growth plates (GPs)
are transient, dynamic structures that drive skeletal growth while
being gradually replaced by bone (endochondral ossification).
Besides expressing a pancartilaginous (PC) program, AC chon-
drocytes (ACCs) express an articular program and GP chondrocytes
(GPCs) a GP program. Through the latter, GPCs proliferate in
columns and then undergo growth arrest, prehypertrophy, and hy-
pertrophy. After decade-long debates regarding the ultimate fate of
GPCs, recent lineage-tracing studies have provided evidence that
many cells escape apoptosis and convert into osteoblasts (OBs) (8,
9). However, many questions remain unanswered (10, 11). For in-
stance, do the same factors originally specify chondrocytes and
subsequently maintain their identity? Does osteoblastic plasticity
develop during GPC maturation or is it set in progenitors and never
lost regardless of differentiation stage, cartilage type, and age? Does
Significance
Cartilage is essential in vertebrate development and adult-
hood. Cartilage growth plates ensure skeletal growth until
closing at puberty, and articular cartilage ensures lifelong
structural and functional integrity of joints. Chondrocytes build
cartilage in development, governed by the transcription factor
SOX9. Using mouse models and transcriptome profiling ap-
proaches, we show here that SOX9 also has key roles to
maintain growth plates open postnatally and to protect adult
articular cartilage from osteoarthritic degradation. In particu-
lar, SOX9 safeguards the lineage fate of chondrocytes by pre-
venting their dedifferentiation into skeletogenic mesenchymal
progenitors followed by redifferentiation into osteoblasts.
These findings provide insights into cellular plasticity and its
molecular control in developmental, physiological, and patho-
logical processes within and beyond the skeletal system.
Author contributions: A.H., R.K., M.A., and V.L. designed research; A.H., R.K., M.A., C.d.C.,
L.Y., R.P.d.S., and V.L. performed research; A.H., R.K., M.A., D.R., R.J.T., L.Y., M.P., and L.Q.
analyzed data; and A.H., R.K., M.A., and V.L. wrote the paper.
The authors declare no competing interest.

CELL BIOLOGY
Downloaded from https://www.pnas.org by 175.122.76.13 on October 5, 2024 from IP address 175.122.76.13.
the chondrocyte-to-osteoblast switch occur via transdifferentiation
or dedifferentiation/redifferentiation? The answers to these ques-
tions should provide important insights into fundamental biological
and disease processes.
Since SOX9 heterozygous mutations were linked to campo-
melic dysplasia, a perinatally lethal skeletal malformation dis-
ease (12, 13), studies in model systems have revealed that SOX9
is a master chondrogenic transcription factor (14, 15). It may not
be a pioneer factor opening chromatin at specific loci, but it po-
tently transactivates PC genes and thereby drives embryonic chon-
drogenesis (16–19). We also reported that it keeps fetal GPCs alive
and prevents osteoblastic conversion of prehypertrophic cells (20).
This fate shift involves up-regulation of Runx2, encoding a master
transcription factor in GPCs and OBs, and activation of WNT/
β-catenin signaling, required for overt osteoblastogenesis (7, 21).
SOX9 is thus an essential effector of the PC and GP programs and
GPC lineage gatekeeper in utero.
Short stature and severe kyphoscoliosis characterize surviving
campomelic dysplasia individuals (22), strongly suggesting im-
portant roles for SOX9 in the postnatal skeleton. Sox9 inacti-
vation in chondrocytes in juvenile and adult mice was reported to
cause GP shrinking and proteoglycan loss in AC (23). However,
it remains unknown if the chondrocyte differentiation programs
and their plasticity are still SOX9 dependent. Addressing these
questions, we show here that SOX9 prevents GP closure postnatally
and protects AC from osteoarthritis. It up-regulates the PC and GP
programs, but is dispensable for the AC program, except in load-
bearing regions. Both GPCs and ACCs exhibit enduring osteogenic
plasticity, with SOX9 preventing their osteoblastogenesis through
dedifferentiation/redifferentiation.
Results
SOX9 Expression Lessens as GPs Retire and Articular Cartilage Ages.
We first asked if SOX9 expression in chondrocytes changes as
mice reach adulthood and start aging. We focused on the tibia
proximal GP and the knee, a complex osteoarthritis-prone joint.
The GP was large in 4-wk-old juveniles, lost two-thirds of its
height as mice attained adulthood 2 wk later, and further shrank
as mice reached 1 y (Fig. 1 A and B). The primary spongiosa
dwindled in adulthood, proving cessation of endochondral ossi-
fication. Thus, although GPs remain open in adult mice, they are
vestigial. SOX9 protein and RNA expression were high in most
GPCs of young mice, but weak and limited to a few chondrocytes
in aging mice (Fig. 1A and SI Appendix, Fig. S1A). AC had similar
depth and organization in juvenile and aging mice but its SOX9
expression strongly decreased with aging (Fig. 1 A and C and SI
Appendix, Fig. S1A). This SOX9 expression lessening as GPs re-
tired and AC aged suggested key roles for SOX9 to maintain
vigorous GPs and AC in youth.
SOX9 Ensures Lifelong GP and Articular Cartilage Integrity. To test
the importance of SOX9 in cartilage postnatally, we generated
mice carrying Sox9fl/fl (conditional null), AcanCreERT2 (chondrocyte
expression of tamoxifen-activatable Cre recombinase), and R26tdT
(tdTomato expression following Cre-mediated recombination)
alleles. Sox9 inactivation in 4-wk-old mice prompted GP closure
within 2 wk (SI Appendix, Fig. S1B), starting with loss of proteo-
glycans and the hypertrophic zone. Mutant mice were conse-
quently smaller than control littermates, but looked otherwise
healthy (SI Appendix, Fig. S1C). Nonmineralized AC also lost
proteoglycans, but remained otherwise intact (Fig. 2A). Similar
results were obtained when Sox9 was inactivated in 3-mo-old mice
(Fig. 2A and SI Appendix, Fig. S1D). While losing SOX9, mutant
GPCs and ACCs became tdTomato+ (SI Appendix, Fig. S1 E and
F). Steady cell density corroborated that SOX9-deprived ACCs
remained alive. Intrigued by the different fates of mutant GPs and
AC, we asked whether SOX9 depletion affected AC susceptibil-
ity to posttraumatic osteoarthritis (OA). We gave tamoxifen to
3-mo-old mice and performed DMM surgery (destabilization of
the medial meniscus) (24) 1 mo later. Two months later, sham-
operated controls had pristine AC, with an OA score close to
0 on the six-grade Osteoarthritis Research Society International
(OARSI) scale (25), whereas mutants scored at 0.5 due to pro-
teoglycan loss (Fig. 2B). DMM-subjected controls showed AC
proteoglycan loss and superficial erosion, scoring of 2, whereas
mutants massively eroded AC, scoring at 4. SOX9 is thus re-
quired to keep GPs open and AC integrity, including resistance
to osteoarthritis.
SOX9 Ensures Survival and Proliferation of GPs but Not Articular
Chondrocytes. GPCs proliferate actively at the columnar stage,
growth arrest upon prehypertrophy, and many ultimately die,
whereas most ACCs are growth arrested and long lived. EdU
(5-ethynyl-2′-deoxyuridine) incorporation assays showed that
GPCs quickly stopped proliferating following Sox9 inactivation
(SI Appendix, Fig. S2A). TUNEL (terminal deoxynucleotidyl
transferase dUTP nick end labeling) assays detected an increased
death rate of mutant GPCs, starting with mature cells and then
spreading to others (SI Appendix, Fig. S2B). MMP13 (matrix
metalloproteinase 13) immunostaining showed that cell death
coincided with endochondral ossification. In contrast, cell prolif-
eration, cell death, and MMP13 signals remained quasi absent in
mutant AC (SI Appendix, Fig. S2 C and D). SOX9 is thus needed
to keep GPCs, but not ACCs, proliferative and alive.
SOX9 Is Required for High Expression of Pancartilaginous and GP
Genes, and Its Loss Is Not Compensated for by SOX8. To gauge the
SOX9 dependency of the GPC and ACC transcriptomes, we
performed RNA sequencing (RNA-seq) assays on laser capture-
microdissected tissues from Sox9 control and mutant mice 7 and
14 d after tamoxifen treatment and from Sox8/Sox9 control and
mutant mice 14 d after tamoxifen treatment (SI Appendix, Fig.
S3 A and B). We included Sox8/9 mutants because initial analysis
of Sox9 mutant data revealed that Sox8, which acts redundantly
with Sox9 in other lineages (26, 27), was expressed weakly in control
GPs and AC and still detectable in Sox9 mutant tissues (Dataset S1
and SI Appendix, Fig. S4 A–C). However, since AC and GPs were
indistinguishable in control and Sox8fl/flAcanCreERT2/+ mice and in
Sox9fl/flAcanCreERT2/+ and Sox8fl/flSox9fl/flAcanCreERT2/+ mice (SI
Appendix, Fig. S4D), we concluded that SOX8 has no essential
functions in chondrocytes and we coanalyzed RNA-seq data from
Sox9 and Sox8/Sox9 mutants.
Hundreds of genes were significantly up- and down-regulated
in Sox9 mutant AC and GPs (SI Appendix, Fig. S3C, and Dataset
S2), and pathway analyses indicated their primary relevance to
cartilage and bone (Datasets S3 and S4). Among them, PC and
GP markers, including Col2a1 (collagen-II), Acan, Hapln1 (link
protein), Fgfr3 (fibroblast growth factor receptor-3, columnar
GPC marker), Ihh (Indian hedgehog, prehypertrophic marker),
and Col10a1 (collagen-X, hypertrophic marker) were sizably
down-regulated (Dataset S1 and Fig. 3A). RNA in situ hybrid-
ization confirmed the Acan, Ihh, and Col10a1 down-regulation
and quick turnover of the GP hypertrophic zone following Sox9
and Sox8/9 inactivation (Fig. 3B and SI Appendix, Fig. S4 E and
F). A few columnar cells became weakly positive for Ihh and
Col10a1 in mutant GPs, and for Ihh in mutant AC, and these
markers were still detectable at RNA and protein levels in AC
months after Sox9 inactivation (SI Appendix, Fig. S4 E and G).
Sox5 and Sox6, which encode transcription factors enhancing
SOX9 activity in chondrocytes, had their expression unchanged
in mutant GPs and AC (SI Appendix, Fig. S4 H and I). Lacking
transactivation capability, their proteins, however, likely did not
suffice to maintain PC and GP genes on (18, 28).
We concluded that SOX9 is dispensable, as is SOX8, to keep
the PC and GP programs on, but is required for robust expres-
sion of these programs in fully differentiated ACCs and GPCs.

SOX9 Backs the Articular Program in Load-Bearing Regions and
Inhibits Early-Osteoarthritis Signature Genes. The AC program
contains ACC-specific and ACC/FLS-specific (shared by ACC
and synovial fibroblasts) genes, whose SOX9 dependence re-
mains unknown. Prg4 (lubricin), an ACC/FLS marker, was par-
tially down-regulated, but still strongly expressed in mutant AC
(Fig. 4A and Dataset S1). This finding was not unexpected since
FLSs do not express Sox9 (SI Appendix, Fig. S4J). The ACC-
specific markers Cytl1 (cytokine-like protein-1), Cilp (cartilage
intermediate-layer protein), Smoc2 (SPARC-related modular
calcium-binding protein-2), and Clu (clusterin) had their ex-
pression slightly altered in mutant AC (Fig. 4A and Dataset S1).

controls and mutants calculated using Student’s t test.

SOX9 keeps growth plates and articular cartilage healthy by inhibiting chondrocyte
dedifferentiation/osteoblastic redifferentiation
https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.2019152118
CELL BIOLOGY
Downloaded from https://www.pnas.org by 175.122.76.13 on October 5, 2024 from IP address 175.122.76.13.
Noticeably, weeks and even months after Sox9 or Sox8/Sox9 in-
activation, Prg4, Smoc2, and Clu expression was virtually lost in
the center of femoral condyle and tibial plateau AC, regions
subject to high mechanical load, but was largely intact elsewhere
(Fig. 4 B and C and SI Appendix, Fig. S4 K and L). The AC
program thus requires SOX9’s support only in load-bearing re-
gions. Interestingly too, Smoc2 and Clu were weakly expressed in
the first and last layers of control GPs, presumably in stem cells
and terminal chondrocytes, respectively, and were expressed in
most remaining GPCs 2 wk after Sox9 inactivation. Expression of
these genes is thus likely SOX9 independent in AC and GP.
Early-OA signs in SOX9-deficient AC and rapid tissue de-
generation upon trauma could be due to cartilage matrix deficiency
only, or also to wider gene expression changes. OA was identified as
a top canonical pathway altered in mutant AC (Datasets S3 and
S4). Down-regulated genes included Timp3 (inhibitor of matrix
metalloproteinases) and Frzb (WNT signaling inhibitor), and up-
regulated genes included Adamts5 (metalloproteinase targeting
aggrecan) (Fig. 4D). Other genes up-regulated in human and canine
OA and SOX9-null AC included genes for matrix components (e.g.,
Vcan, versican, and Tnc, tenascin-C) and catabolic factors (e.g.,
Ctsb, cathepsin-B) (29–31). Inflammation contributes to OA (32),
but typical inflammation markers were not detected (e.g., IL6, in-
terleukin-6) or altered (e.g., S100a8, alarmin) in early-OA human
knees (30) and SOX9-deficient AC. SOX9 expression is thus re-
quired to maintain lifelong AC integrity.
SOX9 Inhibits Osteoblastogenesis of GP and Articular Chondrocytes.
We next asked if SOX9-deprived ACCs and GPCs converted in
OBs. Expression of Runx2 and Sp7, encoding essential osteogenic
transcription factors, were globally unchanged in mutant GPs and
AC (Fig. 5A). Runx2 RNA was easily detected in control hyper-
trophic GPCs and OBs, but hardly in ACCs and resting/columnar
GPCs, and Sp7 RNA was reliably seen only in OBs (Fig. 5B and SI
Appendix, Fig. S5A). Their RNA levels increased in remaining
GPCs by day 4, and even more by day 7 of Sox9 loss. They also
became readily detectable in mutant AC, but only in the femur
load-bearing region. Col1a1 (collagen-I, early OB marker) and
Bglap/Bglap2 (osteocalcin, mature OB marker) were globally up-
regulated in mutant AC and GPs (Fig. 5A) and detected in the
same regions as Runx2 and Sp7 (Fig. 5B and SI Appendix, Fig.
S5B). The RUNX2 and osteocalcin proteins became visible in
mutant GPCs and ACCs 7 to 10 d after Sox9 inactivation
(Fig. 5C). Both proteins could be seen throughout AC by day 14,
but most strongly in load-bearing regions. AC still expressed
RUNX2, osteocalcin, and collagen-I months after Sox9 inactivation
(SI Appendix, Fig. S5C), but remained unmineralized (SI Appendix,
Fig. S5D), possibly because of incomplete matrix turnover.
We then generated Sox9+/+R26tdT/+AcanCreERT2/+ and
Sox9fl/flR26tdT/+AcanCreERT2/+ mice to ask whether mutant GPC-
derived OBs survived in endochondral bone. Of all endochondral
bone cells present below control GPs, 30 to 40% were tdTomato+
2 and 9 mo after tamoxifen treatment, and the same was true for
bone cells occupying the former mutant GPs (SI Appendix, Fig.
S5E). This result suggested that Sox9 deletion did not affect the
fate of GPC-derived OBs.
By showing OB marker expression in cells still embedded in
original cartilage matrix, these data convincingly showed that both
GPCs and ACCs exhibit osteogenic plasticity throughout life and
that SOX9 prevents their premature or ectopic osteoblastogenesis.
Fig. 3.
SOX9 ensures high-level expression of PC and GP markers. (A) RNA-seq assay. Three pairs of control and mutant mice received tamoxifen at 3 mo and
were analyzed 7 or 14 d later, as indicated. Averages of NRPKM values obtained for control and mutant samples, and statistically significant (P < 0.05)
differences obtained in paired t tests for control and mutant data are indicated. (B) In situ detection of Acan, Ihh, and Col10a1 RNA (magenta) in tibia GP and
knee AC sections. Sox9 control and mutant mice were given tamoxifen at 3 mo and analyzed at indicated times. Ihh RNA signals were amplified with Adobe
Photoshop. Arrows, cells positive for Ihh RNA. Counterstaining, hematoxylin. See SI Appendix, Fig. S4E, for entire-knee pictures.

SOX9 keeps growth plates and articular cartilage healthy by inhibiting chondrocyte
dedifferentiation/osteoblastic redifferentiation
Downloaded from https://www.pnas.org by 175.122.76.13 on October 5, 2024 from IP address 175.122.76.13.
Chondrocytes Transit through Skeletal Progenitor Stages as They
Convert into Osteoblasts. To test whether chondrocytes convert
into OBs via transdifferentiation or dedifferentiation/rediffer-
entiation in wild-type and SOX9-deficient conditions, we treated
control and mutant mice with tamoxifen at postnatal day 8 (P8)
or P14, i.e., when GPs are very active and primary and secondary
ossification centers fully developing. Since major changes could
already be seen on GP and AC histological sections 5 d later (SI
Appendix, Fig. S6A), we chose this time point for single-cell
RNA-seq (scRNA-seq) analysis. Uniform manifold approxima-
tion and projection (UMAP) of gene expression profiles performed
with mesenchymal and skeletal cells identified 35 subpopulations,
including progenitor cells, ACCs, and GPCs and OBs (Fig. 6 A and
B and SI Appendix, Fig. S7 A and B and Datasets S5 and S6).
Pseudotemporal cell trajectory analysis projected that control
GPCs became OBs through sequentially undergoing hypertrophic
and terminal differentiation (clusters 29 and 30), and reaching
mesenchymal (cluster 31), OB progenitor (cluster 32), preosteo-
blast (cluster 33), and osteoblast states (cluster 34) (Fig. 6C).
Similarly, scRNA-seq data generated for endochondral cells from
1- to 1.5-mo-old mice linked GPCs to OBs via equivalent terminal
GPCs, mesenchymal, and osteoprogenitor stages (33) (SI Appendix,
Fig. S7 C–G and Dataset S7). Interestingly, most GPCs in mutant
pups were projected to skip late proliferative stages, prehyper-
trophy, and hypertrophy, and to directly reach terminal GPC,
mesenchymal, and osteoblast progenitor stages on their path
toward osteoblastogenesis (Fig. 6 A–C).
Validating these predictions in real time and real space, RNA
hybridization assays showed that Sox9 inactivation prompted
proliferating GPCs to activate markers of terminal GPCs, in-
cluding Col10a1 and Mgp (matrix GLA protein); terminal GPCs
and skeletal progenitors, including Nt5e (ecto-5′-nucleotidase or
CD73) (34, 35), Sox4 (36–38), Mmp13 and Ibsp (integrin-binding
sialoprotein); skeletal progenitors, such as Postn (periostin),
Col3a1, and Mmp2; and pre-OBs and OBs, including Sp7, Co-
l1a1, and Bglap (Fig. 6D and SI Appendix, Figs. S6 B and C and
S8 A and B). Several markers, including Mgp, Nt5e, Mmp13, Ibsp,
Col3a1, and Sp7 were also up-regulated or ectopically activated
in subsets of mutant ACCs. Importantly, the detection of all these
RNAs in cells that were still embedded in original GPs and AC
tissue matrix gave assurance that chondrocytes themselves, and
not invading endochondral progenitor cells, were undergoing
osteoblastogenesis.
Of note, Sox2, Nanog, and Oct4 expression, which marks
pluripotent stem cells and was detected in chondrocytes be-
coming OBs in mouse bone fracture callus (39), was undetect-
able in our mutant samples (Datasets S1, S5, and S6). Gdf5
(growth-and-differentiation factor-5) expression, marking em-
bryonic joint progenitors (40, 41), was also undetected. Pthlh
(parathyroid hormone-related protein) expression, marking GP
stem cells (42), remained confined to top-layer mutant GPCs (SI
Appendix, Fig. S8C). Thus, SOX9-deficient chondrocytes unlikely
reverted to a pluripotent, joint progenitor or GP stem-cell stage.
Together, these data indicated that GPCs and ACCs attain
osteoblastogenesis upon physiological or forced loss of Sox9
expression by transiting through skeletal progenitor states, and
thus through dedifferentiation/redifferentiation.
SOX9
May
Involve
Skeletogenic
Pathways
in
the
Control
of
Chondrocyte Fate. Looking for mechanisms whereby SOX9 may
inhibit chondrocyte-to-osteoblast conversion, we focused on the
TGFβ (transforming growth factor-β) and BMP (bone morpho-
genetic protein) pathways, based on their key roles in skeletal
development, adult homeostasis, and cell fate determination.

https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.2019152118
CELL BIOLOGY
Downloaded from https://www.pnas.org by 175.122.76.13 on October 5, 2024 from IP address 175.122.76.13.
TGFβ keeps ACCs and GPCs at immature stages at least in part
by lowering RUNX2 activity and stability (43–46) and also pro-
motes such cell fate changes as epithelial-to-mesenchymal tran-
sition (4). Tgfbr3, the TGFβ receptor gene most expressed in
control AC, was down-regulated in mutant AC, whereas other
TGFβ ligand and receptor genes were unaffected in mutant AC
and GPs (Dataset S1). Accordingly, TGFβ signaling, assessed by
pSMAD2/3 immunostaining, lost partial strength in mutant AC a
week after Sox9 inactivation and continued to underperform for
months (Fig. 7A). BMP signaling promotes GPC maturation and
OB differentiation, namely by up-regulating RUNX2 RNA and
protein levels, and also permits chondrogenesis and osteogenesis
from progenitor cells (47–50). While BMP ligand and receptor
genes were globally unaffected by Sox9 inactivation, Chrdl2,
Grem1, Nog, and Bambi, encoding secreted inhibitors, were
down-regulated in mutant AC, and Grem1 and Nog were also
down-regulated in mutant GPs (Dataset S1). Accordingly, BMP
signaling, assessed by pSMAD1/5/9 immunostaining, increased in
mutant AC and GPs by day 7 after Sox9 loss and remained el-
evated for months in AC (Fig. 7B).
To test how TGFβ and BMP signaling could contribute to
SOX9-deficient chondrocyte fate changes, we transduced Sox9fl/fl
primary chondrocytes with GFP or CRE-encoding adenovirus,
and treated them with TGFβ1 or BMP2. TGFβ1 partially down-
regulated Sox9 expression, but did not change the expression of
the hypertrophic GPC marker Col10a1, terminal GPC marker,
Sox4, and OB-determining genes Runx2 and Sp7 (Fig. 7C). In-
terestingly, it up-regulated the expression of the progenitor
marker Col3a1 in synergy with Sox9 inactivation, suggesting that,
since TGFβ signaling was still active in Sox9 mutant GPs in vivo,
it could have contributed to the reversal of mutant chondrocytes
to progenitor states (Fig. 7C). Sox9 inactivation and BMP2
Fig. 5.
Sox9 inactivation prompts chondrocyte osteoblastogenesis. (A) RNA-seq assay of OB markers. Data were generated and plotted as in Figs. 3A and 4D.
(B) In situ detection of Runx2, Sp7, and Bglap/Bglap2 RNAs (magenta) in tibia proximal GP and knee AC. Sox9 control and mutant mice received tamoxifen at 3
mo and were analyzed at indicated times. Counterstaining, hematoxylin. RNA signals for Runx2 and Sp7 were amplified using Adobe Photoshop. See SI
Appendix, Fig. S5A, for entire-knee sections. (C) In situ detection of tdTomato (red) and RUNX2 and osteocalcin immunostaining (green). Sox9 control and
mutant mice received tamoxifen at 3 mo and were analyzed at indicated times. Cell nuclei are stained with DAPI. Blue brackets, GP. Note the progressive
disappearance of RUNX2 signal in the mutant GP 2 and 4 d after tamoxifen treatment, reflecting the loss of hypertrophic GPCs and the fact that non-
hypertrophic GPCs have not up-regulated RUNX2 expression yet.

SOX9 keeps growth plates and articular cartilage healthy by inhibiting chondrocyte
dedifferentiation/osteoblastic redifferentiation
Downloaded from https://www.pnas.org by 175.122.76.13 on October 5, 2024 from IP address 175.122.76.13.
similarly down-regulated Col10a1 expression, but did not syner-
gize, suggesting that Sox9 inactivation in vivo may have inhibited
the GP program at least in part through increased BMP signaling
(Fig. 7C). BMP2 showed a trend toward down-regulating Sox4 and
Col3a1 expression, indicating that it unlikely promoted chon-
drocyte terminal maturation and dedifferentiation. Interestingly,
although it did not change the Runx2 RNA level, it greatly in-
creased the Sp7 RNA and RUNX2 and SP7 protein levels, es-
pecially in the absence of Sox9, strongly suggesting that the
increase in BMP signaling observed in mutant ACCs and GPCs
in vivo contributed to converting chondrocytes into osteoblasts
(Fig. 7 C and D).
We concluded that SOX9 could control the fate of GPCs and
ACCs at least in part by modulating skeletogenic pathways, in-
cluding progenitor-inductive and osteoinductive activities of
TGFβ and BMP signaling, respectively.
Discussion
Keeping GPs open throughout development and AC intact
throughout adulthood is essential for vertebrate health. The present
