5 differentially expressed TFs includes LEF1 (P = 3.94E-48), 
FOXO1 (P = 6.59E-26), RORA (P = 4.46E-24), BHLHE41 
(P = 6.68E-24), SOX5 (P = 3.45E-20). The TF differentially 
expressed on day 14 was also analyzed. FOXQ1 (P = 1.23E-
07) and TBX3 (P = 1.77E-05) were identified as markedly 
differentially expressed TFs on day 14 (Table 4).
Validation on transcriptional factors involved 
in chondrogenesis
During MSCs chondrogenesis, the initial stage (before 
day 7) was regarded as the most critical stage in 
determining MSCs’ fate [16]. Therefore, we focused on 
the TFs that are differentially expressed on day 7. The role 
of LEF1, FOXO1, and SOX5 in MSCs chondrogenesis has 
been identified [17–19]. Because our previous studies 
show that melatonin promotes chondrogenic differen-
tiation [15, 20], we are particularly interested in RORA, 
the nuclear receptor for melatonin. Treatment through-
out the induction process with an antagonist for RORA 
SR3335 (1 μM) attenuated the effect of induced chondro-
genesis. While continued treatment with 1 μM SR1078, 
an agonist for RORA, promoted chondrogenesis (Fig. 5A 
Fig. 2  Volcano plot showing DEGs. A DEGs between MSC and C7 group. B DEGs between C7 and C14 group. C DEGs between C14 and C21 group. 
D DEGs between C21 and CHO group
Page 6 of 12
Liang et al. BMC Musculoskeletal Disorders          (2022) 23:985 
& B). The expression of chondrogenesis marker genes 
COL2A1 and ACAN were upregulated upon SR1078 
treatment after 7 and 14  days of induction (Fig.  5C). 
When treated with SR3335 for 7  days, the expression 
level of COL2A1 was suppressed, but no significant dif-
ference between the vehicle and SR3335 in 14  days 
(Fig. 5D).
Discussion
In this study, we identified the transcriptional profile 
during chondrogenesis of human MSCs. Recent studies 
have demonstrated that MSCs are promising therapies 
for treating musculoskeletal disorders [21, 22]. Inducing 
MSCs chondrogenesis is an especially promising strat-
egy to treat cartilage defects because of its low immuno-
genicity and capability to repair cartilage defects in situ. 
However, the mechanism underlying different stages dur-
ing chondrogenesis remained relatively unknown. There-
fore, identifying key genes and pathways may provide 
new insight into optimizing conditions of chondrogen-
esis both in vitro and in vivo.
Cartilage regeneration is a promising disease-modify-
ing OA therapy [23]. The cartilage regeneration process 
involves stem cell differentiation and ECM synthesis. 
However, the therapeutic application of induced chondro-
genesis is limited, partially due to the declined differentia-
tion capacity of MSCs. Osteoarthritis and cartilage defects 
create an inflammatory microenvironment, which makes 
MSCs aberrantly differentiate to the fibroblast phenotype 
[24]. Therefore, understanding the key regulatory factors 
can contribute to optimizing the chondrogenic method 
and microenvironment. For example, activating the pro-
inflammatory IL-6/STAT-3 pathway promotes chon-
drogenesis, revealing that inflammation may play a dual 
role in regulating chondrogenesis [25]. However, over-
production and accumulation of the pro-inflammatory 
factor IL-1β hamper the chondrogenic differentiation of 
MSCs [14].In this study, we found that neutrophil-related 
cytokines are significantly upregulated during chondro-
genesis. Similar to our findings, another study found that 
immune response-related DEGs, including CXCL8 and 
CCL20, are upregulated during chondrogenesis [26].

The PPI analysis suggested some important hub genes 
in the network, as shown in Fig.  3B, including CXCL8, 
TLR2, CCL20, MMP3, BCL2A1, SAA1, RAC2, and IL24. 
These genes are closely related to regulating inflammation 
and response to inflammatory cytokines. Interestingly, 
top DEGs and hub genes reveal some common genes, 
including CXCL8, CCL20, SAA1, and RAC2. CXCL8, a 
member of the CXC chemokine family, encodes the inter-
leukin-8 (IL-8) protein. The role of IL-8 in chondrogen-
esis remained controversial. IL-8 stimulates chondrocyte 
growth and differentiation and may contribute to carti-
lage defect repair [27, 28]. In contrast, other studies sug-
gested that IL-8 may promote cartilage destruction during 
the pathogenesis of osteoarthritis [29, 30]. Therefore, the

The GO and KEGG analysis of the 85 DEGs between C7 and C14 group

Table 3  Top 5 markedly differentially expressed transcriptional factors 7 days after induced chondrogenesis
Table 4   Markedly differentially expressed transcriptional factors 14 days after induced chondrogenesis
Page 10 of 12
Liang et al. BMC Musculoskeletal Disorders          (2022) 23:985 
role of CXCL8 in chondrogenesis remains unclear and 
requires further exploration. As for TLR2 (encoding Toll-
like receptor 2), a study by Lee et al. demonstrated that 
TLR2 promotes chondrogenic differentiation and conse-
quent calcification in vascular smooth muscle cells [31]. 
Moreover, studies found that TLR2 is expressed in MSCs, 
and treatment with TLR2 agonists promotes chondrogen-
esis [32]. Interestingly, stimulation with TLR2 agonists 
led to increased production of IL-8 in MSCs in a dose-
dependent manner [33]. Therefore, TLR2 and CXCL8 
may represent a new pathway in regulating chondrogen-
esis and are worth further exploration.
Fig. 5  RORA promotes MSCs chondrogenesis. A Cartilage pellets collected 7 and 14 days after inducing MSCs chondrogenesis. Indicated treatment 
was added to the culture media. The cartilage pellets were observed with stereoscopic microscope. B The diameter (mm) of cartilage pellet after 
7 and 14 days of induced chondrogenesis, 6 pellets were used for measurement in each group. C RT-qPCR analysis was conducted to assess 
the mRNA expression of COL2A1 and ACAN in chondrocyte pellet with the addition of indicated treatments for 7 days. D RT-qPCR analysis was 
conducted to assess the mRNA expression of COL2A1 and ACAN in chondrocyte pellet with the addition of indicated treatments for 14 days.* 
p ≤ 0.05 and ** p ≤ 0.01 compared to the vehicle treated group
Page 11 of 12
Liang et al. BMC Musculoskeletal Disorders          (2022) 23:985 
	
We also identified several critical TFs that participated 
in chondrogenesis, including LEF1, FOXO1, RORA, 
BHLHE41, and SOX5. LEF1 forms a transcriptional 
complex with β-catenin and acts as an important down-
stream effector of the Wnt/β-catenin signaling pathway. 
A study showed that LEF1 cooperates with WNT5A and 
WNT11, directs MSCs chondrogenesis, and prevents 
chondrocytes from hypertrophic differentiation [17]. 
Besides, FOXO1 knockdown attenuated chondrogenesis 
by regulating SOX9 expression [18]. The role of RORA 
in MSCs chondrogenesis has not been investigated. We 
found that RORA pharmacological inhibition attenuated 
MSCs chondrogenesis while activating RORA promoted 
chondrogenesis. Previous studies suggested that RORA 
plays a contributory role in the pathogenesis of osteo-
arthritis by regulating cholesterol metabolism and IL6/
STAT3 pathway [34, 35]. RORA also regulates chondro-
cytes hypertrophy in tibia organ cultures isolated from 
E15.5 mice [36]. These results suggested that different 
from LEF1, RORA may promote chondrogenesis and 
chondrocyte hypertrophy simutaneously.
In conclusion, this study provided insight into the gene 
expression profile during MSCs chondrogenesis. The hub 
genes and key transcriptional factors were identified, 
which may assist the research for cartilage regeneration 
and the development of osteoarthritis in future studies.
Abbreviation
MSCs: Mesenchymal stem cells; TF: Transcription factor; FC: Fold change; 
DEGs: Differentially expressed genes; KEGG: Kyoto Encyclopedia of Genes 
and Genomes; GO: Gene Ontology; PPI: Protein–protein interaction; RIN: RNA 
integrity number.

===== Munoz2018_MechDev_Xenopus+SkullBoneRegeneration =====
Contents lists available at ScienceDirect
Mechanisms of Development
journal homepage: www.elsevier.com/locate/mod
Bone regeneration after traumatic skull injury in Xenopus tropicalis
David Muñoza,b,1, Héctor Castilloa,b,1, Juan Pablo Henríqueza,c, Sylvain Marcellinia,b,⁎
a Group for the Study of Developmental Processes (GDeP), Department of Cell Biology, Faculty of Biological Sciences, University of Concepción, Chile
b Laboratory of Development and Evolution (LADE), University of Concepción, Chile
c Center for Advanced Microscopy (CMA Bio-Bio), University of Concepción, Chile
A R T I C L E I N F O
Keywords:
Osteogenesis
Bone regeneration
Xenopus tropicalis
Traumatic skull injury
A B S T R A C T
The main purpose of regenerative biology is to improve human health by exploiting cellular and molecular
mechanisms favoring tissue repair. In recent years, non-mammalian vertebrates have emerged as powerful
model organisms to tackle the problem of tissue regeneration. Here, we analyze the process of bone repair in
metamorphosing Xenopus tropicalis tadpoles subjected to traumatic skull injury. Five days after skull perforation,
a dense and highly vascularized mesenchymal is apparent over the injury site. Using an in vivo bone staining
procedure based on independent pulses of Alizarin red and Calcein green, we show that the deposition of new
bone matrix completely closes the wound in 15 days. The absence of cartilage implies that bone repair follows an
intramembranous ossiﬁcation route. Collagen second harmonic imaging reveals that while a well-organized
lamellar type of bone is deposited during development, a woven type of bone is produced during the early-phase
of the regeneration process. Osteoblasts lying against the regenerating bone robustly express ﬁbrillar collagen
1a1, SPARC and Dlx5. These analyses establish Xenopus tropicalis as a new model system to improve traumatic
skull injury recovery.
1. Introduction
Developmental and regenerative skeletogenesis can follow two
distinct developmental routes, known as intramembranous or en-
dochondral ossiﬁcation (Hartmann, 2009). The bone of the skull, or
calvaria, develops through intramembranous ossiﬁcation (Long, 2011).
During this process, osteogenic mesenchymal cells directly diﬀerentiate
into osteoblasts, the cells in charge of depositing the bone matrix and of
creating the adequate biochemical conditions for its mineralization
(Landis and Jacquet, 2013; Stock, 2015). Osteocytes subsequently be-
come embedded within the newly formed bone and regulate its rate of
deposition and resorption (Bellido, 2014). Long bones develop through
endochondral ossiﬁcation, a multistep process requiring (i) an initial
phase of chondrogenesis and the formation of a cartilage template
whose growth progressively sculpts the ﬁnal size and shape of each
skeletal element and (ii) the removal of the cartilage and its subsequent
replacement by bone tissue (Mackie et al., 2008). One classical assay to
study calvaria regeneration in mammals consists in drilling a circular
lesion through the skull bone and observing how intramembranous
ossiﬁcation progressively closes the lesion (Benson et al., 2012;
Yamaguchi et al., 2014). Above a critical size, calvaria defects are un-
able to regenerate, and calvaria bone grafting procedures are often
subjected to failure (Almubarak et al., 2016; Hall, 2005). There is
therefore considerable interest in improving intramembranous bone
regeneration in mammals, as reﬂected by recent studies reporting novel
protocols and experimental approaches for therapeutic calvaria damage
repair (Almubarak et al., 2016; Samsonraj et al., 2017; Sugamori et al.,
2016).
Non-mammalian vertebrates are largely superior to mammals with
respect to their regenerative capabilities (Godwin, 2014; Lee-Liu et al.,
2013). An active ﬁeld of research in regenerative medicine is dedicated
to understanding how teleosts, lungﬁsh, and amphibians can regenerate
a variety of organs and body structures (Godwin, 2014; Haas and
Whited, 2017; Lee-Liu et al., 2013; Nogueira et al., 2016; Song et al.,
2010). Regarding skeletal regeneration, most studies focus on appen-
dages such as ﬁns and limbs (Haas and Whited, 2017; Nogueira et al.,
2016; Rao et al., 2014), as well as jaws (Charbonneau et al., 2016;
Kurosaka et al., 2008; Paul et al., 2016). More recently, a traumatic
skull injury assay was implemented in adult zebraﬁsh, pointing at a
crucial role for osteoblastic dediﬀerentiation in the process of bone
regeneration (Geurtzen et al., 2014).
The Xenopus laevis and tropicalis pipidae frogs are renowned for their
healing and regenerative capacity and have intensively been used to
study how the skin, spinal cord, tail, limb or lens recover from tissue
https://doi.org/10.1016/j.mod.2018.06.007
Received 28 February 2018; Received in revised form 29 June 2018; Accepted 30 June 2018
⁎ Corresponding author at: Group for the Study of Developmental Processes (GDeP), Department of Cell Biology, Faculty of Biological Sciences, University of Concepción, Chile.

E-mail address: smarcellini@udec.cl (S. Marcellini).
Mechanisms of Development 154 (2018) 153–161
Available online 02 July 2018
0925-4773/ © 2018 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
T
damage (Beck et al., 2009; Li et al., 2016). With respect to skeletal
regeneration through endochondral ossiﬁcation, Xenopus laevis was
successfully used to elaborate scaﬀolds improving the repair of critical
size bone defects (Cameron et al., 2013), and to study the early events
that follow hind limb amputation (Beck et al., 2009; Rao et al., 2014;
Satoh et al., 2005). However, intramembranous-driven bone re-
generation has not yet been described in the Xenopus genus or in any
amphibian species. Here, we report the ﬁrst traumatic skull recovery
assay in Xenopus tropicalis, and show that the newly formed bone closes
a 500 μm diameter lesion in 15 days. Considering that the post-opera-
tion survival rate is very high and that Xenopus species are easily
amenable to chemical and genetic screens, we foresee that this novel
assay will facilitate the identiﬁcation and functional characterization of
key regulators of intramembranous bone regeneration.
2. Results
2.1. A novel traumatic skull injury assay in Xenopus tropicalis
In an attempt to implement a novel bone regeneration assay in
Xenopus tropicalis, we used a 500 μm diameter drill to manually perform
traumatic skull injury. In an earlier work, we had reported a develop-
mental timetable of skull ossiﬁcation in Xenopus tropicalis, showing that
frontoparietal bone mineralization initiates from the lateral sides at
stage NF54 and progresses towards the midline where the suture closes
at stage NF60 (Espinoza et al., 2010). Hence, we used individuals whose
calvaria was fully closed, ranging from stage NF60 (pre-metamorphosis
tadpoles) to stage NF66 (recently metamorphosed froglets). Im-
mediately after damage, the skull skin shows an open wound (Fig. 1A)
below which the calvaria clearly exhibits a circular lesion (Fig. 1B). At
5 days post-injury (dpi), the impact site is covered by regenerated skin
(Fig. 1C, D). We then performed a series of whole mount staining
procedures to describe the damaged zone at 0 and 5 dpi. The injury
procedure was very eﬀective, as evidenced by a hollow lesion through
the calvaria at 0 dpi (Fig. 1E). At 5 dpi, the injury site is completely
ﬁlled by an Alcian blue-negative regeneration plug, ruling out the
presence of cartilage (Fig. 1E′). To observe the early phase of the re-
generation process in greater details, dissected skulls were also ex-
amined by confocal microscopy (Fig. 1F, F′ and Supplementary Files S1
and S2). On the one hand, Alexa Fluor 488-phalloidin, which speciﬁ-
cally stains the actin cytoskeleton, was used to label cell bodies. This
approach conﬁrmed that the hollow lesion is largely devoid of cellular
structures at 0 dpi (Fig. 1F and Supplementary Files S1). By contrast, at
5 dpi, the regeneration plug contains densely packed and disorganized
cellular masses and is invaded by numerous blood vessels converging
towards the injury site (Fig. 1F′, Supplementary File S2). On the other
hand, we used Alizarin red staining to characterize the distribution of
mineralized matrix at the impact site. Importantly, a substantial
amount of bone matrix is evident within the circular lesion immediately
after damage (Fig. 1F and Supplementary Files S1). Hence, we will
employ the “old bone” terminology to diﬀerentiate the pre-lesion ma-
trix (formed during larval development) from the newly regenerated
matrix. Three-dimensional reconstructions revealed that some dis-
lodged old bone matrix remains attached to the periphery of the drilled
area (Supplementary Files S1 and S2, and “o” label in Fig. 1F, F′). In
addition, small Alizarin red-positive deposits are present within the
regeneration plug at 5 dpi (Fig. 1F′ and supplementary File S2). Dating
mineralized matrix deposition is crucial to determine if such deposits
were produced during regeneration, or if they are old bone remnants
resulting from the drilling procedure.
2.2. Visualization of ossiﬁcation patterns during Xenopus larval bone
development
In order to simultaneously visualize mineralized matrices of dif-
ferent ages, we adapted an in vivo bone labelling assay previously
Fig. 1. A novel traumatic skull injury assay in Xenopus tropicalis.
Stage NF66 Xenopus specimens were subjected to skull damage and examined at 0 (A, B, E and F) and 5 (C, D, E′ and F′) days post-injury (dpi). A dorsal view of the
head is shown before (A) and after (B) skinning. (C) Dorsal view showing that regenerating skin has covered the wound at 5 dpi. Arrows in (A–C) point at the impact
site. The region delineated by a white square in (C) is shown at a higher magniﬁcation in (D). (E, E′) Calvariae were dissected together with their surrounding tissue
serving as an internal positive control for the presence of cartilage (Sc, skull cartilage), and double stained with Alizarin red and Alcian blue. (F, F′) Three-
dimensional reconstructions of dissected calvariae stained for bone mineral (Alizarin red, red) and cell bodies (Alexa Fluor 488-phalloidin, green). Note that the
hollow lesion (Hl) converts into an Alcian blue-negative regeneration plug (Rp) highly irrigated by blood vessels (Bv) in ﬁve days. The dotted circles in F and F´ show
the injured zone which contains remnants of “old” (non-regenerated) matrix (“o”, located between the continuous and dotted lines). Anterior is left in all panels.
Scale bars represent 1 mm in (A–E and E´) and 500 μm in (F and F′). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this ﬁgure legend, the reader is referred to the
web version of this article.)
D. Muñoz et al.
Mechanisms of Development 154 (2018) 153–161
154
reported in mammals and teleost ﬁsh (Jeradi and Hammerschmidt,
2016; Pautke et al., 2005). For this purpose, live tadpoles were given an
Alizarin red pulse, and, at a posterior developmental stage, a Calcein
green pulse. In this experimental setup, double positive bone regions
will mark the older mineralized matrix produced before the Alizarin red
pulse. By contrast, the bone matrix deposited after the Alizarin red
pulse is expected to be Alizarin red-negative and Calcein green-positive.
To ensure that this assay can detect subtle diﬀerences in ossiﬁcation
patterns, we applied Alizarin red at stage NF56+ (an intermediate
stage between NF56 and NF57) and Calcein green at stage NF57
(Fig. 2). Focusing on hind limbs, we found that the digit tips and distal
phalanges dramatically increase their mineralization pattern between
stages NF56+ and NF57 (Fig. 2A–D). This observation is consistent
with previous reports showing that limb ossiﬁcation progresses in a
proximo-distal fashion in this species (Espinoza et al., 2010). In the
calvaria, the suture also exhibited signs of progressive closure, as the
non-mineralized midline was clearly delineated by a thin region of
Alizarin red-negative and Calcein green-positive bone (Fig. 2E–H).
Hence, this approach was deemed suitable to evaluate ossiﬁcation
patterns during regeneration.
2.3. Bone growth visualization during calvaria regeneration
We next used the Alizarin red/Calcein green tracking strategy to
monitor bone regeneration at 1, 7 and 15 dpi (Fig. 3). Three observa-
tions are noteworthy. First, the circumference of the drilled region al-
ways exhibits a strong Alizarin red and Calcein green signal, which is
probably due to the presence of dislodged bone and broken bone
remnants in the periphery of the lesion (see also Figs. 4 and 5 for data
conﬁrming this view). Second, the midline and the external edge of the
developing calvaria also exhibit a relatively strong signal (see Fig. 3B,
C, J, K as well as Fig. 2F, G). We interpret this pattern as a higher
propensity of both dyes to label recently produced bone matrix (e.g.
osteoid) because the calvaria closes towards the midline and expands
on its edges (Espinoza et al., 2010). Third, some signal to noise ratio
variability was observed between samples (data not shown). We believe
that these three factors explain why the regenerating zone exhibits a
stronger signal intensity than the rest of the calvaria (Fig. 3B′–D′, F′–H′
and J′–L′). Nevertheless, we were able to clearly identify two distinct
types of mineralized regions within the regeneration zone: a double-
positive bone matrix (remnants of an older bone which had been de-
posited before the lesion, labelled “o” in Fig. 3A′–D′ and J′–L′), and an
Alizarin red-negative/Calcein green-positive mineralized matrix pro-
duced during the regenerative phase (labelled “n” in Fig. 3G′–H′ and
J′–L′). Hence, we show that bone regeneration is well advanced at 7 dpi
(Fig. 3E–H′) and that de novo ossiﬁcation has completely closed the
injury by 15 dpi (Fig. 3I–L′). This data conﬁrms that most of the bone
matrix covering the impact site at 7 and 15 dpi is produced during a
regenerative process, and is not an artifact resulting from incomplete
perforation.
2.4. Structural aspects of calvaria regeneration
To gain further structural insights into the process of bone re-
generation, transverse sections of 15 and 30 dpi calvariae were sub-
jected to second harmonic imaging analyses (Fig. 4A, B). Second-har-
monic generation (SHG) is particularly well suited to examine the
distribution of Type I collagen ﬁbrils, due to their highly crystalline
nature (Cox, 2011). At 15 and 30 dpi, the old bone, located outside of
the damaged region, is well-structured in series of parallel lamellae,
thereby exhibiting characteristics of lamellar bone (Fig. 4A, B). By
contrast, the 15 dpi regenerating mineralized matrix is composed of a
thin and irregular type of woven bone (Fig. 4A). At 30 dpi, however, the
regenerating bone matrix is regular, organized in lamellae, and re-
sembles the bone matrix formed during larval osteogenesis (Fig. 4B).
In addition, 30 dpi calvariae devoid of superﬁcial cells were ana-
lyzed by scanning electron microscopy (Fig. 4C–F). Irregular bone
matrix remnants resulting from the drilling procedure are present both
at the periphery of the lesion and within the regeneration zone
(Fig. 4D). Interestingly, the regenerated bone harbors circular cavities
that are not observed within the matrix formed during larval develop-
ment (Fig. 4E). Superﬁcial osteocytic lacunae are also visible in the
regenerated bone matrix (Fig. 4F).
2.5. Histology and gene expression patterns during calvaria regeneration
To grab a better understanding of the cellular and molecular un-
derpinning of Xenopus bone regeneration, 15 and 30 dpi calvariae were
subjected to histological and in situ hybridization analyses. At the per-
foration edge, damaged bone matrix fragments bend ventrally at right
Fig. 2. Bone growth can be monitored in Xenopus tropicalis tadpoles.

B, A′, B′). By contrast, the newly deposited bone matrix harbors os-

surface of both the old and new bone matrix (arrowheads in Fig. 5A,

region and are enclosed within lamellae of newly deposited bone
(Fig. 5A′, B′).
We then examined the expression of Dlx5 (coding for a transcription
factor playing a crucial role during osteogenesis, see Chung et al., 2010;
Hojo et al., 2016; Samee et al., 2009), of SPARC (coding for an evolu-
tionarily conserved collagen extracellular chaperone, see Bertrand
et al., 2013; Martinek et al., 2008; Martinek et al., 2007; Morrissey
et al., 2016) and of collagen 1a1 (coding for a major bone matrix
component, see Enault et al., 2015; Landis and Jacquet, 2013; Stock,
2015). Transcripts from all three genes are robustly detected in osteo-
blasts at 15 and 30 dpi (Fig. 5C–F and C′–F′).
3. Discussion
3.1. An evolutionarily conserved role for endothelia?
Studies in teleost ﬁsh, birds, and mammals have shown that en-
dothelia play a crucial role during embryonic osteogenesis, bone re-
generation, and extracellular matrix mineralization (Ben Shoham et al.,
2016; Blum and Begemann, 2015; Hu et al., 2017; Kerschnitzki et al.,
2016; Maes et al., 2010; Sivaraj and Adams, 2016). Potent angiogenic
signals, such as the vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF), are
produced by cells located at the regenerating site in mouse (Almubarak
et al., 2016; Athanasopoulos et al., 2007; Hu and Olsen, 2017). It is
tempting to propose that this situation is conserved in amphibians for
two reasons. First, as we show here, a thick and highly irrigated
Fig.

15 dpi the regenerated matrix is thin, dis-
organized and displays characteristics of
woven bone. By contrast, matrix located
outside of the damaged zone and within the
30 dpi regenerated bone exhibit collagen
ﬁbers that are tightly packed in parallel
layers characteristic of lamellar bone. (C–F)
A 30 dpi calvaria was examined by scanning
electron microscopy (anterior to the left).
The regions delineated by rectangles in (C)
and (D) are shown at higher magniﬁcation
in (D) and (E), respectively. (C) Whole cal-
varia. (D) Damaged site. Note the presence
of irregular bone remnants (o) resulting
from the drilling of the older matrix. (E)

D. Muñoz et al.
Mechanisms of Development 154 (2018) 153–161
157
mesenchymal plug composed of densely packed cells forms over the
damaged zone at 5 dpi (Fig. 1F′, Supplementary ﬁle 2). Second, as
amphibians belong to the osteichthyans (a monophyletic group of
vertebrates which also includes teleost ﬁsh, birds, and mammals), they
are likely to have inherited, by evolutionary descent, a bone repair
strategy involving endothelia. A detailed analysis of blood vessel for-
mation and function during developmental and regenerative osteo-
genesis will be required to solve this issue in Xenopus.
3.2. Origin of osteoblasts involved in regeneration
We have shown that the regenerating bone matrix is produced by a
population of osteoblasts expressing Col1a1, SPARC and Dlx5 at 15 and
30 dpi. Therefore, understanding the origin of such cells represents a
daunting challenge in the ﬁeld. Bone regeneration can occur through
intramembranous ossiﬁcation (in the mouse skull and tibia and in the
zebraﬁsh skull, see Geurtzen et al., 2014; Monfoulet et al., 2010;
Samsonraj et al., 2017), through endochondral ossiﬁcation (in mouse
unstabilized mid-shaft tibia fractures, see Hu et al., 2017), or through
the formation of an hybrid tissue between bone and cartilage named
chondroid bone (during zebraﬁsh jawbone regeneration, see Cervantes-
Diaz et al., 2017; Paul et al., 2016). Here, using whole-mount staining
procedures and histological analyses, we show that Xenopus tropicalis
frontoparietal bone repair occurs in the complete absence of chon-
drocytes or cartilage and therefore follows the intramembranous route.
Bone regeneration involves profound changes in cell behavior. For in-
stance, neighboring osteoblasts undergo a dediﬀerentiation process
during teleost ﬁsh bone regeneration (Geurtzen et al., 2014). Our
Fig. 5. Gene expression analysis during calvaria regeneration.
Calvariae were damaged at stage NF60 and were allowed to regenerate for 15 (A–F) and 30 (A′–F′) days. (A–A′) Hematoxylin eosin staining of histological sections
located at the transition zone between the “old” and “new” (regenerated) bone. Schematic drawing of (A) and (A′) are shown in (B) and (B′) respectively. Note the
presence of abundant mesenchyme irrigated by blood vessels (Bv). The newly formed bone (green in B, B′) contacts the matrix of the damaged calvaria (red in B and
B′) where empty osteocyte lacunae lie (arrows in A, A′). Osteoblasts cover the surface of both the old and new bone matrix (arrowheads in A and A′). At 30 dpi, an
irregular old bone matrix remnant (o in B′) is found embedded within the regenerated bone, and a cavity ﬁlled with mesenchyme can be observed within the
regenerating region (* in B′). In situ hybridization was performed using a control probe (C, C′), or probes speciﬁc to the Collagen 1a1 (D, D′), SPARC (E, E′) and Dlx5
(F, F′) genes, revealing strong expression in osteoblastic cells at both stages. Dorsal is up in all panels. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this ﬁgure
legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
D. Muñoz et al.
Mechanisms of Development 154 (2018) 153–161
158
results are consistent with this possibility, as we have shown that re-
generated bone grows from the periphery towards the center of the
lesion (Fig. 3C′, G′, K′), suggesting that a cell type present at the surface
of the older bone provides a source of osteoprogenitors in charge of
repairing the damaged region.
The role of Sox2-positive precursors must also be examined, as such
cells are involved in self-renewal and diﬀerentiation of the mouse os-
teoblast lineage (Seo et al., 2011), as well as in Xenopus spinal cord
regeneration which, in this species, only occurs before stage NF54
(Munoz et al., 2015). We indistinctively performed perforations on in-
dividuals whose calvaria was fully closed, ranging from Stage NF60 to
NF66, and failed to detect any diﬀerence in their regenerative capacity
(data not shown). Hence, without ruling out a possible role for Sox2-
positive cells, the “regenerative stages” and “non-regenerative stages”
discovered for Xenopus spinal cord regeneration (Lee-Liu et al., 2014)
do not seem to apply to the bone tissue.
3.3. Bone matrix deposition during bone regeneration
We found that wound closure occurs in 15 days, which is compar-
able to the regeneration of drilled zebraﬁsh calvaria and mouse tibia
(Geurtzen et al., 2014; Monfoulet et al., 2010). Both the mineralized
matrix of developing Xenopus tropicalis calvaria and of 30 dpi re-
generated bone consist of parallel layers of collagenous lamellae at
stage NF60, a conformation typically found in slowly deposited lamellar
bone (Currey, 2003). By contrast, we show that the 15 dpi repaired
region is composed of woven bone containing disorganized collagen
ﬁbers which fail to assemble in parallel lamellae, as has been reported
for the rapidly deposited matrix of regenerating mammalian bones
(Currey, 2003; Kitaguchi et al., 2018; Monfoulet et al., 2010).
We postulate that the drilling procedure locally kills osteocytic cells,
explaining the presence of empty lacunae present in old bone matrix
remnants (Fig. 5A, B, A′, B′). We also propose that regenerative and
developmental osteocytogenesis are not dramatically diﬀerent from
each other because (i) the regenerated bone is cellular and harbors
osteocyte-containing lacunae, (ii) osteocytic canaliculi are abundant at
the surface of the regenerated bone, and (iii) the dimensions of the
superﬁcial osteocytic lacunae correspond to the reported size of Xe-
nopus tropicalis osteocytes (Fritz et al., 2016).
Bone cavities ﬁlled with mesenchyme are visible in the regenerating
region at 30 dpi (asterisk in Figs. 4E and 5B′) and are not observed
outside of the damaged area. While the origin of such cavities is un-
clear, it will be interesting to investigate if bone remodeling plays an
important role during calvaria regeneration. In agreement with this
idea, it has been shown that osteoclasts are recruited to regenerating
ﬁns and scales in teleost ﬁsh (Geurtzen et al., 2017; Thamamongood
et al., 2012) and that human osteoblasts and osteoclasts promote bone
regeneration in vitro (Jeon et al., 2016).
Finally, it is intriguing to observe that regeneration follows a con-
stant pattern and occurs exclusively in the plane of the calvaria. Hence,
it will be important to determine the rules that govern the morpho-
genesis and shape of the newly formed bone. For instance, it is possible
that the soft mesenchymal regeneration plug becomes ﬂattened be-
tween the dermis and the brain, thereby driving the formation of a
discoidal regenerated bone matrix. The mechanical environment, im-
posed by the surrounding tissues, has a profound impact on animal
development (Munro and Odell, 2002; Salazar-Ciudad et al., 2003) and
might also apply to the calvaria regeneration phenomenon reported
here.
To the very best of our knowledge, this study reports the ﬁrst
traumatic skull injury recovery assay in amphibians, using Xenopus
tropicalis as a model species. Considering that (i) the post-operation
survival rate is very high (often 100%, data not shown), (ii) regenerated
bone covers the damaged area in only two weeks, and (iii) Xenopus
laevis and tropicalis are easily amenable to pharmaceutical and genetic
screens (Beck et al., 2009; Nakayama et al., 2013; Ogino and Ochi,
2009; Wheeler and Liu, 2012; Young et al., 2011), we believe that this
novel traumatic skull injury recovery assay will facilitate the identiﬁ-
cation and functional characterization of key molecules involved in
bone regeneration.
4. Experimental procedures
4.1. Bioethical considerations and traumatic skull injury
The Ethics Committee of the University of Concepcion (Concepcion,
Chile) approved all experimental procedures carried out during this
study, which were performed following the guidelines outlined in the
Biosafety and Bioethics Manual of the National Commission of
Scientiﬁc
and
Technological
Research
(CONICYT,
Chilean
Government). Xenopus tropicalis tadpoles were staged according to the
Nieuwkoop and Faber developmental table (Nieuwkoop and Faber,
1967)
and
anesthetized
with
Tricaine
(MS-222,
Sigma),
as
re-
commended (Close et al., 1996; Ramlochansingh et al., 2014). Trau-
matic skull injury was performed by holding an anesthetized tadpole
wrapped in a paper towel under a binocular dissecting microscope,
leaving its head exposed upward. Skull perforation was manually per-
formed using a 0.5 mm microdrill (Fine Science Tools), applied at right
angle from the head at the position shown in Fig. 1A. Operated tadpoles
were transferred to a 1 l aquarium where they recovered motility within
30 min and were raised until needed. Each experiment was performed
with at least 10 tadpoles (n ≥10), and representative results are
shown.
4.2. Whole mount staining procedures
Skulls were skinned and dissected using micro-scissors and forceps
(Fine Science Tools) as described in (Bertin et al., 2015), and ﬁxed in
4% PFA overnight at 4 °C. Bone and cartilage were respectively stained
with Alizarin red and Alcian blue, as described (Enault et al., 2015;
Espinoza et al., 2010). For nuclei and cytoskeletal actin visualization,
calvariae were respectively stained with Alexa Fluor 488-phalloidin
(495 nm excitation, 518 nm emission, Invitrogen) and Hoechst 33,258
(350 nm excitation, 460 nm emission, Sigma) as previously reported
(Fritz et al., 2016).
4.3. In vivo Alizarin red and Calcein green staining procedures
On the day of the bone labeling experiment, Alizarin red (or Calcein
green) was dissolved in 1X MMR buﬀer (Marc's Modiﬁed Ringer solu-
tion, 0.1 M NaCl; 1.8 mM KCl; 2 mM CaCl2; 1 mM MgCl2; 5 mM HEPES-
NaOH at pH 7.6) to obtain a 0.1% ﬁnal concentration. This solution was
diluted 10 times in small tanks containing 4 tadpoles freely swimming
in 90 ml of aquarium water. After a 2 h incubation time tadpoles were
transferred to a tank containing 1 l of aquarium water and raised until
needed. Alizarin red and Calcein green ﬂuorescent signals were ac-
quired on a MVX10 Macro Zoom Microscope (Olympus) using the
mCherry and GFP ﬁlters, respectively.
4.4. Fluorescence, two-photon and electronic microscopy analyses
Fluorescence and two-photon microscopy was performed at the
Center for Advanced Microscopy (CMA-BioBio) of the University of
Concepcion. To acquire multichannel ﬂuorescence and second har-
monic images, we used an LSM780 NLO confocal microscope (Axio
Observer.Z1; CarlZeiss, Oberkochen, Germany) equipped with the ZEN
2011 software v8.0 (Carl Zeiss). The confocal microscopy images were
acquired with 405 nm (Hoechst), 488 nm (Alexa Fluor 488-phalloidin)
and 561 nm (Alizarin red) excitation lasers and with an EC Plan-
Neoﬂuar 20× NA 0.5, WD 2.0 mm or a Plan-Apochromat 63× oil DIC,
NA 1.4, WD 0.19 mm objectives. To visualize endogenous collagen ﬁ-
bers and cytoskeletal actin a two-photon microscopy and an EC Plan-
D. Muñoz et al.
Mechanisms of Development 154 (2018) 153–161
159
Neoﬂuar 40×/1.30 oil objective was used, and images were acquired
with a pulsed IR laser (Chameleon; Coherent, Santa Clara, California,
USA) at 790 nm and a 388–427 nm spectral emission ﬁlter (second
harmonic generation [SHG]) or a 499–648 nm spectral emission (Alexa
Fluor 488-phalloidin). To obtain three-dimensional reconstructions and
the videos shown in the Supporting Information, we used the animation
tool of the Imaris software (Bitplane, Zurich, Switzerland).
Scanning electronic microscopy was performed at the “Centro de
Espectroscopía y Microscopía Electrónica” (CESMI) of the University of
Concepcion. All superﬁcial cells were eliminated from 30 dpi calvariae
using series of trypsin and collagenase treatments as previously re-
ported (Bertin et al., 2015; Fritz et al., 2016). Calvariae were then dried
at 37 °C for 24 h and image acquisition was performed with an ETEC
Autoscan microscope using standard procedures.
4.5. Histological analyses
Paraﬃn sectioning, Hematoxilin-Eosin staining, and in situ hy-
bridization were performed as described (Enault et al., 2015). For DIG-
labelled probe synthesis, cDNAs were ampliﬁed and blunt-cloned into
pBS using the following primers (5′ to 3′): Dlx5-Forward AAA AAA TGA
CAG GAG TCT ATG AGC G and Dlx5-Reverse CCG TAG AGA GTC CCT
GAT GCC A (874 bp), SPARC-Forward AGG TCC GAC TTC AGA ATG AG
and SPARC-Reverse TCT AAG TTA GCA CCT TTG CTG (1045 bp),
Col1a1-Forward GGC ACC CAT GGA TAT CGG AG and Col1a1-Reverse
CGC TGT CAA CTT TTG GGT CG (1197 bp).
Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://
doi.org/10.1016/j.mod.2018.06.007.
Author contributions
SM designed the experiments and acquired the scanning electron
microscopy data. HC performed the lesions and the whole mount and in
vivo staining procedures. DM performed the in situ hybridization and
the collagen second harmonic imaging. All authors analyzed the data.
SM and JPH wrote the manuscript.

===== Nguyen2023_DevDyn_Amphibian+CartilageMaturation =====
P A T T E R N S & P H E N O T Y P E S
Common features of cartilage maturation are not conserved
in an amphibian model
Jason K. B. Nguyen1
|
Patsy Gomez-Picos1
|
Yiwen Liu1
|
Katie Ovens2
|
B. Frank Eames1
1Department of Anatomy, Physiology, and
Pharmacology, University of
Saskatchewan, Saskatoon, Saskatchewan,
Canada
2Department of Computer Science,
University of Calgary, Calgary, Alberta,
Canada
Correspondence
B. Frank Eames, 2D01-107 Wiggins Road,
Saskatoon, Saskatchewan S7N 5E5,
Canada.
Email: b.frank@usask.ca
Funding information
Natural Sciences and Engineering
Research Council of Canada,
Grant/Award Numbers: 2019-05977,
435655-201
Abstract
Background: Mouse, chick, and zebrafish undergo a highly conserved program
of cartilage maturation during endochondral ossification (bone formation via a
cartilage template). Standard histological and molecular features of cartilage matu-
ration are chondrocyte hypertrophy, downregulation of the chondrogenic markers
Sox9 and Col2a1, and upregulation of Col10a1. We tested whether cartilage matu-
ration is conserved in an amphibian, the western clawed frog Xenopus tropicalis,
using in situ hybridization for standard markers and a novel laser-capture
microdissection RNAseq data set. We also functionally tested whether thyroid hor-
mone drives cartilage maturation in X tropicalis, as it does in other vertebrates.
Results: The developing frog humerus mostly followed the standard progres-
sion of cartilage maturation. Chondrocytes gradually became hypertrophic as
col2a1 and sox9 were eventually down-regulated, but col10a1 was not up-regu-
lated. However, the expression levels of several genes associated with the early
formation of cartilage, such as acan, sox5, and col9a2, remained highly
expressed even as humeral chondrocytes matured. Greater deviances were
observed in head cartilages, including the ceratohyal, which underwent hyper-
trophy within hours of becoming cartilaginous, maintained relatively high
levels of col2a1 and sox9, and lacked col10a1 expression. Interestingly, treating
frog larvae with thyroid hormone antagonists did not specifically reduce head
cartilage hypertrophy, resulting rather in a global developmental delay.
Conclusion: These data reveal that basic cartilage maturation features in the
head, and to a lesser extent in the limb, are not conserved in X tropicalis.
Future work revealing how frogs deviate from the standard cartilage matura-
tion program might shed light on both evolutionary and health studies.
K E Y W O R D S
cartilage maturation, RNA-seq, Xenopus tropicalis
1
|
INTRODUCTION
Although many cell types can be described as a contin-
uum, two general types of cartilage cell form during
endochondral ossification: mature and immature chon-
drocytes. Cartilage maturation is a term encompassing
cellular hypertrophy (an increase in cell size) and other
molecular features, so hypertrophic chondrocytes (and
Received: 4 November 2022
Revised: 4 March 2023
Accepted: 9 April 2023
DOI: 10.1002/dvdy.594
Developmental Dynamics. 2023;252:1375–1390.
wileyonlinelibrary.com/journal/dvdy
© 2023 American Association for Anatomy.
1375
their immediate precursors, prehypertrophic chondro-
cytes) can be referred to as mature chondrocytes. In the
diaphysis (or midsection) of a developing cartilage, pre-
hypertrophic and hypertrophic chondrocytes form under-
neath developing perichondral bone.1-3 Resting and
proliferative chondrocytes are typically found at the ends
of a developing cartilage undergoing endochondral ossifi-
cation and can be referred to as immature chondrocytes
to distinguish them from mature chondrocytes.4-9
Most skeletal development studies focus on chick and
mouse, in which cartilage maturation during endochon-
dral ossification is a gradual, progressive event. For
example, chondrocyte hypertrophy begins several days
after chondrocytes overtly differentiate.9-11 During matu-
ration, chondrocytes also change gene expression com-
pared to immature chondrocytes. Mature chondrocytes
progressively decrease expression of Sox9 and Col2a1,
and increase expression of Col10a1, from the levels
expressed in immature chondrocytes.7,12 Additionally,
extracellular proteoglycans appear to decrease in sulfa-
tion as chondrocytes mature.13-16 Subsequently, mature
chondrocytes
mineralize
their
extracellular
matrix
(ECM), undergo apoptosis, and then are subject to fur-
ther remodeling by invading, matrix-degrading endothe-
lial cells to form the primary ossification center.2,17
Histological and molecular features of cartilage matu-
ration during endochondral ossification appear to be
highly conserved among land tetrapods and bony fishes,
despite
variations
in
developmental
timing
among
clades.2,7,9 Similar to the chick and mouse, mature chon-
drocytes in both gar and zebrafish down-regulate sox9
and col2a1 and up-regulate col10a1, compared to imma-
ture chondrocyte expression levels.18,19 In gar, chondro-
cytes become hypertrophic gradually over several weeks,
which is much longer than chick or mouse.18 A few stud-
ies containing data on chondrocyte hypertrophy onset in
zebrafish suggest it occurs rather rapidly over the course
of a day or two.20-22
Limited data on molecular features of cartilage matu-
ration during endochondral ossification are available for
amphibians, and these focus exclusively on the limbs of
the model frog species Xenopus tropicalis. Endochondral
ossification might be generally delayed in amphibians
compared to other vertebrates, since less trabecular bone
and secondary ossification sites form.23-25 And while it is
unsurprising that different skeletal elements undergo
hypertrophy at different times (e.g., forelimb vs hin-
dlimb), no data are published for the frog ceratohyal, spe-
cifically. Despite this suggested difference in overall
developmental timing, the timing of hypertrophy in the X
tropicalis hindlimb is similar to mouse and chick, begin-
ning several days after cartilage differentiation initiates.26
sox9 and col2a1 even appear to be down-regulated in
mature cartilage of the X tropicalis hindlimb.26,27 In a
striking difference from studies of other vertebrates,
though, mature chondrocytes of the X tropicalis hindlimb
do not appear to express col10a1,28 which is “the” defini-
tive
marker
of
mature
cartilage
in
chick
and
mouse.2,7-9,29-31
Modern literature on cartilage maturation in the
amphibian head is sparse, but differences abound from
the standard process described above from frog studies
alone. Larval head cartilages, like the ceratohyal and
Meckel's cartilage, remain hypertrophic through later
stages of development in Xenopus laevis, and the cera-
tohyal persists after metamorphosis as the hyale cartilage,
which never ossifies and shows no evidence of minerali-
zation according to Alizarin red staining.32-35 Despite per-
sistent hypertrophy in X tropicalis head cartilages, sox9
and col2a1 appear to be expressed at later stages of
development.36
Since thyroid hormone, a promoter of cartilage matu-
ration, is active during amphibian development and
metamorphosis, it might drive cartilage maturation in
frog larval head cartilage. Thyroid hormones have two
forms: inactive thyroxine (T4) and biologically active tri-
iodothyronine (T3).37 T3 promotes chondrocyte matura-
tion in many tetrapod models, such as human, mouse,
and chick.38-43 Head cartilages in X tropicalis form by
Nieuwkoop-Faber stage 41 (NF41),44-46 before the larval
thyroid gland even begins to function and far before the
well-known role of thyroid hormones during metamor-
phosis after NF56.47-56 However, trace amounts of biolog-
ically relevant T3 is provided maternally in frog embryos,
since blocking of endogenous T3 signalling in early frog
development produces neuronal defects.52,57 Therefore,
maturation of larval head cartilage in X tropicalis might
also be under thyroid hormone control.
Here, we tested the hypothesis that the standard pro-
cess of cartilage maturation is conserved in the amphib-
ian X tropicalis. To test this hypothesis, histological and
molecular
features
of
cartilage
maturation
were
assessed in limb and head skeletons of developing X tro-
picalis, and early larvae were exposed to thyroid hor-
mone antagonists. Limb cartilage of the humerus
followed a relatively conserved maturation process, but
larval head cartilages, such as the ceratohyal, showed
dramatic differences in timing and gene expression
from the standard process of cartilage maturation. Thy-
roid hormone antagonists did not rescue rapid hypertro-
phy of larval head cartilages, but rather caused global
developmental delay. We finish by discussing the poten-
tial
evolutionary
and
health
significance
of
these
amphibian findings.
1376

RESULTS
2.1
|
Cartilage maturation in the X
tropicalis humerus mostly followed the
standard process
To determine whether histological and molecular fea-
tures of cartilage maturation were conserved in the X tro-
picalis humerus, section histology and expression assays
of stages NF55 and NF57 were compared. The NF staging
system describes the forelimb as being cartilaginous at
NF55, having signs of early perichondral ossification at
NF56, and exhibiting extensive perichondral ossification
by NF57.46 Our own observations of humerus develop-
ment generally matched this progression. Safranin O
staining of sulfated proteoglycans reflected their presence
in cartilage ECM at both timepoints (Figure 1A,G). Most
chondrocytes throughout the NF55 humerus appeared to
be comparable in size to epiphyseal chondrocytes of the
NF57 humerus (Figure 1A,G), suggesting that they were
immature chondrocytes. By contrast, chondrocytes in the
diaphysis of the NF57 humerus appeared hypertrophic,
suggesting mature cartilage in this region at this develop-
mental timepoint (Figure 1G). Quantitative measures of
the cross-sectional areas of individual cell lacunae from
different regions of the humerus at these stages supported
these qualitative observations on hypertrophy. Since lacu-
nae increase in size along with chondrocytes during
hypertrophy, and lacunar spaces are mostly taken up by
hypertrophic chondrocytes,35 hypertrophic lacunae corre-
late strongly with chondrocyte size even though tissue
processing
often
causes
chondrocyte
membranes
to
detach and shrink from lacunar borders.58 On average,
chondrocytes from the NF55 humerus (43 ± 17 μm2)
were indistinguishable in size from immature chondro-
cytes of the NF57 epiphysis (44 ± 15 μm2; Figure 1O).
On the other hand, both NF55 chondrocytes and NF57
epiphyseal chondrocytes were significantly smaller than
mature chondrocytes from the hypertrophic region of the
NF57 diaphysis (154 ± 66 μm2; Figure 1O). Given that
the developmental time from NF55 to NF57 for larvae
raised at 27C to 28C typically was 5 to 7 days, cells of
the X tropicalis humerus gradually became hypertrophic
over several days, similar to typical long bone develop-
ment in other vertebrates.1-3
RNA in situ hybridization revealed that changes in
gene expression from immature to mature cartilage in
the X tropicalis humerus were mostly similar to the stan-
dard maturation process, with one major difference. sox9
and col2a1 were expressed throughout immature carti-
lage of the NF55 humerus, and col10a1 was not expressed
at this timepoint (Figure 1B-D). It is unclear, however,
why staining also appeared in the cartilage ECM. Perhaps
it was due to exosomal transport59 or the substrate simply
diffusing there after reacting in the cytoplasm, but
expression can also be seen in cell remnants in the lacu-
nae. In mature cartilage of the NF57 humerus, sox9 and
col2a1 were down-regulated, but expression of the hyper-
trophic marker col10a1 remained absent (Figure 1H-J).
To ensure that the col10a1 result was not a false negative,
the same probe was used on NF59 teeth. As demonstrated
previously,60 odontoblasts showed col10a1 expression,
alongside some staining in dentin ECM (Figure 1L-N).
Upon closer inspection, regions of early hypertrophic
chondrocytes might have exhibited higher levels of sox9
and col2a1 expression, compared to immature chondro-
cytes (Figure 1H,I). Immunostaining showed that Col2
protein was present in cartilage throughout the entire
humerus at NF55 and NF57 (Figure 1E,F,K).
To provide an unbiased data set on gene expression
during cartilage maturation in the NF57 humerus, laser
capture microdissection was used to isolate immature
and mature chondrocytes (e.g., Figure 2A shows capture
of mature chondrocytes; Figure 2B lists captured areas),
and RNA sequencing was carried out (LCM-RNAseq). Of
11 standard immature chondrocyte (IMM) genes, only
two appeared to be down-regulated in mature chondro-
cytes (MAT) when average normalized counts were com-
pared: sox6 (305 vs 223) and col9a1 (7648 vs 6602), but
these
differences
were
not
statistically
significant
(Figure 2C). In fact, many standard immature cartilage
genes appeared to be up-regulated in MAT compared to
IMM. Transcript counts for sox9 (1330 vs 1159), col2a1
(107 103 vs 40 153), acan (18 515 vs 9340), sox5 (928 vs
795), and col9a2 (234 874 vs 145 037) were all higher in
MAT than IMM, although only col9a2 count differences
were significant, likely due to variations between samples
(Figure 2C). Many standard markers of mature cartilage
displayed higher counts in MAT vs IMM of the NF57 frog
humerus. For example, ihh (3788 vs 9), runx2 (2447 vs
394), mef2c (3208 vs 39), and ifitm5 (173 vs 1) were up-
regulated in MAT, although only runx2 results were sig-
nificant (Figure 2C). Supporting and expanding upon the
in situ analyses above, col10a1 (0 vs 3) and mmp13 (1 vs
0) showed negligible counts throughout cartilage. Gener-
ally, however, these data suggested that the process of
cartilage maturation is relatively conserved in the X tropi-
calis humerus.
Venn diagram analyses revealed there were 8833
genes expressed above threshold in frog cartilage cap-
tured from the humerus, of which 7589 were shared
between IMM and MAT (Figure 3A). Within this overlap,
gene ontology (GO) term analysis of biological processes
indicated at least 109 genes were involved with cartilage
formation (Figure 3B). Some non-overlapping (unique)
MAT genes were sp7, mgp, bmp6, and many muscle
genes: myog, myoz1, myh7, myl2, myf5, myf6, myom,
myot, mybpc1, mybpc2, and mybpc3.

2.2
|
The X tropicalis ceratohyal
underwent rapid hypertrophy and
remained cartilaginous
To understand cartilage maturation in the X tropicalis cer-
atohyal, a careful developmental series was undertaken.
Col2 immunostaining was used to indicate when overt
cartilage differentiation occurred. Background levels of
Col2 protein were detected in mesenchymal cells of the
hyoid arch at stage NF40 (Figure 4A). Specific Col2 immu-
noreactivity was observed in the ceratohyal by NF42, and
this staining appeared to increase by NF43 (Figure 4B,C).
FIGURE 1
The Xenopus tropicalis humerus mostly followed the standard process of cartilage maturation. (A,G) Compared to the NF55
humerus (A), Safranin O staining of the NF57 humerus (G) showed that mature chondrocytes (dashed black lines) in the diaphysis had
become overtly hypertrophic. (B,H) Compared to the NF55 humerus (B), sox9 expression was eventually down-regulated in mature
chondrocytes of the NF57 humerus (H, red arrow). (C,I) Similarly, col2a1 was down-regulated in mature chondrocytes of the NF57 humerus
(I, red arrow), compared to the NF55 humerus (C). (D,J) Expression of col10a1 was undetectable in the humerus at both NF55 (D,
translucent dashes outline the humerus) and even in hypertrophic chondrocytes at NF57 (J, dashed black lines). (E,F,K) Immunostaining
showed expression of Col2 protein in cross-sections of the epiphysis (E, red) and diaphysis (F, green) of the NF55 humerus and immature
and mature cartilage of the NF57 humerus (K). (L-N) Histological staining of an NF59 tooth with Safranin O (L) and Trichrome (M) helps to
identify col10a1 expression in odontoblasts and dentin ECM (N), which has properties similar to osteoid found in bone.103 (O) Cross-
sectional areas of chondrocytes from NF55 and NF57 humeri quantitating the increase in cell size (hypertrophy) from immature to mature
chondrocytes. Asterisks indicate significance at *P < 0.05, **P < 0.01, ***P < 0.001, and ****P < 0.0001 and error bars indicate a 95%
confidence interval. Abbreviations: ECM, extracellular matrix; N, total number of cells measured from three biological replicates; Tri,
Trichrome; Saf O, Safranin O. Scale bars = 100 μm for panels A to K; Scale bars = 50 μm for panels L to N
1378
NGUYEN ET AL.
 10970177, 2023, 11, Downloaded from https://anatomypubs.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/dvdy.594 by Ulsan National Institute Of, Wiley Online Library on [02/11/2023]. See the Terms and Conditions (https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/terms-and-conditions) on Wiley Online Library for rules of use; OA articles are governed by the applicable Creative Commons License
To confirm the timing of ceratohyal development and
to get a clearer picture of chondrocyte morphology, Safra-
nin O staining was performed. At NF39, only background
staining with Safranin O was observed in groups of mes-
enchymal cells of the hyoid arch (Figure 4D). By NF41,
mesenchymal condensation of the ceratohyal was visual-
ized, but increased extracellular Safranin O staining was
not observed until NF42, reflecting formation of imma-
ture chondrocytes (Figure 4E,F). Within the next few
hours at NF43, Safranin O staining revealed some hyper-
trophic chondrocytes in the ceratohyal, suggesting rapid
cartilage maturation (Figure 4G, arrowhead). To our
knowledge, this is the first published data delineating the
precise timing of ceratohyal hypertrophy in X tropicalis.
Indeed, before X tropicalis larvae reached NF44, most cer-
atohyal chondrocytes appeared hypertrophic (Figure 4H),
but no clear progression of hypertrophy, starting with
cells in the middle of the element towards the ends, was
observed
from
NF42
to
NF44.
At
later
stages,
hypertrophic chondrocytes in the ceratohyal continued to
increase gradually in size, agreeing with published results
in X laevis (Figure 4I-K).35 Similar results were observed
for other larval head cartilages, such as the palatoqua-
drate, basihyal, and Meckel's cartilage (data not shown).
After metamorphosis, the X tropicalis ceratohyal remains
cartilaginous, becoming known as the hyale and never
ossifying (Figure 4L), similar to X laevis.34
2.3
|
Cartilage maturation gene
expression was not conserved in the X
tropicalis ceratohyal
To determine whether molecular features of cartilage
maturation were conserved in the X tropicalis ceratohyal,
expression assays were performed. Col2 immunostaining
confirmed that chondrocytes of the ceratohyal began to
undergo hypertrophy during the quick transition from
FIGURE 2
Analysis of standard cartilage markers from laser captured cells of the frog humerus suggest many genes associated with
immature chondrocytes do not down-regulate as they mature. (A-C) Chondrocytes were laser captured from the frog humerus (A), targeting
immature (not shown) and mature cartilage separately and in triplicate (B), from which RNA was isolated and the resulting sequencing data
normalized to determine average normalized counts (and standard deviations) of major cartilage genes present in each cell type (C). Eleven
early chondrogenic markers and seven hypertrophic markers are listed, where counts below threshold are in parentheses. An independent
samples t-test was employed to determine any differences between counts of IMM and MAT. Asterisks indicate significance at *P < 0.05.
Abbreviations: IMM, immature chondrocytes; MAT, mature chondrocytes; n, sample size
NGUYEN ET AL.
1379
 10970177, 2023, 11, Downloaded from https://anatomypubs.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/dvdy.594 by Ulsan National Institute Of, Wiley Online Library on [02/11/2023]. See the Terms and Conditions (https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/terms-and-conditions) on Wiley Online Library for rules of use; OA articles are governed by the applicable Creative Commons License
NF42 to NF43, and remained hypertrophic at NF57
(Figure 5A-C). Col2 protein was expressed throughout
the entire ceratohyal before, during, and after hypertro-
phy. During the quick transition from immature to early
hypertrophic chondrocytes, cells of the ceratohyal did not
appear to down-regulate sox9 or col2a1, nor did they up-
regulate col10a1 (Figure 5D,E,G,H,J,K). Even by the late
hypertrophic stage of NF57, chondrocytes in the cera-
tohyal retained strong col2a1 expression and did not
express
col10a1,
although
sox9
levels
might
have
decreased from earlier stages (Figure 5F,I,L). Similar
results were seen in other larval head cartilages (data not
shown). These data demonstrated clearly that chondro-
cytes in the X tropicalis ceratohyal did not follow the
standard cartilage maturation process.
2.4
|
Blocking thyroid hormone
signalling in early larval stages of X
tropicalis produced a global delay in
development, not a specific inhibition of
cartilage hypertrophy
Given the known role of thyroid hormone in both cartilage
maturation and amphibian larval development,43,47,61-64
thyroid hormone antagonists were used to test whether
thyroid hormone causes the rapid hypertrophy of larval X
tropicalis head cartilages. A population of larvae at NF34
or NF35 were treated with the thyroid antagonists tetra-
bromobisphenol A (TBBPA) or NH-3,52,57,65-71 and Safra-
nin O staining of histological sections determined whether
hypertrophy of the ceratohyal was delayed. After 30 hours
of exposure to antagonists, many hypertrophic chondro-
cytes were observed in the ceratohyals of all experimental
groups (Figure 6A-C; NH-3 data not shown). However,
quantitative analyses showed that hypertrophy was signifi-
cantly decreased in TBBPA-treated larvae (89 ± 38 μm2),
compared
to
untreated
larvae
(119 ± 58 μm2)
after
30 hours of exposure (Figure 6J). Chondrocyte size was
similar in untreated and DMSO-treated larvae. After
26 hours of thyroid hormone antagonist exposure, the cer-
atohyals of TBBPA- and NH-3-treated larvae appeared to
be less hypertrophic than either the untreated or DMSO-
treated control larvae (Figure 6D-F; NH-3 data not shown).
After 7 hours of antagonist exposure, however, the devel-
oping ceratohyals of untreated or DMSO-treated control
larvae
had
undergone
mesenchymal
condensation,
whereas mesenchymal cells of TBBPA- and NH-3-treated
larvae did not appear to have undergone condensation
(Figure 6G-I; NH-3 data not shown). These data suggested
that rather than causing a specific cartilage maturation
phenotype, the thyroid hormone antagonists might be
causing global developmental delay.
To investigate global developmental delay of early X
tropicalis larvae exposed to thyroid hormone antagonists,
whole-mount larvae from each experimental group were
carefully staged at various timepoints of exposure. Gross
morphological comparisons from side, ventral, and dorsal
views at multiple timepoints suggested that TBBPA- or
NH-3-treated larvae had delayed development, compared
to untreated or DMSO-treated negative controls (only
dorsal views are shown in Figure 7A). This developmen-
tal delay seemed to appear relatively soon after antagonist
exposure, persisting throughout later timepoints. Quanti-
tative data on NF stages of each experimental group were
normalized to DMSO-treated controls, and thyroid hor-
mone antagonist-treated larvae began to show significant
developmental
delay
after
10 hours
of
treatment
(Figure 7B; Figure S1). While some significant differences
between untreated and DMSO-treated control larvae
were apparent at specific timepoints, there was no trend
to these differences, with development of untreated lar-
vae sometimes more or sometimes less advanced than
DMSO-treated larvae. Both TBBPA- and NH-3-treated
larvae were significantly delayed in development at all
exposure times after 5 hours (Figure 7B), but TBBPA
seemed to have a stronger effect than NH-3 since TBBPA-
treated larvae had a greater developmental delay after
25 hours and the differences also had lower P values.

between IMM and MAT. (A) A Venn diagram showing 8833 total
genes expressed above threshold in both IMM and MAT of an NF57
humerus of frog, where 7589 are shared between IMM and MAT.
(B) Of the 7589 overlapping genes, GO term analysis revealed at
least 109 are known to be involved in biological processes related to
cartilage formation. Abbreviations: GO, gene ontology; IMM,
immature chondrocytes; MAT, mature chondrocytes

DISCUSSION
Comparative embryology can be used to infer evolution-
ary changes to a homologous process among specific line-
ages. Common experimental model organisms might not
represent well all features of their specific clades, since
each animal might have responded to selective pressure
for traits that are not representative of their clade. How-
ever, similarities in developmental processes among vari-
ous model organisms are very useful in evolutionary
biology, since they might reflect conservation. Here, X
tropicalis was evaluated as a representative amphibian,
comparing results to those published on representative
fishes, birds, and mammals, in order to identify evolu-
tionary trends that are worthy of follow-up study.
While histological and molecular features of cartilage
maturation
seem
generally
conserved
among
many
fishes, birds, and mammals,3,8,9,18,19 cartilage maturation
in the amphibian X tropicalis showed many differences
from this conserved process. As reported previously for
the femur,28 for example, col10a1 expression was not
observed in hypertrophic chondrocytes during cartilage
maturation in both the ceratohyal and humerus of X tro-
picalis. Despite the widespread use of COL10 as a marker
of hypertrophic chondrocytes, the function of COL10 in
cartilage maturation is unknown. Col10a1 mutant mice
demonstrate that COL10 is not required for cartilage
hypertrophy and the general process of endochondral
ossification.72,73 Recent expression analyses of col10a1
orthologs in shark suggest an ancestral role for Col10 in
mineralization.60 If Col10 played a similar role in
amphibians, then the absence of col10a1 expression
might make sense if X tropicalis cartilages do not undergo
mineralization. Larval head cartilages do not appear to
undergo mineralization in X tropicalis or X laevis,34,74 but
similar studies are needed in the limb. Another explana-
tion for the lack of col10a1 expression in hypertrophic
chondrocytes of X tropicalis is that another col10a1 co-
ortholog with cartilage expression remains to be found in
this amphibian.
Apart from lacking col10a1 expression, limb cartilage
of X tropicalis showed a higher degree of conservation to
the standard cartilage maturation process than did head
cartilage. Specifically, the hypothesis that cartilages of
the amphibian undergo the standard maturation process
was mostly supported in the humerus, but not the cera-
tohyal. With the exception that col10a1 and mmp13 were
not expressed, cartilage maturation in the X tropicalis
FIGURE 4
Sub-staging early ceratohyal development revealed chondrocyte differentiation and hypertrophy. (A-C) Immunostaining
demonstrated that Col2 was not secreted by the ceratohyal condensation at NF40 (A), began to be detected in the NF42 ceratohyal (B), and
was abundant in the NF43 ceratohyal (C). Red arrowheads point to lateral edge of one ceratohyal. (D-K) Safranin O staining showed the
progression of ceratohyal development. Mesenchyme condensed from NF39 (D) to N41 (E), and strong Safranin O staining by NF42
(F) demonstrated overt chondrocyte differentiation. Chondrocyte hypertrophy was evident a few hours later in the NF43 ceratohyal (G, blue
arrowhead) and was widespread slightly later (NF43+, H). Chondrocytes in the ceratohyal increased hypertrophy (became larger) from
stages NF47 (I) to NF50 (J) to NF57 (K). (L) Alcian blue/Alizarin red whole-mount staining at NF66 after metamorphosis demonstrated that
the ceratohyal persists as the hyale and does not ossify. Abbreviations: Col2, collagen type 2; Hy, hyale; Mk, Meckel's cartilage; Saf O,

humerus was comparable to other vertebrate models,
where well-known markers of mature cartilage, such as
ihh, runx2, and mef2c, appeared to be up-regulated.75-78
ihh is generally considered to be a marker of prehyper-
trophic chondrocytes, but as transcription factors, both
runx2 and mef2c are critical drivers of cartilage matura-
tion throughout its early and late development, as well as
of bone formation. While our RNAseq data only showed
runx2 levels as being statistically significant between
mature and immature cartilage (Figure 2C), this was
likely due to sample variation given the even larger dif-
ferences in runx2 and mef2c expression. Another notable
exception was that sox9 and col2a1 did not immediately
down-regulate upon chondrocyte hypertrophy. In fact,
sox9 and col2a1 expression might even have undergone
upregulation in early hypertrophic chondrocytes, but
sox9 and col2a1 eventually down-regulated in more
advanced
hypertrophic
chondrocytes
as
cartilage

Gene expression during ceratohyal hypertrophy did not follow the standard process of cartilage maturation. (A-C) Col2
immunostaining demonstrated that ceratohyal chondrocytes had secreted cartilage matrix at NF42 (A), had begun hypertrophy at NF43 (B),
and remained hypertrophic at NF57 (C). (D-F) Levels of sox9 expression were similar as ceratohyal chondrocytes underwent hypertrophy
from NF42 (D) to NF43 (E), but might have down-regulated a bit by NF57 (F). (G-I) Levels of col2a1 expression appeared similar in
ceratohyal chondrocytes from NF42 (G) to NF43 (H) to NF57 (I). (J-L) The typical hypertrophic marker col10a1 was not expressed by
ceratohyal chondrocytes at NF42 (J), NF43 (K), or NF57 (L). Dashed red lines in all images outline the developing ceratohyal. Abbreviation:

NGUYEN ET AL.
 10970177, 2023, 11, Downloaded from https://anatomypubs.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/dvdy.594 by Ulsan National Institute Of, Wiley Online Library on [02/11/2023]. See the Terms and Conditions (https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/terms-and-conditions) on Wiley Online Library for rules of use; OA articles are governed by the applicable Creative Commons License
maturation progressed. In striking contrast, hypertrophic
chondrocytes of the ceratohyal maintained high levels of
sox9 and col2a1 expression, confirming observations from
a previous report.36 While it is unknown what causes
downregulation of these genes in hypertrophic chondro-
cytes, the collective sox9, col2a1, and col10a1 expression
data suggest that the molecular mechanisms of matura-
tion are different in larval head cartilages of the
metamorphosing frog.
Perhaps the most surprising observation was how rap-
idly the ceratohyal became hypertrophic in X tropicalis.
Compared to hypertrophy in the X tropicalis humerus,
which occurred over many days, the initial transition of
cells from newly-differentiated immature chondrocytes to
visibly hypertrophic chondrocytes occurred in only a few
hours. To further emphasize the rapid rate of this devel-
opment, even the relatively quick timing of hypertrophy
in the zebrafish ceratohyal takes about 1 to 2 days.20-22
Furthermore, hypertrophy appeared to initiate almost
simultaneously throughout the entire X tropicalis cera-
tohyal instead of the more conserved vertebrate process
of beginning in the mid-diaphysis and progressing
