bone (fig. S11). However, when compared with the Hgs-­deficient 
mice at P30 (Fig. 7, C and D), the loss of trabecular bone at P90 was 
alleviated. This is consistent with the notion that HC-­derived mpSSCs 
are the cellular source of postnatal bone formation and also indi-
cates that other SSC/BMSC populations are responsible for adult 
bone formation.
DISCUSSION
Here, we identified a previously unappreciated mpSSC population, 
which were derived from HCs and involved in trabecular bone for-
mation, establishing a previously unknown cellular paradigm for 
trabecular bone formation (Fig.  7E). A prominent character of 
mpSSCs is their exclusive cellular origin. Theoretically, marrow 
Fig. 7. Hgs deletion in HCs compromises trabecular bone formation. (A) Representative multicolor IF staining showing a significant decrease in HC-­derived mpSSCs, 
BMSCs, and OLCs in femurs of 1-­month-­old Col10a1EYFP;Hgsfl/fl mice. The framed areas are shown adjacently at higher magnifications. MP, metaphysis; BM, bone marrow. 
(B) Quantification of each cell compartment shown in (A). Data are presented as means ± SD per slide (n = 6). **P < 0.01 as compared with the corresponding cell compart-
ment in Col10a1EYFP;Hgs+l+ mice. (C) Representative reconstructed μCT images of femurs showing impaired trabecular bone formation in 1-­month-­old Col10a1-­Cre;Hgsfl/fl 
mice. (D) Quantification of metaphyseal parameters of femurs of 1-­month-­old Col10a1-­Cre;Hgsfl/fl and control mice. Data are presented as means ± SD (n = 6). Tb. BV/TV, 
trabecular bone surface area/bone volume; Tb. Th., trabecular thickness; Tb. Sp., trabecular spacing; Tb. Nb., trabecular number; Ct.Th., cortical thickness. **P < 0.01, N.S., 
no significance. (E) Scheme summarizing that HC-­derived mpSSCs contribute to trabecular bone formation. Scale bars, 500 μm.
Downloaded from https://www.science.org at Ulsan National Institute of Science and Technology on July 19, 2024
Yang et al., Sci. Adv. 10, eadl2238 (2024)     23 February 2024
S c i e n c e  A d va n c e s  | R e s e a r c h  A r t i c l e
11 of 15
SSCs are thought to be derived from perichondral cells that migrate 
into marrow or HCs that dedifferentiate at the perinatal stage (1, 2). 
Our multiple lines of genetic tracing results demonstrated that most, 
if not all, mpSSCs are the direct descendants of HCs, suggesting that 
the dedifferentiation of HC into mpSSC is a key process bridging 
chondrogenesis and osteogenesis during endochondral ossification. 
MpSSCs emerged at perinatal stage, peaked at the age of 1 month, 
and declined after the age of 3 months. The temporal and spatial 
pattern of mpSSCs corresponds to the active HC transition and 
robust trabecular bone formation in the postnatal stage. Thus, 
adopting a mpSSC fate not only endows HCs with differentiation 
potential but also enables HC descendants to survive in the long 
term and produce progeny that participate in postnatal bone forma-
tion. Consistently, the physiological function of mpSSC is critical for 
trabecular bone formation, as a reduced mpSSC pool caused by loss 
of Hgs led to remarkably compromised trabecular bone formation. 
It is worth noting that the defect in trabecular bone formation 
caused by Hgs deletion was gradually recovered, indicating that the 
effect of mpSSCs loss might be compensated by non-­HC–derived 
cells such as those originated from the perichondrial cells, another 
important cellular source of marrow stromal cells and osteoblasts 
(33, 40, 41), thus supporting the concept that cartilage and peri-
chondrial derived stromal cells functionally complement each other 
in trabecular osteogenesis (33). Furthermore, we revealed that HC-­
derived mpSSCs stand at the pinnacle of the HC dedifferentiation 
trajectory. Previous lineage tracing studies have shown that HCs can 
give rise to over half of all Lepr+ BMSCs as well as marrow-­associated 
SSPCs marked by Pdgfra and Lepr (31, 32). When comparing to the 
localization and transcriptional profiling of HC progeny defined 
here, both HC-­derived Lepr+ BMSCs and SSPCs are possibly down-
stream offspring of Pdgfrb+Kitl− mpSSCs.
MpSSCs serve as a reservoir for previously reported metaphyseal 
mesenchymal progenitors and diaphyseal BMSCs. In the metaphy-
sis region, Grem1+, Gli1+, Osterix+, and Pdgfra+Pdgfrb+Hey1+ 
label multiple types of metaphyseal mesenchymal progenitors that 
contribute to trabecular osteoblasts and bone marrow stromal cells 
(8, 18, 19, 34). In addition, the lower regions of the metaphysis and 
diaphyseal marrow harbor several overlapping bone marrow stro-
mal cell populations marked by Lepr, Kitl, and Cxcl12 (20, 22, 42). 
We found that mpSSCs expressed high levels of Pdgfrb and Hey1 
but little or very low levels of Kitl, Lepr, Grem1, Sp7/Osterix, and 
Gli1, whereas tBMSCs and RetiCs, two mpSSCs downstream popu-
lations, expressed Postn, Gli1, Sp7, Lepr, Cxcl12, Kitl, and Grem1 to 
varying extents. We propose that HC-­derived Pdgfrb+Kitl− mpSSCs 
represent a subpopulation of Hey1+Pdgfrb+ BMSCs and could give 
rise to Gli1+, Sp7+, Lepr+, Cxcl12+, and Grem1+ progenitor cells, 
therefore serving as a key cellular source for skeletal progenitors and 
bone marrow stromal cells. Notably, mpSSCs give rise to a fraction 
but not all of the aforementioned BMSCs populations as evidenced 
by scRNA-­seq, multiple IF staining, and CFU-­F results. This raised 
a possibility of whether other SSC populations with a non-­HC ori-
gin exist in the lower metaphyseal and diaphyseal bone marrow. On 
the other hand, other SSC populations may not be necessary, as the 
extraordinary cellular plasticity of BMSCs enable them to reacquire 
the ability of SSCs as shown in bone regeneration model of mouse or 
zebrafish (43, 44).
The nature of HC-­derived mpSSC provides insight into the hetero-
geneity and interactions of diverse previously reported SSCs at dif-
ferent sites. Previous studies have shown that epiphyseal growth 
plate houses the PTHrP+ SSCs that reside at resting zone and give 
rise to HCs, bone marrow stromal cells, and osteoblasts (5, 10). No-
tably, only a few offspring of PTHrP+ SSCs can extend into the 
metaphysis beyond the age of 2 months (5), whereas the active HC-­
to-­mpSSC transition mainly occurs before P30, suggesting that 
Sstr2+ mpSSC may not be the descendants of PTHrP+ SSCs. 
Together with the fact that the offspring of periosteal SSCs are con-
fined to periosteum, it seems that SSCs at distinct sites exert their 
distinct functions in a site-­specific manner. However, a recent study 
showed that periosteal SSCs control the function of the growth plate 
resting PTHrP+ SSCs by periosteal SSCs-­derived Indian hedgehog 
(45). Therefore, although there is still a lack of evidence for cellular 
cross-­talk between these SSCs, the functional interaction between 
mpSSCs and other previously identified SSCs need to be clarified in 
future studies. Nevertheless, together with murine SSCs identified 
in the growth plate, periosteum, and articular surface (4–6, 46), 
these findings support that multiple SSCs, which have distinct 
temporal and spatial contexts and origins as well as different func-
tional properties, work in concordance to achieve longitudinal bone 
development.
MATERIALS AND METHODS
Mice
The mouse lines used in this study were as follows: Col10a1-­Cre, 
KitlGFP, Ihh-­mKate2tomm20-­Dre, Lepr-­Cre, CAGG-­CreERT, Col2a1-­
CreERT, Acan-­CreERT2, Cre-­activated ROSA26LSL-­EYFP, ROSA26LSL-­nTnG, 
ROSA26LSL-­ZsGreen, ROSA26LSL-­tdTomato, Dre-­activated ROSA26RSR-­tdTomato, 
and Cre/Dre-­activated ROSA26Confetti2 (28, 36, 42, 47–53). KitlGFP mice 
indicated that a green fluorescent protein (GFP) cassette was introduced 
into the second exon of the Kitl locus. For renal capsule transplantation 
experiment, 6-­ to 8-­week-­old nonobese diabetic (NOD) severe 
combined immunodeficient (SCID) immunodeficient mice were 
used as recipient hosts.
We generated PdgfrbLSL-­Dre, Col10a1-­memG-­mitoS-­DD-­Cre-­DD, 
Sstr2-­mKate2-­2A-­CreERT2, and Sstr2-­2A-­tdKatushka2-­2A-­CreXER 
knock-­in mouse lines by CRISPR-­Cas9 mediated genome editing. A 
LoxP-­5 × PloyA-­LoxP-­Dre cassette was inserted after the transla-
tional start codon of the Pdgfrb gene. F0 adult mice were outcrossed 
to wild-­type mice, and F1 mice were genotyped to assess germline 
transmission of inserted fragments.
All mice were maintained in individual ventilated cages. All 
mouse experiments were performed according to the protocols ap-
proved by the Animal Experiment Committee of the Beijing Insti-
tute of Lifeomics and conformed to the Regulations of People’s 
Republic of China on the Administration of Experimental Animals.
Cell preparation for scRNA-­seq
P7.5 Col10a1ZsGreen mice were used for single-­cell analysis. The 
femurs and tibias from 12 mice were dissected and cleaned by brief 
digestion and brushing in 0.2% collagenase type I (Gibco, 17100017) 
at 37°C for 30 min. To exclude cell interference from the secondary 
ossification center and other sites of the growth plate, we only col-
lected the distal part of the femur and proximal part of the tibia, 
with half of the growth plate cartilage excised (fig. S1). To help 
release the embedded cells, cortical bone, trabecular bone, and 
hypertrophic cartilage were stripped and sliced into pieces using a 
syringe needle under a stereoscope. The samples were then digested 
in 0.2% collagenase type I and 0.1% collagenase type II (Gibco, 
Downloaded from https://www.science.org at Ulsan National Institute of Science and Technology on July 19, 2024
Yang et al., Sci. Adv. 10, eadl2238 (2024)     23 February 2024
S c i e n c e  A d va n c e s  | R e s e a r c h  A r t i c l e
12 of 15
17100015) for 2 to 3 hours at 37°C under gentle agitation. The liber-
ated cells were pipetted, filtered, neutralized, and centrifuged every 
20 min. Dead cells were removed using a kit (130090101, STEM-
CELL Technologies). The collected cells were prepared to establish 
two types of single-­cell transcriptomes. The first was the unsorted 
scRNA-­seq data, which were constitutive from skeletal cells of the 
whole bone with hematopoietic and endothelial cells briefly de-
pleted by CD31/CD45/Ter119-­coated magnetic beads (Biotin anti-­
mouse CD31 Antibody, BioLegend, 102503; Biotin anti-­mouse 
CD45 Antibody, Biolegend, 103104; Biotin anti-­mouse TER-­119 
Antibody, Biolegend, 116203; EasySep Mouse Streptavidin Rapid-
Spheres Isolation Kit, STEMCELL Technologies, 19860). The other 
was composed of ZsGreen-­positive cells sorted by FACSAria III (BD 
Bioscience). Single cells were encapsulated in emulsion droplets us-
ing a chromium controller (10x Genomics). The scRNA-­seq librar-
ies were prepared using Chromium Single Cell 3′ Reagent Kits v3 
(10x Genomics), according to the manufacturer’s protocol. The 
scRNA-­seq libraries were evaluated and quantified using an Agilent 
2100 Bioanalyzer/Fragment Analyzer 5300 and Qubit HS. The 
libraries were sequenced on a NovaSeq platform (Illumina) to gen-
erate 150-­bp paired-­end reads, according to the manufacturer’s 
instructions. Two datasets of unsorted scRNA-­seq and one dataset 
of ZsGreen-­sorted scRNA-­seq were established. The median genes 
per cell were 1534, 1601, and 4304, and the median unique molecu-
lar identifier (UMI) counts per cell were 5102, 5528, and 20512, 
respectively.
Processing and analysis of scRNA-­seq data
Raw data based on 10x Genomics were processed using the Cell 
Ranger software suite (v3.1.0) with reference genome mm10, and 
doublets were removed using Scrublet (v0.2.1) (54). Hematopoietic 
and endothelial cells were removed from ZsGreen-­sorted and un-
sorted scRNA-­seq data. Cells with gene number more than 700 (for 
the unsorted scRNA-­seq data) or 2000 (for the ZsGreen-­sorted 
scRNA-­seq data), as well as with a percentage of mitochondrial 
genes lower than 15%, were kept and projected into two dimensions 
by UMAP and clustered by K-­means algorithm using Scanpy (55) 
(v1.7.2) in the Python environment (v3.9). ZsGreen-­expressing cells 
(UMI > 0) were extracted from the unsorted scRNA-­seq data and 
integrated with ZsGreen-­positive cells sorted by FACS. The batch 
effect was removed using the cellranger aggr function. Cells were 
projected into two dimensions by UMAP using a cellranger and 
clustered by the Leiden algorithm (resolution = 0.3) using Scanpy 
(55) (v1.7.2) in the Python environment (v3.9). Slingshot trajectory 
inference was based on UMAP reduction dimensions. The Wilcoxon 
rank-­sum test was performed to identify differentially expressed 
genes in each cell cluster, and the P values were adjusted using the 
Benjamini-­Hochberg correction. Genes with an adjusted P value 
<0.05, log (fold change) >0.5, and expressed in more than 30% of 
the cells in the cluster were selected for GO and KEGG enrichment 
analyses using the R package ClusterProfiler (v3.14.3) (56). Last, sig-
naling entropy was computed using the R package SCENT (v1.0.2) 
to infer differentiation potency at the single-­cell level (38).
Micro-­CT analysis
Femurs were freshly dissected, fixed in 4% paraformaldehyde (PFA), 
scanned, and analyzed using a micro-­CT Skyscan 1076 μCT scanner 
(Bruker Corporation) and software. The scanner was set at a voltage 
of 80 kV, a current of 500 mA, and a resolution of 9.2 μm per pixel. 
A 200-­μm-­thick slice of the femur was reconstructed through its 
longitudinal axis. Trabecular bone was reconstructed from 0.5 to 
2.3 mm below the lowest part of the metaphyseal growth plate and 
then measured for the metaphyseal parameters including BMD, tra-
becular bone surface area/bone volume (Tb. BV/TV), trabecular 
thickness (Tb. Th.), trabecular spacing (Tb. Sp.), and trabecular 
number (Tb. Nb.). The cortical bone was reconstructed from 2 to 
2.5 mm below the lowest part of the metaphyseal growth plate of the 
femur and measured for its thickness (Ct. Th.).
Linage tracing performed on inducible mouse models
To evaluate the lineage relationships between growth plate chondro-
cytes and mpSSCs, we used inducible Col2a1-­CreERT;ROSA26LSL-­nTnG 
and Acan-­CreERT2;ROSA26LSL-­nTnG mouse models. To trace the 
fate of an individual growth plate chondrocyte, a single low dose of 
tamoxifen (40 μg/g body weight) was administrated at P4. Six days 
after tamoxifen administration, the hindlimb bones of Col2a1-­
CreERT;ROSA26LSL-­nTnG mouse mice were harvested. To quantify the 
transition of HCs at different developmental stages, we triggered 
nGFP expression in a vast majority of growth plate chondrocytes of 
Acan-­CreERT2;ROSA26LSL-­nTnG mice by two successive high doses of 
tamoxifen (200 μg/g body) at P7 and P30, respectively. Four or 
10 days after tamoxifen administration, the hindlimb bones of 
Acan-­CreERT2;ROSA26LSL-­nTnG mice were dissected and sectioned for 
immunohistochemical studies.
To test the deletion efficiency of Cre recombinase upon the Loxp-­
STOP-­Loxp cassette within PdgfrbLSL-­Dre locus, two successive high 
doses of tamoxifen (200 μg/g body) were administrated at P8 and P9 
in CAGG-­CreERT;PdgfrbLSL-­Dre;ROSA26RSR-­tdTomato mice, in which 
the Cre activity would be induced in widespread cells or tissues. 
Three days after tamoxifen administration, the skeletal elements of 
CAGG-­CreERT;PdgfrbLSL-­Dre;ROSA26RSR-­tdTomato mice were dissected 
and sectioned for immunohistochemical studies. The deletion effi-
ciency could be calculated on the basis of the percentage of tdTo-
mato+ cells in Pdgfrb+ compartment.
To trace the fate of HC-­derived Sstr2+ mpSSCs in Ihh-­Dre;Sstr2-­
CreERT2;ROSA26Confetti2 mice, a more active form of tamoxifen, 
4-­hydroxytamoxifen, was used at 100 μg/g body to overcome the 
low induction efficiency of Sstr2-­CreERT2 transgene. A single dose 
of 4-­hydroxytamoxifen was injected at P2, and the skeletal elements 
of Ihh-­Dre;Sstr2-­CreERT2;ROSA26Confetti2 mice were harvested at P4 
and P16.
Renal capsule transplantation of hypertrophic cartilage
The rib cages dissected from P1 mice were digested with 0.2% col-
lagenase type I at 37°C for 30 min. The connective tissues, including 
the muscle, fascia, and periosteum, were thoroughly removed by 
repeated brushing and digestion. Hypertrophic cartilage was then 
excised from the T6–T8 ribs with a fine blade under a fluorescent 
stereoscope and transferred underneath the renal capsules of 6-­ to 
8-­week-­old NOD SCID immunodeficient mice.
Marrow stromal cell isolation, successional clonal formation, 
and multilineage differentiation
Bone marrow cells from tibias and femurs of P30 Col10a1-­Cre;PdgfrbLSL-­Dre; 
ROSA26LSL-­EYFP/RSR-­tdTomato mice were repeatedly flushed out using a 
1-­ml syringe. To help release the stromal cells residing between the 
metaphysical trabeculae, the growth plate cartilage was peeled off, 
and the metaphysis part of bones was cut off. They were gently disrupted 
Downloaded from https://www.science.org at Ulsan National Institute of Science and Technology on July 19, 2024
Yang et al., Sci. Adv. 10, eadl2238 (2024)     23 February 2024
S c i e n c e  A d va n c e s  | R e s e a r c h  A r t i c l e
13 of 15
using a mortar and pestle and digested in 0.2% collagenase type I for 
2 to 4 hours. The liberated primary bone marrow and stromal cells 
were filtered, collected, isolated, and plated at a density of 2 × 105 
cells/10 cm dish to allow the formation of individual colonies after 
growth in Dulbecco’s Modified Eagle Medium (Gibco, 11885084) 
containing 12% fetal bovine serum (Gibco, 12664025) and 1% antibiotic-­
antimycotic (Gibco, 15240-­062) at 37°C under a hypoxic condition 
(5% O2, 5% CO2) for 2 weeks. Single clones with ≥600 cells 
(Ø  ≥  4 mm) were selected and labeled as first clones. They were 
equally divided into two parts: One was replated in a 10-­cm dish to 
allow the formation of second individual colonies, and the other 
part was expanded in six-­well plates to reach the cell population 
(≥30,000 cells) for further multilineage differentiation potential test 
of the first clones. We generated third individual colonies (≥400 
cells/Ø ≥ 3 mm) from second colonies (≥400 cells/Ø ≥ 3 mm) via 
the same replating protocol. For in vitro multilineage differentiation 
potential test, each expanded first and third clone was split for dif-
ferentiation into chondrocytes, osteoblasts, and adipocytes using a 
differentiation kit (STEMCELL Technologies, 05507, 05455, and 
05504). The in vitro differentiation reported in this study is clonal. 
Images were taken using a Nikon ECLIPSE Ti-­U inverted micro-
scope system.
Cell isolation, FACS, and renal capsule transplantation
Limb bones were dissected from mice at the indicated age. They were 
briefly digested with 0.2% collagenase type I at 37°C for 30 min. After 
the removal of the muscle and ligament, the tibiae and femurs were 
dissociated by mechanical and enzymatic dissociation according to 
a published protocol (35). Flow cytometry was performed using 
FACSAria III. The total dissociated cells were blocked with rat immuno-
globulin G and stained with fluorochrome-­conjugated antibodies 
against CD45 (BioLegend, 103154, 1:400), CD31 (BioLegend, 102528, 
1:400), Ter119 (BioLegend, 116223, 1:200), and CD140b (Pdgfrb, Bio-
Legend, 136010, 1:400) for fractionation by FACS. Dead cells were 
excluded using 7-­Aminoactinomycin D (7-­AAD) staining (54). The 
300 to 500 tdTomato+Pdgfrb+Kitl− mpSSCs could be enriched from 
the fore/hindlimb bones and scapulae per P10 to P20 CPT;KitlGFP 
mice. Sorted cells (6000 to 10,000) were pelleted, resuspended in 2 to 
3 μl of Matrigel (Corning, 356234), and then injected underneath the 
renal capsules of 6-­ to 8-­week-­old NOD SCID immunodeficient mice. 
After 4 to 6 weeks, the recipient kidneys were dissected for histologi-
cal and immunohistochemical analysis.
Bone injury and transplantation
To transplant isolated cells to the bone injury site, 6-­ to 8-­week-­old 
NOD SCID immunodeficient mice were used as recipient hosts. 
First, a 26-­gauge syringe needle was placed parallel to the tissue be-
tween the cortical bone and periosteum/fascia, making a hole at the 
metaphyseal medial tibial shaft 1 mm beneath the growth plate, 
which was covered by the periosteum/fascia. A second Ø 0.5-­mm 
hole passing through the cortical bone and periosteum/fascia was 
then made by drilling at the diaphyseal medial tibial shaft, 3 mm 
beneath the growth plate. Using a 31-­gauge insulin pen needle and a 
microinjector, 6000 to 10,000 sorted cells resuspended in 2 to 4 μl of 
Matrigel were injected into the bone marrow through the metaphy-
seal hole. After 10 to 14 days, recipient tibiae were dissected for flow 
cytometry or immunohistochemical studies. Before flow cytometry, 
nonskeletal cells were briefly depleted by CD31/CD45/Ter119-­coated 
magnetic beads. To identify the cell type within the callus, six 6-­μm 
serial sections with an interval of 60 μm were cut from the recipient 
tibia and were subject to multicolor IF staining. The number of 
each cell compartment was counted from these six representa-
tive sections.
Multiplex IF assay
Samples of the skeletal elements and kidney were dissected, fixed in 
4% PFA, and decalcified in 0.5 M EDTA/phosphate-­buffered saline 
(PBS)/0.1% PFA. Paraffin-­embedded samples were cut into 4-­ to 
6-­μm sections, followed by deparaffinization and heat-­mediated 
antigen retrieval treatment (Histova Biotechnology, BoneRetrival-
­M, BRM5L, 72°C for 6 hours). Multiplex IF assay was conducted as 
previously described (57, 58). Briefly, sections were incubated with 
primary antibody for 2 hours, followed by detection using HRP-­
conjugated secondary antibody and Tyramide Signal Amplifica-
tion (TSA)-­fluorophores (Histova Biotechnology, NECC7100). 
Then, the primary and secondary antibodies were eliminated by 
heating the slides in retrieval/elution buffer (Histova Biotechnology, 
ABCFR5L) for 10 s at 95°C using microwaves or by incubating slides 
in elution buffer (Histova Biotechnology, ABCCC30) for 20 to 
30 min at 37°C. Serially, each antigen was labeled with distinct anti-
bodies and TSA fluorophores. The cryosections of Ihh-­Dre;Sstr2-­
CreERT2;ROSA26Confetti2 bones were cut at 60 μm, treated with 0.25% 
Triton X-­100/PBS for 20 min, and then subject to antibody incuba-
tion and IF detection. Multiplex antibody panels applied in this 
study include the following: GFP (CST, 2956, 1:400), red fluorescent 
protein (Rockland, 600-­401-­379, 1:800), Pdgfrb (CST, 3169, 1:400), 
Emcn (Thermo Fisher Scientific, 14-­5851-­82, 1:800), Sstr2 (Abcam, 
ab134152, 1:800), N-­cadherin (Abcam, ab76011, 1:500), Pdpn 
(Sino Biological, 50256-­R066, 1:1000), CD200 (Novusbio, BAF3355, 
1:400), CD51 (Abcam, ab208012, 1:400), Ly51 (Sino Biological, 
50082-­T24, 1:1000), Thy1 (Sino Biological, 50461-­T44, 1:1000), 
CD105 (Abcam, ab221675, 1:400), CD31 (CST, 77699, 1:400), CD45 
(CST, 70257, 1:400), Ter119 (BioLegend, 116201, 1:1000), Ki67 
(CST, 12202, 1:400), Lepr (Novusbio, BAF497, 1:200), Hgs (Abcam, 
ab155539, 1:300), and Sox9 (Abcam, ab185966, 1:400). Multiplex IF 
slides were imaged using a confocal laser scanning microscopy 
platform Zeiss LSM880 equipped with 405-­nm/458-­nm/488-­nm/
514-­nm/543-­nm/594-­nm/633-­nm lasers. Some data were further 
processed and statistically analyzed using the Bitplane Imaris soft-
ware (Bitplane AG) or the HALO image analysis platform (Indica 
Labs). In situ mapping and quantification were performed on Imaris 
software (Bitplane AG) or the HALO image analysis platform. Seg-
mented cell compartments were visualized by colored dots and were 
superimposed on tissue section.
Histological analysis
Decalcified or undecalcified samples were embedded in paraffin, cut 
into 5-­μm sections, and subjected to safranin O and Fast Green 
staining or von Kossa staining according to the standard protocol.
Statistical analysis
Data are expressed as means  ±  SD, and significance was set at 
P < 0.05 (see each figure for details). The sample sizes are shown in 
the corresponding results and figure legends. Paired or unpaired 
two-­tailed Student’s t test was used for two-­group comparisons. 
One-­way analysis of variance (ANOVA) followed by Tukey’s post 
hoc test was performed for multiple comparisons. All statistical 
analyses were performed using Microsoft Excel.

===== Zhou2024_ACSNano_ChondrocyteRejuvenation+CollagenIIScaffold =====
Rejuvenating Hyaline Cartilaginous
Phenotype of Dedifferentiated Chondrocytes
in Collagen II Scaffolds: A Mechanism Study
Using Chondrocyte Membrane
Nanoaggregates as Antagonists
Huiqun Zhou, Yulei Mu, Cheng Ma, Zhen Zhang, Chao Tao, and Dong-An Wang*
Cite This: ACS Nano 2024, 18, 2077−2090
Read Online
ACCESS
Metrics & More
Article Recommendations
*
sı
Supporting Information
ABSTRACT: Joint cartilage lesions affect the global population in
the current aging society. Maintenance and rejuvenation of
articular cartilage with hyaline phenotype remains a challenge as
the underlying mechanism has not been completely understood.
Here, we have designed and performed a mechanism study using
scaffolds made of type II collagen (Col2) as the 3D cell cultural
platforms, on some of which nanoaggregates comprising extracts
of chondrocyte membrane (CCM) were coated as the antagonist
of Col2. Dedifferentiated chondrocytes were, respectively, seeded
into these Col2 based scaffolds with (antCol2S) or without
(Col2S) CCM coating. After 6 weeks, in Col2S, the chondrocytes
were rejuvenated to regain hyaline phenotype, whereas this
redifferentiation effect was attenuated in antCol2S. Transcrip-
tomic and proteomic profiling indicated that the Wnt/β-catenin
signaling pathway, which is an opponent to maintenance of the hyaline cartilaginous phenotype, was inhibited in Col2S, but it
was contrarily upregulated in antCol2S due to the antagonism and shielding against Col2 by the CCM coating. Specifically, in
antCol2S, since the coated CCM nanoaggregates contain the same components as those present on the surface of the seeded
chondrocytes, the corresponding ligand sites on Col2 had been preoccupied and saturated by CCM coating before exposure to
the seeded cells. The results indicated that the ligation between Col2 ligands and integrin α5 receptors on the surface of the
seeded chondrocytes in antCol2S was antagonized by the CCM coating, which facilitates the Wnt/β-catenin signaling toward
the loss of hyaline cartilaginous phenotype. This finding reveals the contribution of Col2 for maintenance and rejuvenation of
the hyaline cartilaginous phenotype in chondrocytes.
KEYWORDS: nanoaggregates, cell membrane, type II collagen, chondrocytes phenotype, Wnt/β-catenin signaling, integrin α5
1. INTRODUCTION
Articular cartilage facilitates frictionless movement and load
transmission. As the only cell type in articular cartilage,
chondrocytes are enclosed in dense extracellular matrices
(ECMs) rich in type II collagen (Col2) and negatively charged
proteoglycans.1 Due to hypocellularity and avascularity,
articular cartilage displays a restricted capacity for repair.2
Therefore, cartilage defects often fail to be restored and
ultimately contribute to osteoarthritis (OA). OA is typically
characterized by progressive cartilage degradation with
chondrocyte dedifferentiation, affecting 5% of people world-
wide.3
To address this issue, many efforts have been devoted to
repair damaged cartilage. Due to its native nature, Col2 matrix
is generally considered as a promising environment for
cartilage regeneration.4 Col2 matrix has been found to enhance
chondrocyte proliferation.5 Additionally, chondrocytes re-
Received:
September 19, 2023
Revised:
January 2, 2024
Accepted:
January 5, 2024
Published: January 9, 2024

See https://pubs.acs.org/sharingguidelines for options on how to legitimately share published articles.
gained chondrogenic phenotype by three-dimensional (3D)
cell culturing in Col2 matrices.6 Col2 gel embedded with cells
enabled the effective regeneration of rabbit cartilage lesions in
vivo.7 Recently, a decellularized hyaline cartilage graft
composed of Col2 has been developed for cartilage repair,
which was reported to regenerate hyaline-like cartilaginous
neo-tissue after 6 months of implantation in porcine knee
joints.8 Although extensive studies have focused on cartilage
repair of the Col2 matrix, the underlying mechanism remains
largely unknown.
The phenotypic shift of chondrocytes is recognized as a
critical mechanism that leads to the disturbance of cartilage
homeostasis.9 In general, chondrocytes are cultivated in
monolayer whereby the proliferating chondrocytes alter
morphologies and lose their functional phenotype in a process
identified as dedifferentiation.10 The irreversible phenotype
Figure 1. Fabrication and characterization of antCol2S. (A) Schematic overview of derivation of Col2S and antCol2S. Chs, chondrocytes.
(B) DLS result of CCM vesicles. (C) The water contact angle of the Si surface with and without the CCM coating (n = 5 replicates per
group). (D) The water contact angle of the coverslip with and without CCM coating (n = 5 replicates per group). (E) Weight percentage of
protein content on Col2S, antCol2S, and antCol2S immersed in 1× PBS at 4 °C for 1 day (n = 3 replicates per group). (F) Heatmap of
typical chondrocyte-specific proteins between Col2S and antCol2S, expressed as Z-score. (G) SEM image (top) and fluorescent image
(bottom) of Col2S and antCol2S (Scar bar: 2 and 100 μm). All data were mean ± SEM.
ACS Nano
www.acsnano.org
Article
https://doi.org/10.1021/acsnano.3c09033
ACS Nano 2024, 18, 2077−2090
2078
transformation usually occurs after passage 4.11 A biphasic
phenotype shift can be observed with a loss of Col2 and an
increased synthesis in collagen type I, leading to an ECM with
mechanically inferior properties and thereby nondurable
fibrocartilage formation in vivo.12 Besides, chondrocyte
dedifferentiation has a close link with OA disease. Many
markers of chondrocyte dedifferentiation were found to be
upregulated and participate in fibrosis of the OA process.11
Thus, chondrocyte dedifferentiation can be exploited as an
effective model to study the mechanisms underlying cartilage
restoration.
Owing to surface shielding and functional ligand incorpo-
ration, cell membrane coating has been applied in diverse
scaffolds. Cell membranes can form vesicles and be coated on
the surface of materials to act as a physical and biological
barrier to hinder the interaction between seeded cells and
scaffold. For example, a red cell membrane coated ECM graft
was developed to protect decellularized ECM from host
immune recognition in vivo.13 Apart from ECM-based
scaffolds, the cell membrane is also further applied on
electrospun scaffolds14 and nanomotors.15 Importantly,
proteins on the cell membrane surface endow the materials
with additional biological functions,16 including an antagonism
effect. Membrane nanoparticles prepared from human
embryonic kidney-239T cells were discovered to efficiently
block SARS-CoV-2 infection.17 As a CXCR4 antagonism, the
bone marrow stromal cell membrane has demonstrated the
ability to inhibit homing of leukemia cells to the bone
marrow.18
Numerous mechanistic studies have artificially manipulated
expression levels of receptors at the cell surface to investigate
receptor function in the cell−scaffold interaction, such as
receptor blocking. However, it may affect a broad-spectrum of
cell activities including cell survival,19,20 carrying potential risk
of unexpected cell death. In this study, we focused on precisely
regulating the cell−scaffold interaction from the perspective of
the scaffold rather than the cell. The advancement of cell
membrane coating in antagonism and shielding inspired us to
develop nanoaggregates as an anti-Col2 strategy for the
scaffold in the mechanism study. Chondrocytes reside in an
environment composed primarily of Col2, which enables the
chondrocyte membrane (CCM) to possess a large number of
receptors against Col2.21 CCM-coated scaffold inherits the
surface property of the source chondrocytes and thus presents
antagonism and shielding against Col2. Here, we designed a
mechanism study by exploiting CCM nanoaggregates as the
antagonist of Col2. Dedifferentiated chondrocytes (passage 5,
P5) were chosen as model cells and respectively seeded into
Col2-based scaffolds with (antCol2S) or without (Col2S)
CCM coating (Figure 1A). The phenotype of chondrocytes
seeded in Col2S and antCol2S was assessed. Transcriptome
and proteomic profile analyses were further performed to study
the mechanisms of Col2S for mediating chondrocyte
phenotype. Overall, this study provides a strategy to antagonize
Col2 at nanodimension and insights into the molecular
dialogue in the regulation of hyaline articular cartilage
regeneration.
2. RESULTS AND DISCUSSION
2.1. Fabrication and Characterization of antCol2S. To
obtain sufficient CCM, we isolated chondrocytes from porcine
cartilage according to the experimental procedures shown in
Figure 1A. CCM was derived with the yield of 0.89 mg
membrane protein from 134 million porcine primary
chondrocytes. As shown in the dynamic light scattering
(DLS) result, CCM vesicles were 205.50 ± 2.20 nm, with a
polydispersity index of 0.3859 ± 0.0130 (Figure 1B). Besides,
the zeta potential was measured to be −22.83 ± 0.84 mV.
Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) revealed that CCM
vesicles formed nanovesicles with an average diameter of
approximately 200 nm (Supporting Information (SI) Figure
S1), consistent with the result of DLS. To further verify the
feasibility of membrane coating, the membrane was coated on
a Si surface and coverslip surface. The water contact angle of
the Si surface was decreased from 72.86 ± 2.10° to 23.62 ±
2.45° due to the CCM coating (Figure 1C). The coverslip
represented a hydrophilic surface with a water contact angle
below 2°. When coated with CCM, the coverslip surface
became more hydrophobic with an increased water contact
angle of 19.66 ± 3.37° (Figure 1D). In brief, the water contact
angle was altered after CCM coating, indicating successful
coating formation on these surfaces.
In this study, a scaffold comprising a 3D network of Col2
established by our group was selected as a model scaffold of
Col2S.8 CCM was coated on Col2S to obtain the CCM coated
scaffold antCol2S. The engineered antCol2S was washed at
least three times to remove uncoated vesicles. Protein
bicinchoninic acid (BCA) assay was then conducted for cell
membrane confirmation (Figure 1E). antCol2S showed 1.86 ±
0.31 wt % of protein loading yield (defined as a ratio of protein
content to the weight of scaffold).14 When antCol2S was
stored in 1× PBS at 4 °C for 1 day, the protein loading yield
exhibited no obvious decrease, implying that the cell
membrane coating was stable. SEM observation was carried
out to further ensure the membrane coating. Unlike Col2S,
antCol2S showed a rougher surface and the presence of
numerous nanovesicle-like aggregates. Besides, the fluorescent
microscope image of antCol2S revealed a uniform membrane
coating on Col2S (Figure 1G). Importantly, proteomics
analysis validated the presence and enrichment of key
chondrocyte membrane surface antigens in antCol2S. 403
and 1006 proteins were quantified in Col2S and antCol2S,
respectively (SI Figure S2A). In antCol2S, 611 proteins were
statistically significantly differentially expressed and highly
enriched in cell membrane component and binding molecular
function when compared to Col2S (SI Figure S2B), which is
attributed to the presence of CCM coating on antCol2S.
Proteomics analysis demonstrated significant expression of
chondrocyte-specific markers in antCol2S including integrin
α5, integrin β1, and CD44 (Figure 1F), which is in accordance
with the literature.22 The antCol2S showed the highest integrin
α5 expression, followed by the expression of integrins β1 and
CD44 (SI Table S1). None of them was present on Col2S.
Integrin α5 has been reported to bind with Col2.23 The decline
of integrin α5 has been associated with chondrocyte
dedifferentiation during OA progression.24 A recent phase 1
trial further demonstrated that hyaline articular cartilage
regeneration was partially attributed to the binding with
integrin α5β1.25 In addition, integrin β1 is identified as a
receptor for Col2, and silencing of integrin β1 could promote
dedifferentiation of chondrocytes.26 These membrane-specific
markers further implied the successful translocation of CCM
proteins into antCol2S. Importantly, the enrichment of these
proteins enables the CCM coating to be a potent antagonist
for Col2.

2.2. Col2S-Induced Hyaline Phenotype Rejuvenation
Was Suppressed by antCol2S. To study mechanisms
underlying cartilage regeneration of Col2S, primary porcine
chondrocytes were expanded in monolayer culture from
passage 1 (P1) to passage 5 (P5). We then respectively
transferred the dedifferentiated P5 chondrocytes into Col2S
and antCol2S followed by 6 weeks of culture (Figure 1A).
As chondrocyte morphology was closely lined with
chondrocyte phenotype,27 we have focused on chondrocyte
shapes in P1 chondrocytes (P1) on tissue culture plastic
(TCP), P5 chondrocytes (P5) on TCP, Col2S, and antCol2S
(Figure 2A). To confirm the successful dedifferentiation of
chondrocytes, we compared the morphology of P1 and P5
chondrocytes on the TCP. P1 chondrocytes exhibited round or
polygonal shapes. P5 group, in contrast, displayed flattened or
amoeboid-like shapes, implying the loss of hyaline chondrocyte
phenotype.28 In Col2S, seeded P5 chondrocytes after 6 weeks
of 3D culture showed round morphologies that may have a

SOX9, and COL1A1 via qRT-PCR (n = 3, one-way ANOVA with Tamhane T2 multiple comparison test). (C) Safranin-O and

using Holm-Sidak method). (F) GAG content normalized to DNA demonstrated that cells within the Col2S produced significantly higher

0.05; **p < 0.01; ***p < 0.001; ****p < 0.0001.

https://doi.org/10.1021/acsnano.3c09033

2080
correlation with Col2 synthesis.29 As compared with Col2S,
chondrocytes revealed complex patterns of round morphology
and fibroblastlike shapes in antCol2S, suggesting the partial
failure of redifferentiation. Furthermore, chondrocytes with
bulbous morphologies attached on the scaffold surface and
produced strands of collagen fibers, which demonstrated the
high redifferentiation potential of chondrocytes on the scaffold
(SI Figure S3).
To study the redifferentiation effect of Col2S and antCol2S
on dedifferentiated chondrocytes, quantitative real-time PCR
(qRT-PCR) was performed to evaluate the expression of
cartilage-specific marker genes, such as COL2A1, ACAN,
SOX9, and COL1A1 (Figure 2B). The gene expression value of
P5 was regarded as 1. It is well-accepted that COL2A1 serves as
a typical marker in chondrocyte redifferentiation.30 The level
of expression of COL2A1 was significantly increased in Col2S.
Figure 3. Col2S-induced redifferentiation is suppressed by antCol2S through regulation of the Wnt signaling pathway. (A) Numbers of
DEGs, including upregulated (red) and downregulated (blue) genes in P5/P1, antCol2S/Col2S, and Col2S/P5 comparison. (B) Gene set
enrichment analysis (GSEA) of DEGs in Col2S/P5 (top) and Col2S/antCol2S (bottom) comparison. Enrichment plot: 04310 (Wnt

antCol2S (bottom) comparison. (D) Heatmap of DEGs related to Wnt positive and negative regulators, expressed as log2 (TPM + 1),
transcripts per million, TPM. (E) Heatmap of typical DEGs associated with chondrocyte dedifferentiation expressed as log2 (TPM + 1). (F)

www.acsnano.org
Article
https://doi.org/10.1021/acsnano.3c09033
ACS Nano 2024, 18, 2077−2090
2081
P1 showed the highest expression of COL2A1, followed by
Col2S and antCol2S. Nevertheless, chondrocytes in antCol2S
underwent a downregulation of COL2A1 when compared with
Col2S. ACAN, an important gene encoding aggrecan in
cartilage,31 was significantly upregulated in P1 and Col2S,
whereas antCol2S exhibited almost the same ACAN expression
as P5. SOX9 is a pivotal transcription factor for chondrocyte
lineage during embryonic development.32 Of note, a dramatic
increase in SOX9 expression was observed in Col2S. While the
antCol2S group displayed a slight upregulation of SOX9.
Strikingly, the expression of SOX9 in Col2S was even higher
than that in P1, which is generally regarded as maintaining a
hyaline cartilaginous phenotype. When chondrocytes undergo
dedifferentiation due to monolayer cultures, they synthesize
type I collagen.33 The drastic downregulation of COL1A1 was
observed in Col2S and P1. Instead, antCol2S showed an
upregulation of COL1A1 when compared with the Col2S
group. Above results showed that Col2S could reverse the
phenotype of dedifferentiated chondrocytes, while antCol2S
suppressed this redifferentiation effect.
Apart from evaluating the transcript levels of chondrocytes,
cartilaginous ECM components, GAGs (glycosaminoglycans),
and collagen synthesis were also assessed through histological
and immunohistochemical analysis (Figure 2C). As shown in
Safranin-O staining results, proteoglycan deposition was higher
in Col2S when compared with antCol2S. By immunohisto-
chemistry staining, a similar trend was also observed in Col2
levels of Col2S and antCol2S. It can be seen that Col2 was
greatly expressed in Col2S as compared to the antCol2S and
control group. The presence of type I collagen was insignificant
in all three groups.
To further verify the redifferentiation effect, the actual
amount of GAG in Col2S and antCol2S was assessed by
biochemical assays. After 6 weeks of 3D cell culture, both
groups showed increases in GAG content and cell amounts.
The two groups displayed insignificant differences in DNA and
GAG contents at week 0, indicating the equivalent cell seeding
density before culture. Col2S had relatively higher cell density
than antCol2S at week 6, suggesting good proliferation and cell
viability in antCol2S (Figure 2D). Unlike the antCol2S group,
Col2S was found to have significantly higher amounts of GAG
(Figure 2E). Qualitative analysis was then conducted by
normalizing the GAG to cell amounts (Figure 2F). To confirm
the qRT-PCR and immunohistochemical data, Western blot
was performed to semiquantify the expression of type I
collagen and type II collagen in P1, P5, Col2S, and antCol2S
(SI Figure S4). We found that P5 exhibited the highest
expression of type I collagen, followed by antCol2S. There was
undetectable expression of type I collagen in P1 and Col2S.
Highest level of type II collagen expression was observed in P1
and the Col2S group displayed an increased expression of type
II collagen when compared to antCol2S and P5 group. The
Western blot results were consistent with the aforementioned
data. In summary, Col2S presented a superior potential to
regain hyaline phenotype as compared to antCol2S.
2.3. Transcriptomics Revealed That antCol2S Re-
versed Col2S-Induced Hyaline Phenotype Transcrip-
tional Signature. To investigate the mechanism of hyaline
phenotype rejuvenation, global transcriptomics profiling
analysis of P1, P5, Col2S, and antCol2S was conducted. The
number of differentially expressed genes (DEGs) in each
comparison group (P5/P1, antCol2S/Col2S, and Col2S/P5)
was shown with the amounts of up- and down-regulated genes
(Figure 3A, SI Figure S5C). To verify the consistency among
samples, Pearson correlation analysis was carried out. High
similarity among three replicates of each group was observed
(SI Figure S5A). Gene ontology (GO) analysis was performed
to assess the interactions among DEGs. Among the GO terms
enriched in DEGs between P1 and P5, chondrocyte differ-
entiation and cartilage development were mentioned, which
indicated that phenotype alteration occurred in the process of
subculture (SI Figure S5B). GO terms enriched in Col2S/P5
comparison were significantly correlated with chondrocyte
differentiation, upregulation of collagen biosynthetic process,
upregulation of cell−substrate adhesion, integrin-mediated
signaling pathway, and downregulation of the Wnt signaling
pathway. The GO term of chondrocyte differentiation
suggested the phenotype shift in Col2S. It is important to
note that upregulation of the collagen biosynthetic process in
Col2S suggested higher cartilage ECM protein production,
which was consistent with our previous results. The
upregulation of cell−substrate adhesion and integrin-mediated
signaling pathway indicated the role of integrin involved cell−
substrate adhesion in Col2S. Notably, enriched GO terms in
Col2S/antCol2S comparison included downregulation of the
apoptotic process, downregulation of Wnt signaling pathway,
collagen fibril organization, chondrocyte development, and
chondrocyte differentiation (Figure 3C). The results implied
that the Wnt signaling pathway may be a key regulator in the
redifferentiation of Col2S. Among these DEGs between the
four groups, cartilage-related genes were also detected and
analyzed (Figure 3E). Compared with that in the P5 group, the
upregulation of COL2A1, COL6A3, COL9A1, COL11A2,
HAPLN1, COMP, CRTAC1, UCMA, ACAN, BMP2, GDF5,
and SOX9 in Col2S as well as downregulation in P1 group
indicated that the phenotype of dedifferentiated chondrocytes
was reversed by Col2S. However, these markers were
downregulated in antCol2S, which illustrated that antCol2S
impaired the rejuvenation of the hyaline phenotype induced by
Col2S. Of particular note is the finding that nearly no
COL10A1 expression was detected in Col2S and antCol2S.
The result showed that Col2S induced robust redifferentiation
without chondrocyte hypertrophy. RUNX2, ALPL, and genes
associated with matrix metalloproteinases serve as important
markers in OA cartilage.34 RUNX2 and ALPL were found in
monolayers P1 and P5 but were hardly induced in Col2S and
antCol2S. Lower MMP14 expression and higher TIMP1
induction were also observed in Col2S. Taken together,
transcriptomics profiling data demonstrated that Col2S
rejuvenated dedifferentiated chondrocytes to a hyaline
phenotype, while antCol2S attenuated this redifferentiation
effect.
2.4. Chondrocytes Exhibited Differential Wnt Signal-
ing Pathway Activity in Col2S and antCol2S. To unravel
the mechanism underlying the redifferentiation, signaling
pathways were investigated by transcriptomics. Notably, P5
revealed obvious positive regulation of Wnt signaling as
compared to P1, indicating the role of Wnt signaling in
chondrocyte dedifferentiation (SI Figure S5B). Negative
regulation of the Wnt signaling pathway was observed in
Col2S, while P5 and antCol2S showed positive regulation of
the Wnt signaling pathway (Figure 3C). A similar trend was
also observed in the Kyoto Encyclopedia of Genes and
Genomes (KEGG) signaling pathways analysis. Downregu-
lated DEGs in Col2S/P5 comparison were enriched in the Wnt
signaling pathway. Meanwhile, upregulated DEGs in ant-

Col2S/Col2S comparison were found to be associated with the
Wnt signaling pathway (SI Figure S5D). Gene set enrichment
analysis (GSEA) was further performed to investigate the
involved signaling pathways (Figure 3B). We found that the
downregulation of the Wnt signaling pathway (enrichment
plot: 04310) was significantly enriched in the Col2S tran-
scriptome in both Col2S/P5 and antCol2S/Col2S comparison.
DEGs associated with the Wnt signaling pathway are further
displayed in Figure 3D. As compared to P5 and antCol2S,
Col2S elicited downregulation of Wnt/β-catenin signaling,
identified by higher expression of Wnt negative regulators
(TLE2, APOE, WIF1, and DKKL1) and lower levels of Wnt
positive regulatory genes (DVL1, TBL1XR1, DVL2, LRP6,
FZD1, WNT2B, and FZD6). The above results were consistent

with our aforementioned data. Wnt cascade is involved in cell
proliferation, migration, and differentiation.35 β-catenin
functions as an effector of the Wnt signaling cascade. If there
are no Wnt ligands, then β-catenin is phosphorylated and
degraded by a destruction complex. In contrast, the engage-
ment of Wnt ligands to its frizzled (FZD) receptors will
activate Wnt signaling pathway and initiate the phosphor-
ylation of LRP5/LRP6. Disheveled (Dvl) protein transfers Wnt
signals from receptors to downstream effectors and prevents
the destruction of cytoplasmic β-catenin. Consequently, β-
catenin is stabilized and translocated into nuclear.36
Furthermore, Wnt signaling can be antagonized by WIF137
and DKKL1.38 TLE families are also known for the repression
of Wnt targets.39 APOE acts as an inhibitor of the canonical

2084
Wnt pathway.40 Importantly, it is increasingly believed that
positive regulation of the Wnt signaling pathway will occur in
the process of chondrocyte dedifferentiation.11 Gene ex-
pression of the marker gene for the Wnt signaling pathway
and chondrocyte dedifferentiation was simultaneously pre-
sented in Figure 3F. Col2S showed lower levels of CTNNB1
(the gene encoding β-catenin) as compared with antCol2S and
P5, indicating decreased activity of Wnt/β-catenin signaling. A
converse trend was found in the expression of COL2A1. The
alteration of Wnt signaling in these comparisons unveiled that
the Wnt signaling pathway is the key regulator in the
phenotype shift of chondrocyte seeded in Col2S and antCol2S.
2.5. Col2S Repressed Wnt/β-Catenin Signaling Acti-
vation by Facilitating Secreted Frizzled-Related Protein
3 (sFRP3) Expression. To further validate the findings in
gene expression profiles, proteomics was conducted to
investigate differentially expressed proteins (DEPs) of P1,
P5, Col2S, and antCol2S. High Col2 protein content of the
scaffold affected proteomics analysis. Therefore, chondrocytes
were extracted by enzymic method for proteomics (Figure
4A). The overlap of DEPs in the four groups is shown in SI
Figure S6A. Totally, the results displayed a total of 809 DEPs
between Col2S and P5 where 410 proteins were upregulated
and 399 proteins were downregulated (SI Figure S6B).
Furthermore, 334 DEPs were observed between Col2S and
antCol2S, with 134 and 200 proteins upregulated and
downregulated. GO analyses of DEPs in Col2S and P5
identified variations in molecular function including cartilage
development, chondrocyte development, as well as chondro-
cyte differentiation (Figure 4B), which further implied that
dedifferentiated chondrocytes underwent phenotype shift in
Col2S.
The DEPs of cartilage-related proteins were identified in P1/
P5 and Col2S/antCol2S comparisons (Figure 4C). The
different expressions of P1 and P5 elucidated that dediffer-
entiation occurred after serial cell passage. Alterations of Col2
and collagen type I are typical hallmarks of dedifferentiation.
The level of collagen type II alpha 1 chain (COL2A1) showed
significant increases in Col2S when compared to antCol2S,
while the expression of collagen type I alpha 2 chain
(COL1A2) revealed a relatively lower level in the Col2S
group. Upregulation of collagen type IX alpha 1 chain
(COL9A1), hyaluronan and proteoglycan link protein 1
(HAPLN1), cartilage intermediate layer protein 2 (CILP2),
aggrecan, cartilage oligomeric matrix protein, collagen type XI
alpha 2 chain (COL11A2), collagen alpha-1(VI) chain
(COL6A1), metalloproteinase inhibitor 1, as well as
COL2A1 and transcription factor SOX-9 (SOX9) in the
Col2S group showed the robustly potent redifferentiation
effect on chondrocytes. In agreement with this, the
upregulation of COL1A2, matrix metalloproteinase-14, and
RUNX family transcription factor 2 in the antCol2S group
demonstrated the attenuation of redifferentiation. Proteomics
data were also consistent with the results of RNA sequence.
The volcano plot was employed to illustrate DEGs between
Col2S and P5 (Figure 4D). Among these DEGs, cartilage-
related markers were detected to be significantly upregulated in
the Col2S group, including COL2A1, COL11A2, CILP2,
HAPLN1, COL9A1, and SOX9. Moreover, collagen type I
alpha 1 chain (COL1A1), catenin β1 (CTNNB1, β-catenin),
and COL1A2 were downregulated in the Col2S group.
Strikingly, the Wnt antagonist, secreted frizzled-related protein
3 (termed FRZB or sFRP3) was found to be dramatically
increased in the Col2S group, which was downregulated in
antCol2S (Figure 4F).
To further identify the Wnt signaling pathway, DEPs in P1/
P5 and Col2S/antCol2S involved in Wnt signaling are shown
in Figure 4F. β-catenin, the key driver in the Wnt signaling
pathway, was upregulated in the P5 group, which is associated
with the activation of the Wnt/β-catenin signaling pathway.
We further confirmed that there was accumulation of β-catenin
in P5 and antCol2S as well as enhanced degradation of β-
catenin in the P1 and Col2S groups, which is illustrated by
proteomics and Western blot (Figure 4E,G,H). Glycogen
synthase kinase-3 beta (GSK3β), is a protein kinase and
phosphorylates β-catenin to trigger its destabilization and
degradation.36 Adenomatous polyposis coli (APC) is a
component of the cytoplasmic β-catenin destruction com-
plex.41 The higher levels of GSK3β and APC showed the
severe degradation of β-catenin in the Col2S group. Wnt
secretion mediator Wntless (WLS) can provide binding sites
for WNT proteins.42 Owing to the occupation of sFRP-3 and
massive WNT protein remaining, Col2S displayed enhanced
expression of WLS in response to the higher requirement for
Wnt secretion. Together, we can conclude that Col2S
facilitates the redifferentiation effect through downregulation
of the Wnt/β-catenin signaling pathway. Furthermore, Col2S
exhibited markedly increased expression of sFRP3 which was
downregulated by P5 and antCol2S, suggesting the vital role of
sFRP3 in chondrocyte redifferentiation.
2.6. Col2S-Induced Integrin α5β1 Activation and
sFRP3 Secretion Were Blocked by antCol2S. Integrin
α5 activation was reported to be vital for DKK1 and sFRP3
secretion.25 Given the enhanced secretion of sFRP3 in Col2S
and reduced production in antCol2S by proteomics analysis,
we focused on the expression of integrin α5 in seeded
chondrocytes. Furthermore, it is important to note that
antCol2S may seriously hinder the activation of integrin α5
on seeded chondrocytes due to the antagonism and shielding
against Col2 ligands. Specifically, in antCol2S, the ligand sites
on Col2 had been preoccupied and saturated by CCM coating
before exposure to the seeded cells. Therefore, the attenuation
of redifferentiation may be partly attributed to the decreased
activation of integrin α5, which means that integrin α5 is of
particular importance in the mechanism underlying the action
of Col2S.
To verify the results in proteomics, we further examined the
secretion of the Wnt inhibitor protein sFRP3. The decline of
FRZB (gene encoding sFRP3) expression was observed in
antCol2S through RNA sequencing (Figure 5A). Proteomics
analysis demonstrated that the label-free quantification (LFQ)
intensity of sFRP3 in Col2S was higher than that of antCol2S
(Figure 5C). Additionally, enzyme-linked immunosorbent
assay (ELISA) was performed to determine the level of
secreted sFRP3. The data was normalized to the total cell
number in each group. The secretion of sFRP3 was
significantly increased in Col2S as compared to antCol2S
following a 3D cell culture for both 1 and 6 weeks (Figure 5G).
sFRP3 secretion declined with the dedifferentiation of
chondrocytes (Figure 5F), which is also in line with the
relationship between Wnt signaling and chondrocyte dediffer-
entiation. In a word, Col2S promotes redifferentiation through
sFRP3 secretion, while antCol2S acts as an inhibitor of sFRP3
expression.
To test the effect of integrin α5, we examined the expression
of integrin α5 on P1, P5, Col2S, and antCol2S. ITGA5 (gene
ACS Nano
www.acsnano.org
Article
https://doi.org/10.1021/acsnano.3c09033
ACS Nano 2024, 18, 2077−2090
2085
encoding integrin α5) was markedly increased in Col2S
(Figure 5B), which implied its essential role in Col2S-induced
redifferentiation. Determined by Western blot analysis, integrin
α5 was increased in the Col2S group but underwent a decline
in antCol2S (Figure 5D,E). Integrin α5 expression was
reported to transiently increase during dedifferentiation on
day 6 but then decreased on day 21.43 In agreement with this, a
comparable level of integrin α5 in P1 on day 4 and P5 on day
14 was observed. Integrin α5 has previously been reported to
be required for DKK1 and sFRP3 secretion.25 Activation of
integrin α5 and secretion of sFRP3 colocalized in Col2S group.
The results indicated that integrin α5 binding plays a key role
in the Col2S induced redifferentiation effect, while antCol2S
repressed the binding of integrin α5 on seeded chondrocytes.
To elucidate the detailed mechanism underlying the action
of Col2S, the schematic illustration is shown in Figure 5H.
Integrin α5 on chondrocytes is activated in Col2S, and thereby
upregulates the secretion of sFRP3. Enhanced expression of
sFRP3 in Col2S leads to the degradation of β-catenin that was
found to initiate inhibition of SOX9 transcriptional activity.44
The upregulation of SOX9 was well accepted as a cause for the
upregulation of Col2.45 However, integrin α5 on chondrocytes
remains inactive in antCol2S. sFRP3 secretion declines with
inhibited integrin α5 activation. Wnt ligands subsequently bind
to FZD (Frizzled) and thereby initiate translocation of β-
catenin into nuclear. As a result, SOX9 transcriptional activity
is inhibited, followed by the downregulation of Col2.
On the basis of the aforementioned findings, we proposed a
schematic illustration to describe the mechanism of modulat-
ing hyaline cartilaginous phenotype in chondrocytes in Col2
based scaffold with or without CCM nanoaggregates as the
antagonist of Col2 (Figure 6). In Col2S, integrin α5 on
chondrocytes binds with the ligands of Col2S and thus is
activated. Enhanced expression of sFRP3 in Col2S results in
downregulation of the Wnt signaling pathway, followed by
degradation of β-catenin, activation of SOX9, and upregulation
of Col2. Contradictorily, in antCol2S, there are reduced
accessible ligands for integrin α5 on chondrocytes as the
ligands are preoccupied and saturated by CCM coating. As a
result, integrin α5 on chondrocytes remains inactive in
antCol2S. sFRP3 secretion is attenuated and hence leads to
the upregulation of the Wnt signaling pathway followed by
translocation of β-catenin into the nuclear, inhibition of SOX9,
and downregulation of Col2.
Figure 6. Diagram depicting the mechanism underlying hyaline cartilaginous phenotype rejuvenation of Col2S and antCol2S.
ACS Nano
www.acsnano.org
Article
https://doi.org/10.1021/acsnano.3c09033
ACS Nano 2024, 18, 2077−2090
2086
3. STUDY LIMITATIONS
The findings from this study should be interpreted in the
context of the following limitations. Due to the inaccessibility
of sufficient human articular chondrocytes, this study only used
primary chondrocytes isolated from porcine to establish an in
vitro model to evaluate the redifferentiation effect of Col2S
and antCol2S with limited transferability to human cartilage
studies. Although there are anatomical similarities between
large animals and humans, the interspecies differences in
chondrocytes should be considered.46 To get data closer to
human physiology and pathology, the redifferentiation effect of
these scaffolds and the mechanism proposed in this study need
to be confirmed on human articular chondrocytes of the knee
joint.
Another point is that chondrocyte derived membrane
nanovesicles in this study have achieved a low production
yield because of the complicated and time-consuming
preparation procedures. Through freeze−thawing, shear, or
frictional forces, ultrasonication, and other approaches, cell
membrane nanovesicles can be obtained from parent cells. To
achieve a higher yield, it is better to develop improved
production methods or harvest sufficient amounts of cells.
4. CONCLUSION
Despite potent cartilage restoration of Col2-based scaffolds,
the underlying mechanism is unclear. Considering the
antagonism and shielding of cell membrane coating nano-
technology, in this work, CCM nanoaggregates derived from
chondrocytes acted as antagonists of Col2 for the scaffold in
this mechanism study. Col2S was found to rejuvenate
dedifferentiated chondrocytes to regain a pure hyaline-like
cartilaginous phenotype, whereas antCol2S repressed this
redifferentiation effect. Using transcriptome and proteome
techniques, we discovered that Col2S facilitated chondrocyte
redifferentiation via the Wnt signaling pathway, in which
enhanced expression of sFRP3 and binding to integrin α5 play
a crucial role. However, the ligation between Col2 ligands and
integrin α5 receptors on seeded chondrocytes in antCol2S was
inhibited by CCM coating and thereby dictated the Wnt/β-
catenin signaling toward the loss of the hyaline cartilaginous
phenotype. This study develops an anti-Col2 strategy to
explain how Col2 based scaffolds modulate chondrocyte
phenotype and highlights the contribution of Col2 in
rejuvenation and maintenance of the hyaline cartilaginous
phenotype.
5. EXPERIMENTAL SECTION
5.1. Primary Chondrocytes Harvest and Subculture. Primary
chondrocytes were isolated from porcine cartilage slices according to
the established protocol.8 The collected primary chondrocytes
(passage 0, P0) were cultured in chondrocyte culture primary
(CCP) medium containing Dulbecco’s modified Eagle’s medium
(DMEM), 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS), 1% penicillin−streptomy-
cin, 0.2% nonessential amino acids (NEAA), 0.2% 4-(2-hydrox-
yethyl)-piperazine-1-ethanesulfonic acid, 0.1% vitamin C, and 0.1% L-
proline. P0 chondrocytes were passaged for in vitro expansion to
harvest P1 chondrocytes (P1). Thereafter, P5 chondrocytes (P5)
were collected by subculture from P1 chondrocytes in 2 weeks
(Figure 1A).
5.2. Derivation and Characterization of antCol2S. After the
digestion of porcine cartilage slices, a large number of chondrocytes
were harvested. The chondrocytes were then centrifuged and washed
with PBS at least three times. Collected P0 chondrocytes were
resuspended with hypotonic lysing buffer, frozen, and thawed three
times. Cells were observed under a microscope to confirm the
complete lysis. Then the mixture solution was transferred to a Dounce
homogenizer and passed at least 30 times. Followed by hypotonic
lysing buffer solution, differential centrifugation, and PBS wash, we
ultimately obtained the chondrocyte membrane (CCM) vesicles. The
membrane content was determined by using a BCA kit (Beyotime)
with BSA (bovine serum albumin) as a reference. CCM was uniformly
dispersed in deionized (DI) water. Dynamic light scattering (DLS)
was conducted on a Malvern Panalytical instrument for testing the
size and zeta potential of membrane vesicles. Given the high content
of Col2, we selected the decellularized hyaline cartilage graft
developed in our laboratory8 as the representative of Col2S. To
visualize the effective coating on Col2S, the membrane was stained by
DiI (a lipophilic tracer for cell membrane labeling). Subsequently,
Col2S was immersed in the chondrocyte membrane suspension and
sustained for one night to obtain antCol2S. Then antCol2S was rinsed
by PBS for 2h to wash the uncoated membrane. The protein loading
yield of antCol2S was determined with a BCA kit (Beyotime).
AntCol2S was observed under a fluorescent microscope. After
lyophilization, the dry Col2S and antCol2S were sprayed with gold
and observed under scanning electron microscope (SEM). To identify
the protein types and contents at the surface of antCol2S, the
remaining dry Col2S and antCol2S were sent to APT-bio Technology
CO., Ltd. (Shanghai, China) for proteomics analysis.
5.3. Fabrication of Chondrocytes-Scaffold Constructs. P5
chondrocytes were seeded in Col2S and antCol2S at a density of 8 ×
105 cells/scaffold. The cell-laden constructs were cultured in a
chondrocyte culture (CC) medium comprising 20% FBS, 1%
penicillin−streptomycin, 0.01 M NEAA, 0.01 M 4-(2-hydroxyethyl)-
piperazine-1-ethanesulfonic acid, 0.05 mg/mL vitamin C, and 0.4 mM
L-proline in DMEM for 6 weeks. The culture medium was changed
every 3 days.
5.4. Cytoskeleton and Nuclei Visualization. After 6 weeks,
Col2S and antCol2S were fixed in 4% paraformaldehyde along with
collected P1 and P5. The fixed four groups of samples were incubated
with FITC-conjugated phalloidin (Beyotime) for 1 h in the dark.
After being treated with 4′,6-diamidino-2-phenylindole (DAPI)
(Beyotime), the samples were observed by confocal microscopy
(Leica).
5.5. Quantitative Real-Time Polymerase Chain Reaction
(qRT-PCR). qRT-PCR experiment was carried out to investigate the
gene expression level of cartilage-related markers including COL2A1,
ACAN, SOX9, and COL1A1. Briefly, P1, P5, Col2S, and antCol2S
were washed and treated with Trizol to extract RNA. The RNA
concentration was determined by a NanoDrop (Thermo Fisher
Scientific). 0.5 μg of total RNA was reversed by the cDNA Synthesis
Kit (Invitrogen). SYBR Green-based real-time quantitative PCR
(qPCR) was conducted with a CFX Connect Real-Time PCR System
(BIO-RAD) with an All-in-One qPCR Mix (Gene Copoeia). GAPDH
was selected as the reference gene. 2(−ΔΔCt) was used for the
calculation of gene expression levels. The forward and reverse primers
of target genes are listed in Table S2.
5.6. Histological and Immunohistochemical Analysis. Col2S
and antCol2S were embedded in paraffin and sectioned into slices
with a thickness of 5 μm by using a microtome (Leica). H&E and
Safranin O staining were conducted according to a standard protocol
of the manufacturer (Solarbio).
For Col2 and type I collagen staining, the deparaffined and
rehydrated sections were treated with anticollagen II antibody
(abcam, ab34712) and collagen type I polyclonal antibody
(Proteintech, 14695-1-AP) overnight at 4 °C. Then the samples
were incubated with HRP-Goat anti-rabbit/mouse secondary anti-
body (Proteintech, PK10006). Images were taken by bright-field
microscopy.
5.7. Biochemical Assays. Col2S and antCol2S constructs after 0
and 6 weeks were frozen at −20 °C and subsequently lyophilized for
24 h. Every construct was immersed in 1 mL of digestion solution
consisting of 0.1 mM disodium ethylene diamine tetraacetic acid and
0.2 mM dithiothreitol containing 0.3 mg/mL papain. Cell amount was
tested by DNA content which was determined by Hoechst 33258
ACS Nano
www.acsnano.org
Article
https://doi.org/10.1021/acsnano.3c09033
ACS Nano 2024, 18, 2077−2090
2087
assay (7.7 pg of DNA per cell). GAG content was determined by a
dimethylmethylene blue (DMB) assay.
5.8. RNA Sequencing and Data Analysis. The collected P1, P5,
Col2S, and antCol2S constructs after 6 weeks were sent to BGI
Health (HK) Company Limited for RNA extraction, library
preparation, RNA sequencing, and data analysis. DEGs defined as
differentially expressed genes were determined by log2(fold-change) >
1 and p-value < 0.05. The heatmap, GO analysis, KEGG pathway
analysis, and GSEA were performed at Dr. Tom system of BGI
company https://biosys.bgi.com/.
5.9. Proteomics. After culture for 6 weeks, Col2S and antCol2S
constructs were collected and immersed in a digestion solution (1
mg/mL Collagenase II solution, Thermo Fisher Scientific) to
moderately extract chondrocytes. The collected P1, P5, chondrocytes
in Col2S, and chondrocytes in antCol2S were sent to APT-bio
Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China) for 4D-Label-free
proteomics analysis. DEPs defined as differentially expressed proteins
were determined by log2(fold-change) > 1 and p-value < 0.05. The
heatmap and GO analysis were carried out at https://bio-cloud.
aptbiotech.com/.
5.10. Western Blot. After 6 weeks of culture, P1, P5, Col2S, and
antCol2S constructs were collected for protein extraction by RIPA
buffer, proteinase, and phosphatase inhibitor cocktails (Thermo
Fisher Scientific). Prestained Protein Ladder was employed to
visualize the protein bands (Thermo Fisher Scientific). The used
antibodies were listed as follows: anti-beta catenin antibody (abcam,
ab16051), anti-integrin alpha 5 antibody (abcam, ab112183), goat
anti-rabbit IgG H&L (HRP) (abcam, ab205718), goat anti-mouse
IgG H&L (HRP) (abcam, ab205719). Densitometry analysis was
performed by ImageJ.
5.11. Enzyme-Linked Immunosorbent Assay (ELISA). The
cell supernatant of P1 and P5, as well as Col2S and antCol2S
constructs after culture for 1 and 6 weeks, were aspirated and then
measured by human sFRP3 ELISA kit (Thermo Fisher Scientific).
Standard and samples were added to the plate, and then biotin
conjugate and Streptavindin-HRP solution were added sequentially to
the wells. Further, the TMB substrate and stop solution were added to
stop the colorimetric reaction. The absorbance at 450 nm was read by
using a microplate reader (Spectra Max 190). Cell amount was tested
by DNA content which was determined by Hoechst 33258 assay (7.7
pg of DNA per cell). The data was represented as sFRP3 secretion
normalized to the cell amount.
5.12. Statistical Analysis. Statistics were analyzed by using
Student’s t test or one-way ANOVA in GraphPad Prism 8. Data are
represented as means ± SEM. Statistical significance was taken as p <
0.05 (n.s., p ≥0.05; *p < 0.05; **p < 0.01; ***p < 0.001; ****p <
0.0001). All results were confirmed in at least three independent
experiments.
